Title: Social listening or social intelligence?
Body: How do we progress from using social tools to listen better to our customers to creating organizations that consistently respond and adjust to what they are hearing?

Title: Jam is the answer
Body: Not sure I get your question ? But if you are asking how we could listen better to our customers I beleive that Jamming is one answer to this.

Title: Can we do a permanent Jam for the world?
Body: Now that would be value add!

Title: Continuous Jamming
Body: I agree, this would be a great way for organizations, including government https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=75424&c=45061, to filter and capture improvements. It would need automated summary/deletion/archive but the basics are here.

Title: More details?
Body: Ben, this is a rich question. Can you provide more details as others may want to provide comments.

Title: refer to post on Product Reviews
Body: I think we are missing opportunities to turn product reviews into social intelligence about our products and programs....We can get much of our intelligence right on our site...using product reviews as a key method...not the only method...but a key method...:-)

Title: A bit of elaboration
Body: To elaborate a little, we might define "social listening" as the act of using online tools to tune ourselves to conversations about our companies, products, services, solutions, etc. We might define "social intelligence" as more of a closed-loop state in which we are not only listening to what our customers/partners/employee are saying about us and our products, but responding to that information by adjusting how we make, distribute, market and support our products - and understand that we are doing this consistently across the enterprise by measuring and monitoring this closed-loop process.

Title: Listen first to filter the noise
Body: Social intelligence is a way companies will get value from Social Business: Listen to what your customers are saying, filter out what is important for your business, and quickly take action as an integral part of your business process.  You ask a good question ... how to get started? There are so many aspects that need to align to make this work. After giving it some thought, I'd suggest that the most critical element is to create a 'pull' from business owners with a critical need for social listening input ... rather than trying to 'push' this to them. One way to start to convince them is to provide them with filtered and prioritized conversations that are relevant to them. Once they see the impact and potential value to their process, it can drive a sense of urgency for the need to include the social voice of the customer.

Title: Continuous, Actionable Insight
Body: This is a critically important topic for businesses that want to realize the full value of monitoring millions of conversations. Let's assume for a moment that already you're using some type of social media analytics tool to mine and filter all this data, perhaps also getting trend and even sentiment data. Then what?  Ideally, that data need to find other relevant data, which then puts it in context and can be routed to those who need it. (And you may not know you need it until it's there.)  So  ...   businesses need some type of feedback framework or exchange that can continually ingest all this real-time insight and subsequently route relevant information to those that need to improve products and services, craft more targeted campaigns, produce better market research, or enhance customer experiences.  If it's not actionable, it's just noise.

Title: The power of one among the many
Body: I must challenge the notion that large amounts of data need to be analyzed and millions of conversations shifted through before value can be realized from social or action taken. Social analytics tools can be extremely powerful. But organizations and individuals should not discount the power of social business if they don't have the tools and resources to mine millions of conversations or connections. Many times it is the single idea among the many that sparks the next big innovation. It's the individual connection among the masses that forms a partnership, combines resources or starts a new market.  The collective wisdom of a group can be a powerful force in business, but the power of the ONE (the one connection, the one good idea, the one relationship) among the many can sometimes be just as powerful. That's the beauty of social business - - it doesn't have to be one or the other. It can and  ...   is many different things to many different organizations and individuals.

Title: finding those valuable nuggests
Body: Exactly right. The challenge, of course, is recognizing those valuable ideas, and making sure that they don't get lost amidst all the noise. I believe that there can be social software solutions to help identify and surface the good ideas.

Title: Context, Aggregation and Sentiment Intelligence
Body: Sentiment Intelligence Systems, (not recommendation engines or the not valuable Like/Dislike function)is something I've been working on for a while, that may be the core for the framework you are talking about. In its nascent state my thought is this: situational analysis and the specific context within which the user find himself or herself at any given moment is really unaddressed in current social networks. My situation is different than yours or any other jammer at any given time. Location changes, interests, change, but capturing my situation when I search for a product or service, is likely the best information a company can gain because it addresses not only why I want the product or service, but why today which is also an important questions. Gen X is fickle, frugal, but situations and tastes change based on circumstance (situation). Text analytics and heuristic engines can provide the technical means to create that analysis. From that point, garnering additional relevant information as you describe has a unique value to the enterprise, and its ecosystem of partners. Applying a rules engine would then trigger dispatching that information to relevant individuals in not only the enterprise but across its value network of supply chain partners all the way back to perhaps engineering. In this way the information can then be  ...   used for not only better products and services but for tremendous cost savings. None of this by the way is inventing new technology, it is simply putting the right pieces together in the right way.  From a technology point of view, text analytics together with a heuristic query engine would be able to deal with the constantly changing circumstances of the user and the need to pare down the conversations about a given topic, product or service.  Then applying a business rule (trigger) the feedback loop would send relevant data to any entity in the relevant ecosystem, including the individual.

Title: Product & Service Actionable vs.Human Insight
Body: I think the value of the social dialogs extends beyond the actionable aspects of creating or improving a product or services.  1. The ability to openly express an opinion or thought to a broad audience has potential morale-improving impact to the participants... beyond their ideas.  2. The culture of an open organization can have long-term positive systemic impact beyond any specific topics.  3. SMEs are identified by the content of their postings.

Title: Nuisance of "custom ads" when I visit web pages
Body: I highly agree!!  Just a few days ago I had the experience: I registered on a forum and the ad area (or sponsor area, if you will) is sprawling with advertisement from web shops I had visited (not buying, just "window shopping"). It flashes and icons bounce up and down ... I hate it.  It does not focus my attention on the advertising web shop, but it also distracts my concentration on the forum entries. So I scroll down to get it out of my vision, and now the same stupid stuff appears on the very right or left side of the screen. You guessed it, flashing, and bouncing.  I'm  ...   aware that retailers are interested in the stuff I might like.  Although, I want a control that allows me to turn off annoying advertisement, without hampering the functionality of the web page. I can block cookies, yes, I can block pop-ups. Will the page still work?  If advertising cannot be turned off, why in the world must it "scream" at me?  So it becomes even more important to disclose as little information about yourself as possible.

Title: right thread?
Body: Sorry Isabella...while I agree this is a frustrating experience, and it may - at a very high level - tie to relevancy of content, I'm referring to a completely different filtering strategy...one that impacts the business (or lines of business) rather than individual users and their web experience.  Apologies if I misunderstood your comments.

Title: context-sensitive aggregation
Body: Bob's "if it's not actionable, its just noise" and Debbie's concept of "filtered and prioritized conversations that are relevant" struck a chord with me, and continue to have me think about the possible future steps of context relevant aggregation.  In another thread, https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=84516&c=45319#45319 I posted some thoughts on a more ideal aggregation function, one that's not only centered around the user, but what the user is doing at the time. if the user is preparing to give a speech on business analytics to a specific audience, then the activity stream in all its myriad forms, could be pared down to items that might help the user with that speech. that could be images or charts to reuse, updates on people likely to be in the audience, insight on the technology used to conduct business analytics and more. the tools available even now could do the necessary automatic filtering and paring down of the Mississippi-sized  ...   torrent of data flowing on all topics to a targeted stream of those that are relevant to the focus task at hand for the individual.

Title: Dynamic Dashboards
Body: We park our cars in the same garage, Michael...  Love the dashboard idea, and I tied your other post to one by Steve Gray recommending a 'social business dashboard' (my response to Steve's post https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=75439&c=45299 ).  There are similarities in your ideas but I believe some notable differences in usage scenarios, so we should pursue both in parallel.  ...   Your contextual dashboard (Social BizDeck ??) idea sounds closely related to an idea I brought up to the IBM Mashup Center team in relation to the Mashable War Room solution they've been working on. At the Industry Solutions Lab (ISL), we have C-level executives of customers and partners coming in every day. It would be ideal to have a dashboard of all relevant internal and external information about those visiting companies (even individuals, if possible) in preparation for those events...recent IBM meetings, their install base, aggregated social media info, etc. Gathering all that information today is time-consuming and will almost certainly overlook some meaningful content.  So IMHO this is a great use case for, as I think you put it, the efficient 'social aggregation and dissemination' of information that would enhance overall client value and specifically the ISL client experience.

Title: Turn listening into action
Body: To truly become a listening organization would mean that we turn the intelligence gathered from social listening into action. To do so requires an infrastructure that allows IBMers to take appropriate action on this intelligence.  With such a infrastruture in place, IBMers could harness the intelligence from the ŌĆ£web miningŌĆØ research and use it to build informed social business strategy and outreach/engagement plans.  More specifically, having this infrastructure will allow IBMers to:  ...   ŌĆóStay abreast of top posts, developing stories in their area of interest/expertise & connect with top on-line influencers  ŌĆóIdentify and assign social conversations to program champions to engage in an ongoing dialogue with subject matter experts and/or influencers - a coverage model for social business  ŌĆóKeep pace with the market trends/conversations in real time user generated content (UGC) with the ability react immediately  ŌĆóKeep up ŌĆō as needed - with social network ŌĆświldfiresŌĆÖ as they occur  ŌĆóPass posts to other IBM SMEs as appropriate i.e. legal, Communications, etc.  ŌĆóAllow for administrative controls & assignments i.e. SMEs can label engagements ŌĆśdo not engage,ŌĆÖ ŌĆśengage now!ŌĆÖ etc.  ŌĆóImprove productivity with workflow and engagement effectiveness reporting capabilities (metrics/analytics capability) which currently takes place manually  ŌĆóOpen new channels to uncover sales leads, which in turn increases validated lead revenue  ŌĆóImprove employee productivity while reducing costs  ŌĆóHarness intelligence to predict future intention i.e. desired state, desired products, planned activities. A valuable predictor to help us inform messaging, go-to-market plans, investments ŌĆ” the list goes on.

Title: Get users & customers to ACT
Body: The most successful marketers manage to get their customers engaged in "doing" something instead of passively listening to what they say in social networks. They may be unhappy about something and not say anything at all.  A good example would be to get people to suggest product features and vote for the best idea. Potentially having an incentive/prize attached to the best contributors.

Title: Consumer Insight for sentiment & action
Body: IBM Cognos« Consumer Insight was recently announced to help marketing professionals transform customer relationships by analyzing sentiment, affinity and evolving topics in social media sites. Perhaps, applying this as part of the marketing and development processes could help turn listening into action. Christoper Christopher Dziekan and his BA team might help this discussion.

Title: IBM Cognos Consumer Insight - Social Analytics
Body: As Don says ... IBM Cognos Consumer Insight has been released into the market as a great solution for performing deep analysis and correlations for clients around "what are your customers saying about you?" to drive actionable insights to make smarter decisions and ultimately driving better business outcomes through deeper and more targeted customer interactions. The information can also be distributed to a larger audience with Cognos 10 allowing for collaboration inside our clients - helping them to determine how to best treat and interact with their customers.  Find out more here ... http://www-01.ibm.com/software/analytics/cognos/analytic-applications/consumer-insight/

Title: Bridges between customer & org + internal agility
Body: In the context of services and solutions organisations, I think two things are necessary in order to consistently respond and adjust to customer feedback:  - plenty of bridges between customers and the organisation  - organizational agility, so the org can respond to customers  I see two important structural components being important to this:  ...   1. Marketing experts (experts who market)  At least one expert for each specific topic area that is relevant to the organization. The expert(s) must be willing to get out there and collaborate with customers (incl. manage communities), provide valuable thought leadership that represents the service/solution/brand and build a bridge between external customer needs and development of the internal organisation's services/solutions & operations.  2. A matrixed marketing and communications team  A cross functional team, including market analysts, product development, sales, customer support and all other customer-facing functions FOR EACH specific target market/topic area. These people would work together on behalf of the company to provide a consistent, quality customer experience and ongoing customer relationship management to fulfill on the Marketing Concept, i.e. determine and then satisfy the desires and needs of the target market in order to achieve the needs of the organization.  In order to achieve this, I think the organisation would need to be very agile.  What would make an org more agile?  It would need to be made up of intelligent, self-reliant people who are passionate about delivering consistent quality and evolving the services / solutions in their topic / business area over time.  These people would need to be empowered to network/coordinate with colleagues internally to find solutions to problems / answers to questions and respond externally. That same specialised, matrixed team would need to have the power to adjust the value / offerings within their specialist area too. I think they'd need to be led by a 'Marketing Expert' (an expert who would lead both the internal and external 'tribes' interested in that specific topic area to arrive at the best possible win/win outcomes for both the customer and the company).  That's my vision of the future! I'm interested to hear other's thoughts.

Title: Structure and encouragement
Body: Rowan, I think your approach is very logical. It is also natural for us to think about management of this phenomenon in a structured way as you describe. The concern I have about appointing people to be champions or managers of the social business is that choosing the right people for this is not easy. For example, marketing people are not necessarily the right people to manage this, anymore than the accounting team. Good social networkers and community managers come from the least expected part of the company. What is essential is that the social network can be established without a manager, as it is then truly democratic, and allows the natural social leaders to emerge.

Title: company- or customer-centric
Body: Rowan, I mostly agree with you, but isn't your vision still very much of a company-centric view?  What if, instead of acting "on behalf of the company", business was driven "on behalf of customers"?  The main problem I see is that most of our thinking still revolves around the company and its structure, not around business itself. To become more agile, companies must adapt, well, to the markets, which are weighted through relationships, not through structured transactions.

Title: Where will the leaders come from?
Body: Simon, I completely agree with your excellent point that people can't be 'appointed' as social leaders - they must emerge. So will these people come from the top, the middle or the bottom?! (And if/when people emerge from the bottom, will that be confronting to Snr Management who struggle to understand the new power currencies?) This ties into a separate post on balancing bottom up and top down leadership here: https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=84531&c=44917&n=15#44917 Wherever they come from, I strongly believe the 'Marketing Experts' (experts who market) would need to organically emerge. The skill sets needed for that role are, as you pointed out, not easy to define or find and they would need to be intrinsically motivated to perform the associated tasks.  ...   You're also right that open, social leaders will need to emerge from all parts of the business, as all parts of organisations will need to consistently respond and adjust to what they are hearing (based on feedback from internal and external).  Do you think these emergent social leaders could also be described as emergent change agents?

Title: Social Change Agents
Body: Rowan, Social Change Agents is a good description and just like those who understood the Internet early on, these will be the early adopters that harness social media. Do we have some good company comparisons of the power of early understanding that we can talk about from the Internet period? How about Amazon, eBay, Google and others? These companies did not exist before the Internet and now are some of our biggest and most successful. Certainly almost every business has changed how it interacts with the world as a consequence of the internet. In the same way that Facebook, Twitter and You Tube have become businesses through their pursuit of harnessing human social interaction, and are today our examples of Amazon, eBay and Google, I believe that social business is an emerging phenomenon at least as important as that of the Internet.

Title: Negotiating customer and co needs
Body: Hi Thierry, interesting questions.  If business was driven "on behalf of customers" how would it be different? Would the company be placing customers needs above their current structure (reasonable and inevitable, I think) or their own financial performance (probably never going to happen)?  I'm imagining the customer facing company staff (i.e. most staff in the future) would work to create the best win win scenario possible for the customer AND the co, but at the end of the day, the company staff will need to make decisions that work for the company.  ...   Of course there could in the future be a situation where it becomes more normal for agents to negotiate between customers and co's - in which case they'd err on the side of whomever is paying them?!  In terms of the style of transactions/interactions - I think companies will need to communicate/collaborate with and respond to customers in the ways customers dictate. For example, I recently decided not to buy a modem from a co in future if they don't have Twitter support.  Have I understood the essence of what you were suggesting or missed something? What do you think?

Title: Business models redesign
Body: You are right, the bottom line comes first. ;)  There is a thick literature on 'entrepreneurial values' and how much those are needed in big companies. I think that many business models are flawed because they structure their approach around products and services first, which means setting up supply chain, production, customer service... from an internal view of what a company should be. Socializing customers relationships (for instance) might help, but falls short when they have to react to sudden market conditions changes or losing key competitive advantages.  The  ...   needed shift is to put the customer very early in the model. Instead of developing the company, we need to develop our customers. This might imply a lot of redesign, but would result in business built around the customer, not around its own operations. Today's attempts to grow into social business merely mean facing the customer with friendlier attitudes (what you call acting the way customers dictate).  Of course, I have no actionable framework for a truly 'social business' transformation, bus I see it as shifting from a grocery where the customer is in front of a desk on which a "customer is king" notice is put down, and the clerk behind, to one where the clerk guide the customer through shelves to help him choose the best to solve his actual problem.

Title: Not necessarily the case
Body: It's not necessarily the case that a company will always act to promote the bottom line before the needs of the customer. There are counterexamples. Look at Tony Hsieh's work at Zappos: he wanted to make sure that everyone in the company was focused on delivering fantastic service to the customer. Profit would come as a consequence of doing that.  Large multi-national public companies are subject to the whims of the markets, which makes things perhaps more challenging / may make executives more averse to making that kind of cultural shift.  But  ...   models where customers are *absolutely* put first (and some models where employee satisfaction comes first) do exist and can work fantastically well. The key for them to succeed is that everyone has to trust that everyone else will do their best, and that this will delight the customer.

Title: Intelligent and self reliant people
Body: You've nailed just what is needed. Thank you.  The social media and other comms channels are just tools that get used, after that.  I'm concerned that several people see social media as a be all and end all silver bullet item, and pretty soon, it gets reduced to another item on a checklist of things to do.  ...   "Christmas party for employees" Check  "Set up an internal social network" Check  Not saying that's a bad thing and even that can lead to a surprisingly large adoption, but social media has to fit into a much broader picture. Which is what you pointed out and is worth looking at lots more.

Title: ...taking the time to think about what's relevant
Body: Suresh, thanks for your comment. I laughed when I read the part about social being on the checklist of to-do items. This drives me nuts too. You might get a smile out of this post in a different thread on that topic: https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=75424&c=44884&n=15#44884 The earlier post it relates to is here: https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=75424&c=30498&n=15#30498 I  ...   am so glad to hear you out there saying something similar :)

Title: Its not funny when it becomes real
Body: There's some people who are "corporate bloggers for hire", "blog consultants" etc that basically come in, set up social media x, y and z - and then move on once their consulting gig expires.  Sometimes, that's the only thing that remains in the company's social media strategy.

Title: Strategy first, then process, behaviour - then too
Body: Hi Ben,  you're asking the right question but in the wrong order I think  Social tools don't do anything, save maybe bring in a lot of customer opinions  If you know why some or most of them are worth listening to, you'll be able to build a business case for that, mobilise people, develop (company) behaviour  ...   and last, and maybe least, use tools to do so because you want to scale that  But IMO tools can never be the beginning - they come at the very end  Having said that, it might be a good way to bring cutsomer opinion under the attention of someone, but that would serve an entirely different goal?

Title: There is No Substitute for Passion and Raw Intent
Body: Ben, there is a very interesting Reggae song - "now that we've found love, what do we do with it?" A lot of companies approach all things "social" with a check box mentality. Its almost as if the "doing" is the "destination." Listening is a means to an end. Constantly adjusting to the listening is the ultimate sign of a living, breathing, learning company - a function of mindset, passion, and intent.

Title: Empower the employee
Body: Analysis of the social conversations is great ŌĆō not be discounted-- but for meaningful insights to be captured and considered, also look to the employees who are having those virtual conversations with customers. Give them the ability to share their ideas, impressions, etc., of their customer interactions, and obtain a collective view of the top customer trends, delights, concerns. Map those insights to what the data analysis suggests and engage the strategists across the enterprise to ŌĆ£read the tea leavesŌĆØ to see what surfaces--maybe a really get idea for new a product/service, or a tweak to a customer experience that can alleviate pain points, or something broader such as an overall brand disconnect. It is all about context, and making sure that the right people in the organization are part of the process. Otherwise you end up with mountains of data that isnŌĆÖt actionable and/or great ideas with no way to implement the change.

Title: Social Intelligence linked to Action
Body: With releases like the latest Cognos BI release that integrate corporate social software and introduction of Cognos Consumer Insight I think IBM has taken a major step forward to achieving this. When organizations start to see patterns in their market they can can now use collaborative decision making to better understand how they can take advantage of a trend or mitigate a risk. For example a company may see demand increasing for a new feature after a product launch from a competitor ( say a camera on a tablet::). This can be quantified allowing decision makers to bring that information directly into the evaluation of different product profitability scenarios or segmentation of their customer base.

Title: Most conversations are ...
Body: Margaret Millar said : ŌĆ£Most conversations are simply monologues delivered in the presence of a witness.ŌĆ£  But who was Margaret Millar? A marketer? A Digital Marketing Manager? A social Media guru?! Margaret Millar is surely one of late twentieth-century crime fictionŌĆÖs best writersŌĆ” And yet asa mystery and suspense writerŌĆ”she wrote :ŌĆ£Most conversations are simply monologues delivered in the presence of a witness.ŌĆ£  What is interesting here, is that she wrote this before 1994 when she died at 79. So, no suspense or mystery, here. just a very todayŌĆÖs comment written  ...   way back before social media even "existed". That made me ponder : Are we still having a monologue conversation where our customers and BPs remains our witnesses ?! Or are we succeeding with having a conversation with them, engaging with them. I believe that we do better each day

Title: Marketing to the next generation
Body: Will the core part of marketing strategy be defined by social in the future? With a new generation (Gen Y/Millennials) just starting to enter the workforce, I think that they will drive a new way that businesses advertise, find leads and progress opportunities. Utilizing social tools for marketing is already main stream, but lead progression and nurturing can evolve significantly through presence awareness, social analytics and mobile devices. Would love to hear from some of the students that attended Lotusphere last week to understand what drives their decision making and their ideas in how social technologies can be utilize to captivate the younger generation.

Title: Where they spend their time
Body: It's all about reaching people where they spend their time. Other posts have mentioned social channels being used as an inexpensive way to spam people, but if that's where people are spending their time, if those are the communication channels they prefer to use, then that's the right way to reach them.

Title: Spam
Body: Interesting fact, every time I 'spammed' the Jam audience about the daily update for the jam, there was a spike in logins/participation...seems like we as a community are still a bit tied to email alerts!

Title: Jam behavior
Body: It would be fascinating to analyze what drives behavior in this jam. What causes some threads to grow to tens and even hundreds of replies? What causes others to become echo chambers between two people?  It could tell us a lot about how to market in a social setting.

Title: Agree
Body: It certainly is an interesting dynamic

Title: Yes
Body: Indeed it is.

Title: Do customers want to be social with businesses?
Body: Hello, and welcome to the jam!  Over the years, as I watched businesses develop deeper customer engagement strategies, and leverage new media tools to get cloer and closer to their customer base, I've been left to wonder:  - do customers really want to engage with businesses in that way?  - what is their motivation?  - to what extent are they concerned about privacy?  - are there industries where "getting social" with customers is more appropriate than others?  Would love to hear people's perspectives and experiences!

Title: not all customers want to be social with business
Body: privacy is the main concern when going social with business

Title: It depends on the business
Body: I'd love to be social with amazon.com - them letting me know what deals, etc. are relevant to me. I am social with the deal sites (Groupon, LivingSocial) - so I can tell my other friends what the cool opportunities are.  I have no interest in being social with the company that makes my prescription medications. Not only do I not need to be reminded in this channel about my health, I don't want anyone to see it.

Title: What is a safe entry point for businesses?
Body: Yes, customers do express privacy concerns. Knowing this, do you feel that there are some safe ways for business to launch social initiatives without turning people off??

Title: How to get an understanding of trust level?
Body: I'm really trying to understand where our clients (b to b) are in terms of using social media to initiate that right entry point. Are there any best practices on understanding where that trust point is? How it varies between the client org as a whole (security, mgmt, IT) as opposed to our user whom we are interacting with extensively via phone and email? We've had suggestions/proposals ranging from client interviews, ethnographic observation, surveys to just start a Facebook page and see what happens.

Title: Privacy Concerns
Body: Privacy concerns are legitimate, but surely social media provides a large enough canvas that employees of most companies can engage without revealing private matters.  I think that establishing boundaries and then trusting employees is key. Most people don't want to put their employers in jeopardy.

Title: It all built on Trust
Body: Social interaction is a method of how information is shared and discovered. The inclusiveness and ease of sharing of these interaction model brings flexibility throughout the collaboration process. I believed it is important to provide "private" or "access controlled" community, so providing a "safe" place to collaborate openly with other community members.

Title: All bus is people is is trust bus
Body: As brewer Fred Heineken said: all business is people business. Trust is the word, for brands, for people. Thats why you like to listen (even now) to Walter Cronkite etc.

Title: It's about Risk Mitigation ...
Body: I think the risk is there but this is not about risk elimination but about Risk Mitigation. For most of our clients, when we show them the conversations happening about them, they realize that they are being talked about extensively. The point they bring home is that they need to have more risk mitigation -- as the risk is already there.

Title: mitigate the risk
Body: It is so important that companies do what they can to prepare and force the employees to act upon this "talk" or potential risk. There is a need to plan for getting all levels of employees to be ready to handle and deal with the "talk" - therefor I think it is very important to provide the right support and tools to the employees. It needs work to get the movement going and therefor a need for people to take the lead - and lead by example. If the employees in general get used to tools that support this, they have better chance to react and respond to the "talk". It is a movement and it needs to be started and planned for now.

Title: NPS
Body: Do customers really want to engage with businesses in that way?  I think that you need to focus on your promoters when involving your customers online. NPS (Net Promoter Score) gives you an easy way of finding those customers. Ask all customers to rate you on a scale from 0 to 10 how willing they are to recommend you to a friend or colleague. 9s and 10s are promoters and these will be much more willing to involve in social media with you.  "What is their motivation?"  ...   Think of Simon Sineks Diffusion of Innovation, some of your clients actually want to be early adopters and opinion makers - these are very likely to also be promoters. Support that by boosting your social capital with the group and give them the right tools to promote you. Then they'll do it because it helps themselves and boost their own ego, not so much to help you or boost your brand.  "To what extent are they concerned about privacy?"  This boils down to actually explaining up front that this is public. If they do it to boost their own ego, it would also be odd to keep it private, so privacy shouldn't be that much of a concern if your strategy is right.  "Are there industries where "getting social" with customers is more appropriate than others?"  Are there industries where products can only be "low-touch" and not involve people in the process of making them or distributing them? Maybe a coal mine, but really I have a hard time thinking of any other.

Title: Social without question
Body: I think people want to participate in a social way. So, if you are talking about Facebook and for instance are in the insurance sector (as do I) people don┤t want to hear if we have a special auto insurance for them. But maybe they will like to participate in contest about auto insurance and participate. Maybe they will join a social cause promoted by the insurance company,....  We as a company need to be prepare to talk and listen to our customer. And especially prepared to hear bad comments.

Title: Combination of Risk Mitigation and Realizing Value
Body: I think that while privacy and mitigating the sharing of what could be potentially classified as intellectual property, I think that most clients are just concerned that there will be no value add for them in the conversation.  As a part of developing our customer engagement strategies, we have to ensure that when we approach our clients that we are able to express solutions to actual problems and not waste their time. Having been on both sides of the fence, I can honestly say that at times it becomes very difficult to pull a signal from the rest of the noise that is worth listening to.  So,  ...   in summary:  1) Regardless of the method of interacting our customers (traditional face-to-face or new media tools), we need to ensure that we are providing clients with relevant information/solutions to problems they are currently facing.  2) Have a systematic and reliable method of providing focused information so as to not waste their time.  3) Consistently deliver relevant results so we can build up a trust level where the client can eventually feel confident in reaching out to us. That way we are not always relying on tools and still maintain that personal aspect of the relationship.

Title: People buy from people
Body: Anything any organization can do to make working together has to be good for the bottom line, and that's why any commercial organization is in business.

Title: Important Idea Here
Body: people buy from people  I may not want to be "friends" with all the companies I do business with, but I'm sure there are people in those orgs that I'd really enjoy connecting with on a number of levels. Opening your organization and allowing your employees to interact with customers (and potential customers) creates opportunities that you won't see with a traditional org mindset.

Title: Good point: people are social with PEOPLE
Body: A very good point here: especially when dealing with a large external organization, the sense that someone in it cares about you can be a real differentiator in terms of your feeling of satisfaction and success. This can be experienced both through the design of offerings that anticipate your needs and are useful and even pleasurable to use, as well as in the extent to which their interactions with you feel personalized and sensitive to your needs. I saw an interesting Tweet from someone at one of the Social Business talks at Lotusphere, saying that at one time, all business was social. Then technology got in the way. Now we have a way to make business feel social and personal again, on a larger global scale.

Title: Social media - a vital channel
Body: Great point - social media is becoming a vital channel in the selling process - we are familiar with potential buyers checking an organisation's website before buying - but social media adds other dimensions to that otherwise static information - namely the feeling about a product or service or the personal experience gained - this can add enormous value to the buyer and some real challenges to the seller

Title: Social media has to go beyond selling
Body: I agree and like to extend this idea further.  Being Social with business might be after all slightly overrated. Organizations tend to connect over social media with a not so social intent ŌĆō social media is a great platform that allows them to interest people with what more they can offer rather than the understanding what interests people. Like Matt put in ŌĆō a vital channel for selling.  Take a quick look at some of the social media exchanges of popular brands and read the comments (and not just the summary of the flags) that accompany  ...   the organizations pitches ŌĆō it provides a very interesting perspective about the customer / or potential buyers. a). About 8/10 are not serious about the whole social exchange, just patrons surfing the net, whiling away time. b) About 1/10 (or less) are serious buyers, but they hardly capture your attention. C) And the last 1/10 (or more) are those who found an excellent platform to express their anguish against the organizations products or servicesŌĆ” ouch!!  Social media has to go beyond selling products or services, it has to do with harvesting experiences of customers and translating that into useful actions that can result in better customer experience ŌĆō We have to think of ways to effectively engage with customers and provide adequate incentives to attract serious patrons ŌĆō the net result of the social exchange should be that total brand / product advocacy is positively affected and more people are interested in being part of that experience.

Title: Great Point: Social media has to go beyond selling
Body: I totally agree with Hansraj that companies need to look at the real value of Social services and that it is not all about selling a product or service as a short term goal. That social intercation could be the source to other valuable leads or ideas which could help in the long term goal of the company.  The only downside to not having a sales goal is that it becomes difficult to calculate a ROI for using social media.

Title: Interesting:Social media has to go beyond selling
Body: Interesting and I agree for the most part. The question is how can you harvest the customer experience and turn them into loyal patrons while at the same time balancing the fine line of ROI. There must be a tipping point? That is what I often wrestle with when thinking of these opportunities.  Thoughts?

Title: Engagement is great for universities
Body: We have thousands of people who want to engage with us -- alumni, current students, potential students, and friends. They like us, and they want to be close to us. Social media gives us the chance to "touch" them every day.

Title: It all depends on what we mean by "social"
Body: I may not want to "friend" a company, but I might be very interested in the use of social tactics to learn more about the offerings and value a company can provide to my business. Groupon is a great example in the b to c environment.

Title: People...
Body: Instead of saying customers we say people, people very much engage with companies in this way. Do all, no. Do all want to talk to someone on the phone? Do all want to go into a bank branch and talk to someone? Do all want to use the self-service till?  Of course not. This is another way to interact. As with all forms of communication, people will have preferences and some forms will have advantages over others. Having the option is probably more important. If I want to take money from my bank account I go to the till, if I want a mortgage I would like to talk to someone after playing with their online tool.  Privacy  ...   comes into it but it always has.

Title: The 'people'
Body: You hit a key combination there, 'customers' versus just 'people'. Businesses want to connect with their current customers, but they also are concerned with the potential customers as well. So when discussing socializing with a business, let's make note that I may friend, like, watch, follow, stalk, whatever your term may be, a company, it may be for my own research. But the company's interest lies in ROI. That street goes both ways.

Title: The distance between customers and business shrunk
Body: Every time I hear of a customer solving a problem via socmedia I get a smile, how many times have we heard an exec say that if they only knew of the problem, they would solve it. Some times there are filters that get in the way of communications, SM closes some of that gap.

Title: Reply to - do customers want to be social w/biz
Body: Just my opinion but...  "do customers want to engage with businesses on social?"  - I think this entirely speaks to where you are, what channel you are comfortable with as a consumer/customer and what it is you want to say.  The  ...   Gen X, Y and Millennials would probably prefer twitter over a phone call or email to a company. But why the engagement? I would have to imagine that statistics or sentiment shows "negative" questions or comments being higher than positive brand accolades. Much like my Grandmother might feel more comfortable with walking into the local bank or phoning the airline, the younger generation prefers "ease of use". How easy is it to send a tweet to a major brand with a complaint that you've had a bad experience... very.  Does it mean you will get a reply? That depends how engaged the brand is with that social channel. Will the reply be satisfactory? Well, hopefully if it is a serious issue, rather than just you blowing steam, they will make an effort to take the topic offline.  The Kevin Smith @southwest example is always highlighted or the JetBlue Valentines Day stuck on the tarmac experience, but these speak to two unique scenarios. One is a famous person, the other was a crowdsourcing/viral experience that brought on a positive customer change.  @ASk_WellsFargo @BofA_Help are two perfect banking examples that see the benefit of being out there as a csr extension into the social web. They have proven thus far that it can work and they know where to draw the limits on engagement with respect to privacy and personal information. Some customers might not be as experienced and need to be told when the conversation needs to be taken offline.  I think it all comes down to convenience and ease of use. Mobile has us more "on" than "off" and yes there is a generational gap in usage of new social media channels but that gap will get smaller. Though the banks have embraced the customer service extension into the social realm, they kept the "9-5 mentality" and say when they are "closed" or through responding to inquires for the day. That may or may not translate to a society that is expecting everyone else to always be "on" as well.  We are witnessing an interesting time in communications and it is happening right now.  Mike  @mikemost

Title: Customer expectations
Body: Great post to get the discussion going!  My initial reaction is that customers not only want to engage business in an interactive and timely manner, but they are coming to expect it. These high customer expectations are a challenge for business, in many dimensions. The rewards are high - engagement with customers drives loyalty with the opportunity to 'spread the word' quickly about your product. It also comes with competitive risks if you don't meet (and exceed) these customer expectations.

Title: Trust and Interaction across channels
Body: Customers want to be able to interact with a business across channels, including social, where they are conversing and seeking information. If you're not able to offer a seamless and transparent customer experience across channels, or at least make the effort to, your company will be missing an opportunity to build a deeper relationship with a customer. By *joining* the conversation instead of trying to dictate it, businesses can build a platform of trust.

Title: It's all about customer jobs and preferences
Body: Customers want what they want when they want it. If you focus on what the customer is trying to do, and give them a shorter path to success to get it done, you're on the right track. Social may be on this roadmap and it might not.  Social technologies can enable and provide a helpful platform for co-creation, collaboration, and community interaction.  For some customers, the social web is a preferred engagement channel. For others it isn't.  ...   The answer to this question is specific to each company, and depends on deeply understanding the prospect and customer base, knowing who they are and what they are trying to accomplish, and then engaging with them in the way that they prefer to be engaged with.

Title: Know what your customers want
Body: This is a very good point made by Brian Vellmure. As a self-employed nano business person I know that my customers prefer to hold my "products" in their hands to aid them in their purchasing decisions. Having said that, social media is giving a greater way of communicating with same said customers. In the world of Art, selling art via the virtual world is still in its infancy and 'social business' remains more of communication and general information exchange. Measuring ROI for this type of social business is too young yet to measure.

Title: Customer Service
Body: Customers may expect many things from being social with a business. Some want to get closer and have "insider" informations because they're passionate about the brand or the industry.  Others only want to make the business know they have issues with their product/service and want it to be fixed.  Being social is not only about marketing and communication...social tools are channels that can be used for many purposes. Customer service is one of the most valuable ones because it both helps to make customers happy and publicly show how the organization takes care of them.

Title: Customers social with Business? Hello Facebook
Body: I agree, it does take a changing paradigm, but we are already there in the retail space. Facebook, twitter and other links are rampant where you can ┤like┤ amazon books, your coffee maker brand, or University.  What is the motivation of the customer? Community in a virtual world, naming their unique relationships to the millions of online potential ┤friends┤.  Concerns about privacy seems to vary, foursquare is likely not used by former stalking victims, but seems to be well used by a large segment.  ...   I worry about the privacy of linking my facebook, linkedin, and yahoo accounts as all references to private, personal and commercial B2C boundaries are completely blurred.  I see the value in the wisdom of crowds in all direct services businesses. Not so much where B2B exists.  What do you think?

Title: Right media, right time, right engagement
Body: This discussion is great, but would love to hear insight into the approaching the types of customers appropriately. With all the online tracking ability, you can tell what type of customer you are working with and then just approach them in the correct way- or not at all

Title: It's not so much about wanting to be social...
Body: I think it's more about "incenting" the consumer to be social. Consumers are so concerned about privacy that you have to offer something in return for access to their information, for access to a sale even.  That's the reality- put yourself in the consumer's shoes and ask yourself: "Why Would I Like Your Brand?"  "Would I like your brand more if I offered a sea of promotions?"  "Would  ...   I like your brand more if I offered a promotional product of value that linked to a re-occuring deal via a QR Code?"  Social Media is evolving into Mobile Media. We get up, and we're attached to our smartphones- if it's accessible and there we're more likely to enter it than not.

Title: As much as things change things remain
Body: Fundamentally 'getting social' has always been part of the Customer / Vendor relationship. In my view all that has changed is the reach and ease of connection between vendor and their customers, between customers with each other and between customers and prospects.  The core reality of 'getting social' is bringing the 'word of mouth' dynamic into the 21st Century with a degree of semi-permanence for the content or 'words'.  As such buyers always;  ...   * sought recommendations from trusted sources to mitigate risks.  * sought community affiliation with people with similar interests / purchases  * sought to mobilise and warn potential buyers of poor product, service, or value  * were happy to talk about positive experiences  Vendors always;  * sought means to gather customer feedback  * build customer community and 'lock-in'  * foster and share positive customer stories  * connect with other vendors and influencers in their supply chain  This historically was achieved by non-digital means through established communities; the local village; the user group; the special interest club; the coupon club; the weekly market; the print or local broadcast media.  Now these objectives can be achieved with greater ease and with greater effectiveness if planned and executed properly and attentively.  So consequently customers want to 'be social' as much as they ever have.

Title: Perfect Way to Sum It Up.
Body: Brilliant.

Title: do customer want to get social?
Body: It depends! I think there's a transaction size/ complexity dynamic here. For small/ cheap mass market stuff, maybe not relevant beyond twitter et al; for large complex transactions (I sell IT outsourcing), then maybe there's scope through a protracted engagement process.  There's lots of specific content to share, so engaging directly and giving each party sight of what the other is working on *could* be great, for the right customer.  The *could* caveat is down to confidence in the security.

Title: Interest in being social varies in B2C vs B2B
Body: There are different motivations for being social depending on the business. A number of people mention groupon, which is B2C and has a clear value proposition (for some people, at least).  But if I am a CIO or a HR executive or a Java developer, I might have a different view of the value of being social. An executive might be interested in being social with their peers, in a private social group (is that an oxymoron?). A Java developer might find value in meeting other developers whom they might not have a chance to meet in 'real life'. So as with everything, we need to think about user segmentation and apply social appropriately!

Title: IBM VP for Digital Strategy speaks at Roundtable
Body: IBM's, Ben Edwards, recently of The Economist, participated in a Social and the Enterprise Roundtable, at Dartmouth College's Tuck Business School. http://www.tuck.dartmouth.edu/digital/ Social Media is not a new way of going after a familiar market but a new way of establishing genuine connection with prospects, clients and employees. As well, helps to address the need to better retain both key clients and employees.

Title: Do customers want to be social with businesses?
Body: Customer desire a trustworthy relationship with a valued brand, imo.  What I don't want: visit local credit union where I've been customer for 15 years. Have 6 figure deposit. Lifeless, tedious and lonely environment. Even though I have retirement and college financing needs, no one ever explores with me. Attitude: what do you want- albeit with a smile.  What I want: visit any Apple store. Experience is same as my iPhone, iTunes, their web site etc. Regardless of my age or appearance, green haired girl spends 20 minutes discussing merits of a $400 hard drive. Welcoming, alive, connected. Attitude: we know why you are here. USAA and IKEA and VanCity  ...   are similar, related examples.

Title: Do customers want to be social with businesses
Body: I don't think they do unless it is about recruiting and what the company culture is like. So I don't think product push is the way to go for most companies. The question is how do we back into a company being spoken about yet make an affiliative connection? How can our employees help us with this?

Title: B2C has led the way... Bring on B2B
Body: The alignment of Social relating to consumers dealing with businesses is proven. Who hasn't looked at LifeHacher and other sites to evaluate new product purchaces or joined a forum before purchase. What I am more keen to see is the evolution of B2B Social... yes there are bloggers out there, but the ability to see a more intuitive, social review around B2B related products in dealing with clients...

Title: Do customers want to be social with businesses?
Body: I think that customers want to be social with business if they find an advantage in doing this. "What is in for me?"  I guess this has more chances to happen when someone buys something which has an "added value" and, thus, she/he expects some form of "engagement" from the company selling that value. Proving with facts how the interaction with customers has been important in changing how my company is doing business, would be really a killer message, imo.  I tend to think also, that the more the "social" gets into B2C, the more people will be overwhelmed with participation in too many networks... So, it will be important to always think to whom I want to interact with. Perhaps it would be interesting to see clusters of companies aggregating around a common  ...   form of social interaction with customers, in order to keep a high level of interaction and to also add an additional value to the sum of the parts.

Title: Do customers want to be social?
Body: Customers also will try to engage with business and their experiences could drive further engagement or withdrawal.  Their motivation could be getting more and taking informed decisions and hence getting things "most of the time right".  They will be concerned with privacy very much. this I think would be a high priority and can make or mar this space.  To start with yes. Less damaging, less mistakes or those areas that do not affect customers unduly even if they go wrong might benefit from "getting social". On the other hand, those industries where "benefits will outweigh all kinds of risks" would also benefit immensely from "getting social" with customers --these two areas customers will respond better.

Title: Social Media and privacy are parallel but differen
Body: Being connected to a business socially does not necessarily mean sharing social information. Sharing a like or a product does not even mean I bought it. Yet some then associate that I must have bought it to use it.  Being able to reach and communicate in a forum that fosters more communication helps drive business and gives the feeling the company listens.  The type of industry being social does not matter. The type of data collected and required for the consumer to be social does.

Title: More and more every month
Body: The interaction with customers via social is increasing commensurate with the messaging that we are participating and welcoming feedback via social channels. To be sure it is a minority for sure, but definately growing.  We have found that if you are offering good advice, tips and tricks, etc. the followers tend to appear. If you are just spamming marketing information not so much. There needs to be good synergy between blogs, tweets and social collab.

Title: Business objectives drive the approach to social
Body: The strategic objective of customer relationship management is a fundamental component that needs to be structured correctly when addressing the question of "Do customers want to be social with businesses".  If the interaction with the customer is first identified and aligned with the company's strategy, it can then be executed with increased effectiveness by leveraging the appropriate tools and approach. The approach becomes key where the solutions are just an enabler. This is a big challenge for companies because of the many options available and so much communication taking place from all areas of an organization.  How  ...   do you create better alignment towards the company's strategic objectives so that social media interaction with the customers supports rather than hinders the objectives. For such a task, modeling is critical. Establishing effective, clear and visual models that remove noise and draw out the significant variables of customer relationship management can assist with such complexity. These models can provide a succinct perspective that can then be executed on, validated and refined based on external and internal forces that impact the overall results.

Title: Being Social isn't the same as Friending
Body: Fifth Third bank wants me to friend them on Facebook. I may like their services, but my life would be depressing if I considered them a friend.  The point is, I think consumers definitely want to talk to companies via social networking, but it has to be on their terms and through the channels they want. That might be via Twitter or Blogs and in some cases, could include Facebook. However, its important to know your customers well enough to understand which channels will work.

Title: Can't be social with a BRAND!
Body: Customers can and will participate in the social dialogue but it needs to be be with the people or the "faces" of the brand. Personalities within organizations I believe are underated. These key personalities can shape the conversation, engage customers who want engagement and offer real value. In return customers should become more loyal and more in tune with the brand. Apple has seen great retail success through this type of personal social engagment at their retail stores.

Title: Yes clients want to chat to you if you are social!
Body: Especially the upcoming consumer -- they want to be social. They want to work for companies that are social and they want to do business with people that are social.

Title: Folks wont be social with brands just to be social
Body: I agree it's important to social with a real face and voice -- it's hard bing social with a neutral, non-descript entity. For a moment think bck to a masquerade prty you may have attended. The comfort level of interacting with your friends behind a 'mask' was markedly lower than when the masks came off, wasn't it?  It's the same with brand interactions.  I always tell clients that it is important to put a smile, a twinkle in the eye, warmth in the handshake -- no, not of just face to face interactions --  ...   but social media interactions too.  It's possible, yes it harder work than being bland and outsourcing social media management, but it's possible. And rewarding too.

Title: Customers want to be listened
Body: I think that Customers are happy to engage with "their" Brands. At the begining it can be out of curiosity to see how a big and distant brand react to one single voice.  However, as soon as the customers felt listened, their reaction is extremely responsive in terms of participation and feedback and viral propagation of the communication mechanism.  Unfortunately, from I have seen in Brand pages in Facebook for example the WALL is just one-way broadcasting of news/publicity and not the real conversational medium it should be.

Title: Do customers want to be social with businesses?
Body: Yes - for better customer support. Customers can always get better support by knowing vendors better and developing people relationships within the vendor organization.  It is in the interest of both parties to push people relationships - and what better way than though social networks.  Sesh Raj  dsapps.com

Title: Customers being social with business
Body: Human Beings are social and certainly would like to be involved with various institutions. When it comes to business, the question arises how social one wants to be.  Thinking from a business owner perspective, they would want to know their customers / clients very well. Often retailers have discounts for loyal, brand conscious customers and understand their buying behavior and patterns. So, businesses use marketing strategy to target their best customers with various offerings.  A  ...   customer would want a business to offer him/her the best price. With the explosion of smart phones, the best price of any item is moments away. Yet, customers prefer their brand irrespective of the price. With databases stacking every item a customer purchases, how close should a business get to a customer?  Privacy is key here. Would a drug store want to send a list of future drug purchases based on past purchases? Probably a bad idea. The same offer if done by an electronic / clothing / other retail store, would be termed smart and customer savvy.  To give an example of a predictive model I worked on, one of the most famous brands in the world wanted to test various types of car advertisement based on specific areas within a US city. So the question was, should the adv be targeted at the household (HH) or zip+4 or DMA (Designated Market Areas) level. With the given laws, HH or Zip+4 was getting too personal/ inappropriate and hence, the advertisements were chosen to air on a suitable network at the DMA level in that city. Based on the sales of that specific type of car, the model was tweaked with inputs from car dealers and customized to specific cities across the US at a DMA level.  The bottom line is customers want to get social with businesses that do not interfere with their privacy and yet understand their needs in order to offer them value based on pricing.

Title: Engaging for a reason?
Body: I agree with the earlier comment that it's not so much customers engaging with the business as it is people engaging with other people to achieve some goal.  I was at a talk earlier today where the group was asked to answer a "what if you were in this situation" question from the perspective of several different people, both inside and outside the company. The story that the speaker was trying to spin was that the most engaged people are those who have a genuine interest in the outcome of the interaction.  If  ...   I am a customer, what is my interest in the outcome of the interaction? What is my interest in the company through which the interaction happens? Do I wish them success (or ill)?  If the "social" helps me through an interaction, maybe this is a good thing.  There are many directions these thoughts could go. Jam away.

Title: Getting social
Body: I think there are some great use cases where the social interaction can have a lot of value to both parties. These cases are a bit different than the social analytics/recommendation examples mentioned by others in this thread.  Customers want to be heard and feel like they are getting great service. The social interaction can be a great way to provide feedback and report problems. If the company is quick to respond to the concerns, that builds brand loyalty and in the process could trickle down to future product improvements.  Another  ...   example might be in the innovation space for product development. Post the challenge and crowd source ideas... Not so different from this JAM!

Title: Staggered approach to Social
Body: I think every businesses want and need to be Social today. But this should be approaches in a controlled and staggered manner.  There are two aspects to Social, the External (outside the organization) and the Internal (within the organization).  Three points in the Value Chain where External interaction can be leveraged is Product Development, Sales and Customer Service.  Social tools can also be highly effective for internal collaboration and idea sharing specially during the product development phase.  ...   Social adoption should be approached in a staggered manner which should be tightly coupled with the objectives for adoption.  As a start businesses can use social media for new product development ideas and as a sales strategy where connect with the external world is required.  Adoption of social media tools within the organization can be leveraged for collaboration within globally dispersed design teams, cross functional teams etc. At the next stage the boundaries of the organization can be extended to select partners and suppliers. This can be highly effective for global businesses which have grown through mergers, acquisitions and JVs.

Title: Do customers want to be social with business?
Body: Customers are people, too. Absolutely - Web 2.0 flies because "sharing" is a very elemental human desire. Whether customers instantly flock to a company's social media offering - who cares! What matters is that it's there - the mere fact that it's there is a huge statement that says "We're here for you, our door's open, come in anytime, we welcome you." Engagement follows.  When it comes to B2B - those customers are also people. They may not be retail customers but that's irrelevant. Social media engagement allows B2B to show their social engagement, latest expertise offerings, speed, flexibility, personality. B2B surveys often reveal that customers perceive B2Bs as distant. Social media can make a huge difference in that respect. B2Bs also stand to greatly benefit in ways beyond the customer. By engaging in social media they can reach and positively influence shareholders, regulators, media, etc. etc.

Title: Yes and No
Body: #NAME?

Title: Need to be social with a person, not a business
Body: We have to be mindful that social is about connecting with people, so a "business" can't be social, but people that work in that business have to create and cultivate relationships. We all need to be mindful of this when we "ghost write" blog content or try to tweet from a generic ID...just not as effective or authentic.

Title: Does business understand the term "like"?
Body: Social Media is full of provocative words such as "Friend" and "Like" that I think we as private users know are just diluted terms that are a neccessary part of using the platform. For instance on Facebook I think we often use the term "like" more as an acknowledgement when we don't feel compelled to comment rather than as an indication of strong opinion.  I'm not sure business fully appreciates this and I think that there is a danger that businesses can view their "likers" as being an active group of advocates when the truth is that many people in the group will have visited once to interact with some content and may never retern  Businesses  ...   need to consider whether people "like" them or their content. I think its the latter and so it becomes important in a Social Strategy to keep re-engaging people (even the people you think are "likers") with compelling content that encourages the community to return and interact.

Title: Customers want to be heard, not be social
Body: This is a great question.  I think at one level, marketers confuse 'doing social' with 'being social' -- just because one communicates via a social media platform, it does not mean that one is showing 'social' behaviour. Perhaps something to ponder over.  That said, I believe that customers are interested in having a means of bing heard by brands and their views being acknowledged, responded to and ideally accepted and acted upon. That is at heart what drives a customer's interactions with a brand. Not 'socializing' with a brand. For that, the customer  ...   has his family, friends and real 'social' circle.

Title: Quality of service
Body: There will need to be a visible commitment of resources i.e. of social business competent people - actual headcounts- to attract customers..These people should have the power to actually change, influence the way their companies work. The year long tradition of using call centers to ward off customers, keeping them in endless phone loops, with people on the lines unable to solve problems as is often the case with telecom providers has made people weiry. And wary of the seriousness of wanting to really listen to customers.

Title: Less about "social" and more about sharing
Body: I believe it's less about if customers want to be social with business. Customer's absolutely want to have an open and free flowing dialogue with business in pertinent topics. But more importantly, they'd like to hear about what others are saying about the business they are engaging with or trying to make a decision on whether they will do business with them or not. They want to hear the candor from other customers about the customer products, quality of support, and the value of the offerings from other customers. This may help them to improve their current use of their solutions or make other purchasing decisions.

Title: Do customers want to be social - Yes
Body: We've tested this in IBM. Customers appreciate guidance from vendors - especially when it can be given quickly in near real-time from multiple sources. Imagine that a customer is in a meeting and gets a to-do to answer to a question and make a recommendation. The customer sends out a tweet. By the end of the meeting he gets a few responses and recommendations. That customer is already on their way to find the right solution.

Title: We must consider Social as a new channel
Body: Reading some of the posts it looks like we are thinking social as "the channel". With a lot of advantages of others, we must avoid thinking SM as the best and only one channel to reach our customers (either B2C or B2B).  It looks like it is better for B2C, and easier to adopt. However, people is part of companies and will take advantages of this not structured communication way.  We must not close/discard all the previous available channels

Title: Social with social services
Body: I am a customer of "social services" that are not-for-profit organizations. They rely on their consituents to keep them in business. They are beginning to embrace social media to spread the word, in fundraising, in advocacy and in reaching new "cusomters". Because these organizations focus on people with disabilities, there is an extra level of concern among the "customers" about privacy.  With cuts in funding these social service organizations need to now rely even more on their consituency.

Title: Every Country, Every Industry
Body: As I have traveled the planet and met thousands of businesses since we launched Connections in 2007, today there is not a country or industry segment that does not have many many client references and case studies of folks that have embraced social software,..changing the way they work,.. and becoming a social business.

Title: Social business - means what? To me...
Body: People are social, they choose what they want to share, with whom, and how. Not just to friends, but also to companies. We share personal information with banks, retailers etc. Companies want to convert more consumers to customers and retain them (for most part). Companies try to learn more about customers to provide more value (from automated payments to shipping details to past purchases).  As long as the two rules are met:  1. Companies collect information from customers with their consent  2.  ...   People (not companies) share to others what they want to share  ..Social business is already on its way.  The rest of the rules: information security, privacy, compliance etc are all part of business already dealt with.  So the real question becomes: can companies leveraging other people's insights to provide better service to the individual? Seems yes, as long as the identity is not extractable. For e.g. companies learn from prior customer support engagements on how to better their product or service. Companies collect feedback from customers on how to better serve them - this becomes yet another source of feedback on what customers want / how companies can better serve their customers.

Title: Customers want an "experience" not just a product!
Body: Just a short response from my personal perspective: there are definitely companies that I, even as a jaded customer, believe I have a personal relationship with. My favorite airline, my favorite laptop brand, and if I believe they reciprocate this "loyalty" in even the smallest way I'm willing to extend them my digital support (like them) and some slack. In thinking about this, I think products and services that customers "experience" rather than just "use" are the ones most likely to benefit from social relationships. And if you're clever, even if you sell widgets, your brand is more about the experience then the widget. Coke, Gatorade, Apple, lots of examples.  So this is a key value proposition that Social Business provides even the most modest, practical business--the opportunity to transition your customer relationship from utility to experience. I'm reminded of Aristotle's 3 levels of friendship--utility, shared likes, and shared values. If your business  ...   proposition is utility, your hold on customers will be much more tenuous than if you share experiences and even better values with your customers.  Am I the First Person to mention Aristotle in the jam?

Title: Consider *all* the customers - be accessible
Body: Social businesses need to involve all their customers, not a select few. Otherwise they are leaving a percentage of customers out in the cold.  Businesses should include capability for participation for those with disabilities (physically limited, blind, poor vision, mobility impaired, deaf, hard of hearing). One would never leave out the retired population, but they are experiencing disabilities: aging sight, mobility, and hearing needs to be able to effectively use the tools.  Worse, leaving out customers may be more than lost revenue, it may leave exposure to risk for discrimination, as has been experienced by several companies  ...   in the past few years (e.g. Target, Apple).  By ensuring social applications are usable by all, businesses get involvement from *all* customers, and probably gain more than before.

Title: Think B2B, B2C or above?
Body: Traditional business route has B2B and B2C, but at this moment we see "Social to Social(S2S)", "Social to Customer(S2C)" and "Customer to Customer", actually, I think. My on-line shopping attitude also like "S2C".

Title: Collusion of Mediocrity
Body: In many cases the "social" element is part of an unspoken collusion of mediocrity. The engagement can be insipid and diluted because of the wish to avoid calling the mistrusting relationship that often exists between customer and supplier by its real name - mistrust!.  The prevalence of small print, the distortion of language (For example, where an "Unlimited Data Usage" policy for a mobile phone is subject to a small print fair use policy that actually renders "unlimited" into "limited" in all but name), and the general usually unspoken thought that the customer thinks the supplier is out to benevolently entangle them, often leads to fake dialogue more based on false niceness and hype than real dialogue.  The  ...   solution I think is that all social engagement has to be asterix free and devoid of any small print and hidden terms and conditions in a customer supplier relationship.  The motivation on both sides has to be fair dealing, honesty and authentic openness. All are often lacking, both sides collude and the result is mediocrity.

Title: To be social with business in different ways
Body: Clients prefer different ways to engage...attending f.e. events are no the most efficient way to be informed about topics of your interest...remark of one of CIO about cloud: ŌĆśYou donŌĆÖt see me visiting a cloud seminar anymoreŌĆÖ tells this CIO, ŌĆśIf I want to know something about cloud I look in the cloud called YouTube.'

Title: It's about tangible value
