Body: Customers seek tangible value from business relationships. So, it's more than being 'social'. It's about combining or integrating social ways of communicating and connecting in a business sense that enables greater value. The lesson - don't just do social and think customers will come. Intermix social techniques with business value and they will come. For example, if a customer is considering a transaction over the web, it might be valuable to compare with a community of others prior to making a decision. And, while in the community be prompted with a discount or some kind of offer.

Title: Engaging yes but not be friends
Body: Building on Rebecca's post, I think there are differences in being social. It is not industry only, it is the way how to be social.  Her example of Amazon reminded me that I am very happy to contribute to provide my feedback, to make suggestions for improvement, share new business ideas with companies. I interact with a number of companies in that way and this kind of 'being social' is absolutely fine with me.  I am actively contributing to platforms like trip advisor and if a hotel, restaurant etc., including if I am asked if I would mind to share my feedback. I will be more than happy to share my opinion and experience(whatever it is and sometimes they might not like it).  ...   I am not becoming friends with companies on social networks like facebook. For me, here the risk is too high that if I become a 'friend' and I am not absolutely careful about my settings, everybody might have access to all kind of private photos, my friends etc. and I definitely don't want that.  The best offer gets ignored by me if I have to become facebook friend of a company to get access to the special offer. It makes me so angry that I don't buy at all anymore from that specific company.  I would be happy to provide feedback to pharmaceutical companies if I can decide to use a creative name and character and not my real name.  The answer is it depends and how can you be social with companies.

Title: Do customers want to be social with businesses?
Body: Business are using all sorts of methods of collaborating to get work done and the outcome is that they are becoming social businesses whether they want to be or not. All too often a company is leveraging "social" tools within their business and they don't realize the impact on security, productivity, client satisfaction and risk. The elements of what is at stake are immense. Business need to find the strategic and sound way forward since Social business is inevitable.

Title: Social media is a utility - Communities are key
Body: Social media technologies such as facebook should be viewed as utilities that enable communities to form and evolve. There are competing social media networks in China where facebook doesn't exist...and hundred of millions of community members. Although these social "utilities" can generate value/revenue, it is the communities and their actions that are exponetially more valuable. Without engaging and learning about these utilities we may miss out on all of the innovation that is being created in those communities. Innovations that provide business improvements, social utility and in some cases radical social change.

Title: Customers want to be social ABOUT businesses
Body: By looking at social media using search tools it is clear that customers are discussing businesses/ brands as well as other topics. The start point for a business looking to engage is to bring value to those existing conversations, rather than trying to own them. Part of this is accepting that some aspects of the conversations will be negative in tone, and working out the right way to respond - acknowledge, refute, ignore etc. If customers are having problems getting value from a business's products or services, the business will want to be part of solving those problems, but also support other customers who want to solve them with peer to peer interactions. Trying to move the various online conversations towards more of a supportive community provides immediate benefits to customers and businesses (problems fixed faster, the ability to feedback comments and concerns, all through a comparatively low cost channel) whilst growing longer term value in terms of trust and loyalty. That's not to say that poorly handled social engagements by businesses cannot do more harm than good, so this all needs to be part of a well thought through customer experience initiative - but done properly, customers will and do value when businesses take part in the conversation.

Title: Latest IBV CRM Study - It's all about value
Body: In our latest IBV CRM study ŌĆō due to be launched very shortly - we asked this very question. Do customers want to engage with businesses via social media, and if so ŌĆō what are the triggers? If not, what are the inhibitors? Turns out most customers are very pragmatic. While a little more than half doesnŌĆÖt engage with businesses at all this way, those who do want to get something of tangible value in return: discounts, coupons, etc. And they want to use social media to transact. Good news for those companies that want to monetize social media! Customers were far less interested in engaging with companies to feel closer to the brand or joining a community. Concerns over privacy and getting lots of spam were among the top reasons customers were reluctant to engage.

Title: LISTSERVER. Open Source.
Body: I just thought of LISTSERVER reading your posted question. I do not know if this is relevant or not. But IT folks (customers) used to subscribe to LISTSERVERs a lot. Then maybe it what started the Open Source. I do not know. I am just speculationg.  Open Source is customer and company combined. Everyone is part of the identity. There is no company/provider nor customer. All is one.  So customer shape up the business actually and the company provides the vision.  ...   Hope to get a summarized compilation of your voted best ideas that replied to your question.  Thanks

Title: It's not the #s Baby! Faux Followers
Body: Some businesses (and people) seem to think that the number of fans, followers or friends you have is the end game. It is not the number baby!!! It is the quality of the follower. The tippers if you would.  Seth Godwin wrote ŌĆ£Many brands and idea promoters are in a hurry to rack up as many Facebook fans and Twitter followers as they possibly can. Hundreds of thousands if possible. A lot of these fans and followers are faux. Sunny day friendsŌĆ” A slightly better idea defeats a much bigger but disconnected user base every time. The lesson: spend your time coming up with better ideas, not with more (faux) followers.ŌĆØ  What do you think?

Title: Fauslowers
Body: Could not agree more.. http://fauxlowers.com/ service helps identify fauxlowers..

Title: tried it but ..
Body: Didn't see anything back. Have you used this service?

Title: Contagious
Body: A better goal is to have contagious ideas, and the RIGHT followers who will distribute it into influential channels. Now, having a lot of followers certainly increases one's chances of having the right followers, but we'd do better by saying smart things when the right people are listening -- that encourages them to listen to what else we have to say and share those ideas with others.  Ironically, it's a bit like Watson. It may come up with hundreds of potential answers to a Jeopardy question, but knowing which is likely right, and only saying it when it is sufficiently confident, builds its credibility. That credibility, in turn, catches the attention of people who marvel at its smarts.

Title: Love "contagious ideas"
Body: @Joshua Great turn of a phrase and a nice way to describe what creating interest/engagement/influence is all about in social influence marketing.

Title: Love the Watson analogy!
Body: Great discussion point.  How do you attract and find those influencers?

Title: Attracting influencers
Body: Very interesting discussion point. It is always seen that water gets the color it is mixed with. In the same manner, you would need to attract, to attach, to follow and to reciprocate online influencer. Understand what message they are carrying forward, how they are sharing that with their followers and try to tune your goals, interests and messages accordingly. Similarity of thoughts/ideas, reciprocation/sharing and being master of engaging contents could attract influencers like ships guided by lighthouse.

Title: There's Life Beyond The 'Like'
Body: I totally agree that it's not the number of fans or followers one has via social media, but the quality of those fans. How many are customers? Loyal customers? Brand advocates? Even potential customers?  Too many marketers get caught up in the rush to tote up Fan numbers -- I've seen so many brands that have an attractive offer up on their Facebook pages, with a catch: one needs to Like the page before one can see the full offer and avail of it. What does that result in? Lots of Likes for sure, but how many folks truly like the brand? Perhaps fewer than might have if the brand didn't resort to this twisted way of pulling them in!  In  ...   fact, I've seeded a discussion around this very theme on my blog on The Future Of Digital For Brands that I run as a LinkedIn Group at http://linkd.in/hUPZqf. I am happy to say, most folks agree that what counts isn't fan base, but fan quality.

Title: How do you find the high quality ones?
Body: I am really interested in how others do this one.

Title: Finding High Qualtiy "Ones"
Body: @ Sandy - Check out my ohter post that has a link to Forrester analysis of listening platforms.  I think that is the key method to find out who the most influencial individuals are, and let's you easily start building a relationship with them.

Title: Smart Social CRM can help find Life beyond Likes
Body: In order to determine the real valuable Fans and Followers -- the folks who have real Brand Love (beyond the superficial Like on a Fan page), it takes a re-orientation of the social communication strategy.  Most marketers start out with a bunch of themes (their agenda), and then prioritize them giving weights to ech theme. This is followed in what and how often they communicate via their social communication plan.  Social CRM flips this around and looks at key consumer themes -- tied back to determining the 'loyalty/love' facets the marketer wants to understand.  Social  ...   communication then takes this focus -- one may find lower engagement in terms of Likes and Comments but the engagement tha ensues will be more meaningful from the point of uncovering real valuable ('Loving') customers.  This bunch can then be nurtured, both via social media and through more conventional CRM, eNurture means offline.  Unfortunately too many marketers wake up to this reality only when they have 100s of 1000s of fans, instead of adopting this approach from the outset.

Title: Create content which inspires your network
Body: A lot of tweets, messages, pings etc is more about to give yourself a stage, because no one is really listening to you in daily live instead of telling something really interesting to share.  Train yourself to inspire your followers, fans, friends in 140 char. and you will get the rights fans. The ones who also like you in the "real" world. Keep in mind that making use of social media has a lot of impact on your personal branding.

Title: Do you have a way to find those tippers?
Body: I am curious how companies do this "finding" their 1% that drive the rest of the community .. on twitter in 140 characters and beyond.

Title: idea for getting the good twitter followers
Body: It would be great to merge a Saleplace/Salesforce type of application with links to Twitter and be able to track what they say about your business on Twitter/Facebook/Yelp/Foursquare/GoWalla. In exchange for being "friended" you can give discounts for products/services you offer as a business.

Title: How do we measure influence
Body: Within the scope of a set of terms and hashtags (e.g. #smarterplanet) I'd blend a few metrics:  Number of retweets and @ mentions  Number of times a link is followed -- should be able to get this information from the URL shorteners  Number of followers seems like an OK number, but the above 2 metrics measure influential actions.  Truly  ...   influential Tweeters would have their Tweets retweeted by people who don't follow them.  In contrast, The recent "Most Influential People on Twitter" ( http://www.informationarchitects.jp/en/c140/ ) seems more focused on the number of followers.

Title: It's about geting to the Prosumers
Body: We at Euro RSCG follow a method of identifying Prosumers -- that 10-15% of consumers who are spreaders of opinion. It's important to note that these are not Trend setters, but the spreaders of trends... Information is their currency of influence, and since the internet is where there's lots of information, these folks are always on, connected, switched on, in the digital space...  Key is culling these folks out, and putting information into their hands, an giving them access to the latest before anyone else... and then letting them loose, to spread influence via their networks, and beyond. (Think of the ripples in water when you toss a stone in).  A  ...   program I am very gung-ho about is 'Subject Matter Expert Curation' -- which goes beyond th usual suspect SME to create over time a growing community of experts who through their networks cn spread a brand's POV further anf farther.... 140 characters at a time perhaps.

Title: The tippers are social by nature
Body: The tippers in an organization are social both on and offline by nature. They are the connectors of the world who are always making introductions based on expertise to friends, family and colleagues. They are also usually not afraid to speak their mind or approach people they may not know personally in real life, but may know by association (degrees of separation thought that we are all somehow interconnected).

Title: Quality vs Quantity
Body: Until people realize that the social medium changes how we view media, this thought process won't change. The fact that advertising has morphed its model for the internet making a bigger monetary impact for clicks opposed to just view is along the same idea.

Title: 80/20 rule in social media
Body: Seems to me that the rule applies in this space, too: 80% of your quality social interactions will happen with 20% of your social network. So Sandy is asking how you identify who that 20% is?  I think you need some kind of dashboard to look at engagement and identify which topics from your networks (Twitter, FB and LI) generate the most RTs, replies and DMs. Then you also need to look at who your top engagers are, then you can see what they have in common. I know of tools that do the former, but not the latter....

Title: social media
Body: Counting and analyzing RTs, link sharing and dashboard are all the tactical tools of strategy to find out, to attract and to retain influencers. Before you start applying these analysis tools, you would need to think through message you want to spread, relationships you would like to build, tune to needs and necessities of your surrounding ( internal/external) customers and plan accordingly. The complexity of online social interaction is multi-folds as the technology allows you to reach not only to one nation but be international, going beyond physical boundaries of countries, cultures, businesses and connect to varied crowd. So one message to suite all may not be easily possible. So strategical planning of these endeavors and relative governance would be very necessary.

Title: some factors
Body: I measure the relevance of my network based on how much buzz they create when I share unique and compelling content. Are they sharing, re-publishing, commenting, driving progressive results in light of my hard work?  Too many folks get bogged down scratching their heads over flat responses to their average posting activity and media publishing. Not all you say and do online will spread like wildfire. Your average posting activity is to keep your core followers interested in you and what you are sharing, and to keep the fringe folks at least tuned in and connected. If you balance out your efforts and nurture your network through the lulls, the moments when you have something big to share will pay off in spades.  ...   The problem I see most is the lack of time many spend nurturing their relationships in meaningful ways, pending your big post of the month/week/ whatever. Are you able to be interesting in the lull? Are you able to stay human and likeable in the lull? Do you have the time (or are you being given the time) to do all of this had holding properly? Are you even a decent publisher who can offer compelling and unique information on a regular basis?  I agree, Sandy, that pure numbers of followers don't count, but when the numbers reflect conversations started, sharing stats, and search rankings enhanced, you have tackled the first big step toward success.

Title: Social Business Network
Body: I build my network as if I were building my own small business. Who would I want on my team? What skills do they bring to the table? What collective knowledge could we bring to a problem. I imagine my network as a "consulting firm focused on the Payments Industry".

Title: Like your own small business...
Body: But online there are so many people you don't know. How do you get to know them before you select them?  Sandy

Title: personal brand
Body: If your personal brand is strong enough, and if you are interesting enough, they select you IMO. You do the focus work as it all moves along, deciphering who they are, where they work, what they do, and how well they communicate and add value to the network. The personal hunt and peck is fine, but if you have fantastic content you are offering to the world, they will find you and make your efforts less strenuous.

Title: Trusted referrals outperform following 10,000
Body: Social media works as people business. Behavioral traits, such as relationship and trust, will maximize the value derived from followership. Similar to person-to-person communications, quality social media exchanges will build a mutual understanding of the expertise, needs, and communication styles of each participant. Positive results will prompt more trusted and richer interactions. At such point, information referred to by a trusted social media source will be more relevant and actionable to the recipient than an ocean of creative but possibly ambiguous or irrelevant tweets.

Title: Content is King but the Analyst rules the Kingdom
Body: A consistent focus on the quality content for your target audience and followers/fans will grow. But in order to produce quality content for your "target" audience...you must focus on the follower data and use great analysts/tools. Analytics are key to finding the people who want and need to hear your messages.  Jeremiah Owyang has a great blog post on elements of social data...I am sure there are others out there.  The 7 Elements of Social Data  ...   We segmented data that has been tried and true for years on the top tier: demographics and product. Then, we segmented data that digital marketers are striving to tackle now in the middle tier: Pschographic, behaviroial, and refferal data. At the bottom tier, we listed out experimental new types of data that most companies have not harnessed, the newcommers location based and intention data. http://www.web-strategist.com/blog/2011/02/08/seve_elements_of_social_data/ Look at Mashable's view of Pepsi's Social Media Command Center powered by analytics http://mashable.com/2010/06/15/gatorade-social-media-mission-control/

Title: Facebook fans
Body: I agree with Sandy on fake fans or followers in Facebook or Twitter. The virtual or the FB world is full of fakes. ItŌĆÖs difficult to differentiate them from the real customers or even the Brand Ambassadors. They Brands boasts of 50,000 or a million fans. There is little or no interaction with the customers from the Brand owners. Only effective interaction with the customers can help in growing their business rather than having stupid posts on their pages. A company to keep or to get new its customers should actively engage them in activities.

Title: Not the Numbers - Quality of Relationship
Body: In any relationship -- business or social -- it's not how many relationships you have (most humans can only manage about 100 hundred friends -- the size of a village), but rather their quality.  I always judge real friends by a simple (imaginary) test: if I called them in the middle of the night and asked them to come meet me at the main intersection of our city, would they come? If you're lucky you have a few friends like that.  With on-line friends, you need to judge by on-line standards:  ...   1. How many of them read and respond to your posts (ever notice it's usually the same people who either love you or hate you?).  2. If you ask them for a favor -- friend "him," read this and respond if you agree, join this group -- how many will turn up for you? I bet it's never more than 10%  3. Do they ask you for further opinions, attempt to create ongoing conversations? Just how engaged are they?  It's the quality relationships that will carry you forward in your business objectives.

Title: Articulated networks are special
Body: One of the defining aspects of social software is the ability to define "relationships" with other people. These usually visible "articulated networks" are incredibly important for underlying social analytics to do information recommendations and filtering.  We have been experimenting with a research project to dynamically create friend lists to better filter information and enhance serendipitous discover.

Title: It's not the #s Baby! Faux Followers
Body: So perfect analysis of social follower-ship and campaign success. It is quality of your followers. It is how you engage your followers. It is not how many are following you back. Sometimes followers want to rub off the goodwill from bigger brands themselves by associating and following. Online social endeavors are similar to in person networking events. You do not have to start selling to crowd but to engage, to like and to reciprocate for relationship building. And sometime small strong relationship which can spread your message far long and deep.

Title: Research worked on this
Body: I saw an interesting project at the Innovation labs at Lotusphere out of IBM Research Dublin that figured out a particular way to measure influence in online environments--really any environment but they used Twitter as the example.  The difference is that it actually looks at the semantics of discussion--a very technologically challenging problem--to understand topical relationships, and combined it with particular influencers, and their influence activity as well. Compared to Klout which seems to focus primarily on counting propagation of messages and not on semantics.  Yes,  ...   it is still data-supported, but it is a much deeper look at the problem. I think the developer was Marie Wallace but I can ask Mike Roche from Dublin labs to find out more. [Yes, this is shareable public]

Title: Graph Theory can help
Body: There is more than one type of "follower" or "friend" in a social network. There are at least 4 types:  - Your Targets: people you want to reach out to  - Gatekeeper: knows the targets  - Pulsetaker: knows the most people who know the target people  - Hubs: knows the most people  ...   Graph theory (which predates online social networks) can answer some of these questions, at the academic level today.  The key is know your targets (which can change based on offer/topic/discussion/event, etc...), you can apply these algorithms to your connections to maximize the return on campaigns towards your existing follower/fan base.  At the end of the day, it comes down to how many fans/followers you can get to actively "engage" in a given particular marketing activity. Engaging (which can take the form of comments, content creation, etc...) allows your message to spread way beyond your fan base.  And for any of those techniques to work (be statistically significant), a sizable fan base (without particular focus on quality) is needed.

Title: 1000 True Fans
Body: Kevin Kottke has a great post about the value of 1,000 true fans for artists: http://www.kk.org/thetechnium/archives/2008/03/1000_true_fans.php You don't need millions of disengaged "followers". 1000 true fans can make amazing things happen.  How do you find those fans? Create lots of value, and people will step forward.  I'm  ...   not sure if targeting specific people and hoping they'll become your true fans works quite the same way, but maybe you can work on wowing people just because.

Title: There Is NO Social Customer
Body: That is the title of a superb blog post put together just recently by Thierry de Baillon (@tdebaillon) and which folks can find over here: http://kcy.me/1xk3 It comes to question whether customers are social or not. For a minute, imagine they are not, like he states, what happens next? Do we still want to become a social business if our clients are no social customers? How do we delight them then? Even better, how do we "listen" to them, if they themselves are no social customers? Thoughts?

Title: Social Networks Drive Opinions
Body: I do not agree with M de BAILLON I'm afraid. His contention misses the point and evidence that social networks drive opinions, which in turn affect our decisions about lots of things, such as which hotels to stay in (Trip Advisor) and which products to buy etc etc. So let's assume that social networks do work, the question becomes how do we get the best out of them for business goals such as improving performance, raising morale, driving objectives and sharing ideas.

Title: Different tools, same behaviors
Body: Thanks Luis for linking to my post :)  Simon, I'd like to expand a bit on this. What I suggested is that if social networks are expanding our customers' possibilities to express their opinion, they don't fundamentally transform the relationships customers have with businesses. As a proof, the multiple studies which say that they DON'T want to be your friend.  The main new behavior triggered by SN is the easiness with which empathy can be created (which, in turn, can easily transform into hate). But even that  ...   is not new, it is a side-effect of crowd creation, which has been so many times exploited by political leaders from all sides.  My point was to say that to address customers' needs and expectations, we have to go beyond "social media", and pro-actively provide them with tools which can expand the conversation between them and us. Interruption is no more an option, soc nets are theirs, not ours, we have to build on CRMs, engagement platforms, open innovation tools and strategies...

Title: Customers have always been social
Body: Hi Simon & Thierry! Great conversation shared so far! I think you both bring in some good valid points as to where we are: obsessed with engaging with customers since they are now more social than ever. I have been thinking some more about this one myself, since I am putting together a blog post as well on the topic, based on Thierry's and must say that, to me, the situation is slightly different: customers have always been social; they have been connecting with themselves for decades! Back then using Forums and newsgroups, IM, email, etc. etc. And they have been talking amongst themselves. The difference now is that vendors are no longer treating them as if they didn't know better. Vendors have smartened up (And not the other way around!) and realised that if they would want to influence customers' opinions and inclinations to purchase a product they need to be immersing themselves in the conversations they are already having.  Thierry  ...   makes a good point about providing them with the tools to spark those conversations, since they trigger engagement, but let's not confuse ourselves, those interactions amongst customers have always been there, it's just the vendors are catching up and finally start to give in the notion they know better than their customers; it's actually quite the opposite, with the rare exceptions obviously, which is why I am questioning whether customers have always been social and it is actually vendors who have been humanised and socialised altogether with the emergence of social tools. Somehow I sense it's the vendors who progressed tremendously in this area. Customers were already there, waiting for them to show up!

Title: Observable conversations
Body: Great point, Luis: yes, customers always have discussed, the new (and really important) fact is that now anybody can see what they are saying. They didn't change, their level of exposure has changed.  But is this a reason for vendors to join the conversation? I don't think so, and that's why I am quite unhappy with the "social customer" expression, which is a kind of appropriation of the conversation through the tools and platforms at their disposal.  Observable conversations... Isn't observable work (exposure throughout the company, of course, but also exposure to customers) a truly valuable answer  ...   ?

Title: Perhaps we still want to command and control
Body: Hi Thierry! Of course, it is! Observable work in this context fits rightly in! The whole concept behind it puts everyone at the same level, no hierarchies, not structures, no mission to control, just basically participatory all around, which I guess is what we are missing from "social customer"; indeed, you hinted something with the above comments that I think is right spot on! With such concepts and terms like "social customer" what vendors are saying basically is that they still want to control the conversation and feel they are on top of it, i.e. customers, highlighting they still know better by taking over.  That's a very dangerous road to follow, I am afraid, and I agree with you that it would indicate how certain things may not have changed much after all... Hummm, think I would rather prefer to stick around with the mentality of observable work, even with customers...

Title: Tools for social networks
Body: Thierry, I absolutely agree with the point that leveraging technology to deliver business value on the social phenomenon is the real challenge. I think that technology visionaries understand about linking technology to human behaviour. As a technologist I am excited about what can be done to deliver business value from the technology we deploy in the social environment. It's a different perspective perhaps to commentators and philosophers who observe from their viewpoint. Both are valid of course, and have much to offer each other :-)

Title: The Social Customer
Body: As said in my comments on your blog, Social denotes that there is a change not so much in capabilities, but rather in expectations vis-Ó-vis what they expect from the company, namely that the company interacts with them more like they do with their peers. And they are also getting wise to the fact that they can now more easily organise to counterbalance the power of the company to impose its services on them - through the power of choice.  The juxtapsotion of "social" to the word "customer" serves as a signal that expectations have changed in terms of customer experience. "Social" Business uses the same construct so as to push decisionmakers to adapt.  Language  ...   is a vehicle for ideas. Until "Social" has run its course, it serves a purpose, and we can go back to calling people use our services "customers" again, the same as "social business" will go back to being "business as usual".  If you really want to insurrect against a term, it should be customers. This term does not take into account the fluctuating rainbow of needs and expectations of people that continuously adapt based on interaction with others and their environment.

Title: If not customers, what should they be?
Body: Hi Mark! Very intriguing commentary! Lots of good points, too! Wondering though if we are to change the naming of "customers", would should it be like? Does "clients" suffer from the same issues you highlight about "customers"? Would be interested in knowing some more on what concept would work to represent customers better. Any ideas you may be working on already?  I must admit that I still think when talking about "social customers" the original connotation that Thierry mentioned in his blog would still apply :-((

Title: interesting
Body: I agree with the premise here, but would add a twist. It cannot be assumed that the average client is social. What I believe is that the average client that has been kept aware of your brand and offerings can often be curious. If awareness is not an issue, it can often simply come down to search and response.  What is the most common click on your homepage? I'll guess it is search. How are most people finding your brand on the web? Search. What this boils down to in my opinion is what are they finding? Is it an organization that is well poised for that moment? Can they find the information they seek easily, and then can they take the necessary steps to follow through if desired?  ...   With a socially active business, you are afforded the opportunity to move from the old web model where potential or existing clients may find a page that may answer their questions and provide an email address or phone number to call, to a much richer experience where they can find like minded clients, experts, deeper digital connections...  Providing a much broader base of information, connections and people to clients - you may call them social or not - is at the heart of the matter. When I am researching a purchase I read what others have experienced, from average consumers to experts. If I can find a connection to a trusted professional, even better.  No, they may not be social clients, but they are the same kinds of clients we have had all along - ones considering a purchase who seek information with ease - and they should be afforded a richer experience when connecting with the brand.

Title: Tie actionable behavior with social business lingo
Body: Gary Barnett's' post, "We're using the wrong language folks", offers an interesting perspective into this issue. He contends that the the social media lingo impacts the perspective of social business. Going forward, I intend to reference actionable, relevant behaviors along with the social media lingo. I think this is a missing ingredient in our social networking strategy and will help engage our internal customers in a way that is meaningful to them. See his post for more details on the important perspective.

Title: moving target
Body: Sue, I'll add that the lingo is also a moving target. In the last 10 years I have worked on a New Media team, a Social Computing team, and a Social Media team. The last incarnation are now referred to as the Social Business team. Getting bogged down with lingo and losing focus on core goals for your team, or for your client can lead to confusion.

Title: Letting Go of Control
Body: One quandary of this new shifting nature of relationships between businesses and customers and other constituencies is centered on control.  Businesses typically see social media, networking and the rest as just a more subtle, indirect way of control -- winning new business, enhancing a brand, improving customer preference, etc. They still want that bit of control reflected in traditional goals of marketing and communications.  And to Thierry's good point, people are not always starving to have a social relationship with every brand or business. Though I think they are much more willing, and likely, to be positively influenced by an interaction with an actual person, employee or representative of a business through social channels.  ...   People are the killer app of the social business.  Until we can get past this limited view of the nature and dynamics of social, and see through to what authentic experiences between businesses and customers can and should be, we will continue to be stuck in the control trap.

Title: authenticity and trust
Body: Jack, you've hit on a point that I think has been missing in this thread. Everyone/anyone can be social today - whatever context you want to put that in but what people (customers) are always looking for is that authentic connection. And it is those connections that lead to trust that lead to strong relationships, business or personal. After all, we are all customers somewhere.

Title: terminology
Body: This is a great thread...I'm excited to see our terminology shifting from hierarchical constructs that emphasize the power differential to words that refer to the interactions. Admitting that the relationships cannot be easily categorized uni-directionally is the first step. For example, our "customers" are very often also partners, suppliers, influencers, collaborators, co-inventors, etc. Also the acknowledgement that INDVIDUALS are the ones communicating and transacting business...not just the corporate entity...and this will be more fluid/flexible in future.

Title: Social business tools in IT Helpdesk.
Body: How can we use Social business tools in IT Helpdesk environment to increase customer saticaction?  Please provide your inputs.

Title: Look for and actively engage in user communities
Body: And even build a user community for your users.  Lufthansa and other airlines have senior customer service reps actively participating in flyertalk.com, which is a message board for frequent flyers.  Lotus in India for example has the LUG Lounge - http://www.luglounge.in This  ...   is a set of Lotus User Group communities. Regular face to face interactions and webinars combined with support content available on the site and linked off it.

Title: Thank you
Body: Thank you for providing your input. Could you please also let me know how a user community can be created for Customers-reps interaction.

Title: Identify needs to start with
Body: Make documentation / FAQs online, searchable and commentable  Try to connect users who have similar problems so they can share experiences [a forum might help]  Put the social media site in your signature etc - and move certain non critical support tasks over to the site.  Automate  ...   your ticketing system and integrate it with the site (at least so that bug fix changelogs etc get automatically published when a software goes GA)  Lots of possibilities.

Title: Can we collaborate?
Body: Can we work on this offline, even after the Jam closes. I would need your guidance on this.

Title: Starting Point Should Be Customer Value
Body: Customers don't buy 1/4 drills, they buy 1/4" holes." Ted Levitt's timeless advice lives on - originally offered in 1958 in an HBR article called "Marketing Myopia." The various aspects of "Social Business" that are being discussed in the many corridors of this Jam, are essentially 1/4" drills. If we focus excessively on them, then we will be guilty of repeating traditional errors associated with being inward looking and excessively product or technology focussed, as opposed to customer need/benefit/solution/experience focused.  All things prefaced with "social" that are being discussed, no matter how they are defined, are essentially a means to an end. It is the end that must form the starting point, not what's in the bag, or what the Joneses next door are doing.  We must begin outside the company - with customer value - because without a customer there is no business and the only way to create and sustain a meaningful and lasting handshake with the customer is to offer relevant and meaningful value. And then work our way to those competencies and platforms that will help a company more effectively create, deliver, nurture, sustain, renovate, and innovate customer value. And if some of those competencies and platforms happen to be "social" wonderful!  In the end, lets not forget the value of a business is determined not by the number of times its CEO tweets, or the number of friends/fans it has on FB. It is determined by the sum total of the current and future value of all its current and potential customers. So, let's start where it matters most -with customer value - and then respond with what it takes to win the game.

Title: Tapping into customer conversations
Body: Gaurav ... You make a very important point. I would be keen to hear your thoughts on taking some of the "social" tools to start conversations with the customers and plugging inputs from those interactions back into the business. What are your thoughts about getting our customers together in forums like this and just listening to them? Simply put I guess my question is, that in a Global Economy, where your customer may be half way around the world, what are the means we can use today to get them talking to us?

Title: Customer Conversations
Body: Companies need to be extremely proactive in generating and sustaining conversations with their clients. Forums like this are formal initiatives and are extremely effective. However, Jams, or forums like Jams take time, money, and resources - and most importantly are not continuous. They need to be augmented with ongoing, informal initiatives (informal does not mean casual or ad-hoc; it just means stripped of rituals; one of my professors used to say it very well - formalized informality is 10 times better than informal formality; meaning you have formal processes but nobody follows them). That is one reason why companies invest in always on digital forums that allow them to have frequent and spontaneous fireside chats and water cooler conversations with their customers. And let's not forget the proverbial - open door. Even digital doors can be open or shut!

Title: Any specfic company success stories
Body: Does anyone have any specific company success stories that they can share regarding this type of interaction? I'm in the banking industry.  Thanks

Title: Social media - top strategic issue for companies
Body: Due to the availability of all kind of data about products, social media finally forces companies to become customer-centric. Word of mouth distributed customer experience is more powerful than any kind of marketing slogan.  Due to this social media driven market pressure, customer management has to be reinvented in many companies by being defined as a philosophy. The customer relation takes the stage as the central strategic issue thereby displacing the product focus.  Key success factor for becoming a "customer-centric" company is the integration of the horizontal business processes marketing sales and service. This means a thorough social media strategy from the beginning should focus on the integration of a variety of communication channels.  Do you agree with that?

Title: It's all about the Experience!
Body: Hi Dr. Ledig - Yes, I do agree! Why? I think a lot of organizations get bound up by organizational conflicts and process conflicts. The customer doesn't care about these things...the customer wants a great experience. This is true whether buying a consumer product or a set of business services.  I recently had an experience with a company where I walked into their store, and had a BAD experience and almost thought about canceling all the services I receive across this organization's channels b/c I was so frustrated. However, I then went outside and my husband said, let's call the service number. We had a GREAT experience and got what we wanted and the company retained a long-standing customer who spends quite a bit of money each month.  What  ...   was the distinction? The store didn't really care who we were or how long we'd been customers or how much money we spent each month on multiple services. The person helping us didn't understand the product/service we wanted to buy and she was ill equipped on the customer service front. The woman on the service line could see all the information about us. In addition, the woman was personable and resourceful and demonstrated her skills and experience on multiple fronts.  Social media did play in. I tweeted both my bad and good experience and named the company. I didn't hear back from anyone about what happened....I would have been happy to elaborate if asked.  I also don't know if any other current or potential customers read my tweet and made different choices because of my experience.  The point I think you make is, do companies integrate multiple perspectives to listen, understand, learn, and adapt in order to continually enhance their products, services, reputation and sales? If they do so, will they be more successful in the market?

Title: Social media means transformation!
Body: Thank you for for interesting reply, Mrs. Okimoto! I would like to go even step further than just what you pointed out. What you say should be already adapted by companies in the form of total quality management and/or continious improvement processes. TQM to day is still rather product-focussed. Social media will lead companies to finally think and become customer-centric causing an appropriate transformation process.

Title: Thinking about quality programs...
Body: Dr. Ledig - I started my career as a process consultant and grew up in the re-engineering craze. I am not an expert in TQM or Lean, but I know a bit. I remember using SIPCO (Supplier - Input - Process - Customer - Output). What I remember is despite the Customer in the equation, the focus was on eliminating non-value steps, waste, redundancy, time. We'd map hand-offs, but we didn't really map "collaboration" points. And, in those days other than face to face, by telephone, or email, there weren't technologies to enable the collaboration.  Okay, now fast forward. I've talked to clients who are big into quality initiatives...and what I've heard from many of them is: "this social technology and collaboration is great, but we've squeezed all discretionary time out of our business lives." They've become so "efficient" they don't have time to explore to reflect to think.  ...   I think process efficiency is incredibly important but I think it needs to be balanced. The other side is that innovation gets lost in the shuffle. Engagement decreases because people are pushed so hard they burn out and shut down. Mistakes get made because the reflection time and opportunity to do something more effectively.  My two cents...Ms. Okimoto : )

Title: Figure out where the people you want to reach are
Body: I would like to learn about how others are reaching their customers. Specifically, what are the Social Business best practices for a B2B business development executive to reach C level and VP level executives in the industrial sector. Linkedin has been helpful to me to connect with new prospects. I have not gotten much value from Linkedin Groups I have joined and participated in. Any other suggestions?

Title: Who are they when you talk to them?
Body: If you're just reaching out to them as C or VP level execs, you don't have a lot of options other than LinkedIn. But what else do you know about them? You mention sector - do people in the sector read particular blogs? Go there. Do you know they are geographically clustered? Reach out to them based on where they're located.  One of the biggest advancements brought on by social is the ability to talk to people where they already are, but you can't forget that they're people first. DJ Waldlow at Blue Sky Factory has a great blog on a B2B brand that took advantage of the Super Bowl to talk B2B: http://blog.blueskyfactory.com/b2b/9-reasons-why-marketos-3rd-quarter-super-bowl-email-worked/

Title: Find a way to make the connection, then talk
Body: [Hi Brian! Small world: we met through distant network connections with some assistance from LinkedIn.]  I have found the best option is to talk to people and tell them what I am looking for. As I get to know them, they lead me down new paths that I may not have considered previously. How do I find these poeple to talk to? LinkedIn has been an interesting starting point for those conversations. So has Twitter. But the conversations need to be in person or on the phone, or maybe mediated by the platform in question.  There are discussions out there - beyond the Jam - on how to use various platforms to connect with people. One aspect is that there are so many suggestions,  ...   that it's almost like there aren't any suggestions. Maybe someone should write these up with a focus like this question.

Title: People are drawn to what matters to them
Body: Great question -- I think the problem occurs when marketers orient their social media play around their agenda rather than around what their customer's agenda is. This only pushes people further away from you (as in sense it's an anti-'social' approach).  I think LinkedIn Groups can be made to work well too, if the orientation is changed from us-out to them-in.  Some months back, I set up a LinkedIn Group on The Future of Digital for Brands -- and the response both in terms of member numbers (over 1000 from 30+ countries in under 90 days), 30% of which are C-level folks, with active and high quality participation has been heartwarming. I think what I did right  ...   was not push my agenda but shape it around what matters to the members. It's early days, and this is by no means the definitive guide to attracting customers, but I thought it's worth sharing nonetheless. You can join the Group at http://linkd.in/bcb5CY in case you'd like to see it in action!

Title: Crowd-sourced contacts
Body: Where do you think we are heading with Crowd-sourced contacts? Any one you know have expertise in CleneStep technology?

Title: Get Social internally before going external?
Body: There are some great case studies of the success of social business reaching out to customers.  That said, what are the Jammer's experience/opinion/methods of fostering a culture of proactively Getting Social internally, and do you need that internal culture embedded into the company DNA to make a success of external initiatives?

Title: Social inside or outside firts
Body: David, I think adopting a Social business for external customer engagement is the better first step. Bypassing the political issues of internal employee engagement may provide some clear benefits for the company, before moving to internal employee engagements. Internal engagements have great benefits, but also risks of political landmines around tools, ownership, scope, etc that lead to endless debate. External engagement can be very focused, have great business-impact around customer service, and show an organization how to become a Social Business

Title: Company's Social DNA
Body: I agree that it's important for a company to have social culture built into the DNA. I think there are many ways to accomplish this. Building an internal social network is certainly one way to begin on this journey. Another way is to promote, encourage and reward social behaviors from the management team. Executive role models can go a long way in shaping the company DNA in support of external social initiatives.

Title: Promote, Encourage & Reward
Body: Very much agree with Debbie. There needs to be endorsement of external social engagement by management and appropriate role modelling.

Title: Definitely.
Body: The driving force behind all of this is an active leadership culture. It's not that much of a leap...the good leaders likely already practice MBWA (management by walking around), this is a digital extension of that practice.

Title: Depends on the social medium
Body: I would like to believe that if an organization is looking at establishing a customer facing social experience that they would also be contemplating an internal social culture as well; and hopefully it would lead.  It also depends though on what type of social technology we are referring to. If we are talking about portal implementations than there is definitely value of establishing a consistent technology across both external and internal sites.  But if we are talking about messaging or links to external networks such as Facebook, Twitter, etc where you are trying to establish a brand, I think those  ...   technologies will not be utilized internally as there is still a guarded view towards providing employees access to these types of resources while on 'company time' or the potential loss of control over what IP is disseminated outside the company walls.

Title: It's not all or nothing
Body: Creating a more collaborative and sharing culture internally through microblogging, employee community platforms, blogs, wikis and the like can be a great way of getting people used to communicating differently and more transparently.  It's kind of like fitting training wheels on a bike. Once you have found the balance - the right tone, what, when and with whom to share, you can take them off and wander out into the wild blue yonder to make new encounters and widen your horizon.  That said, not everyone values being in an agora, and not all roles can divulge all they know 'out there'. Rules of engagement inside the enterprise can  ...   be completely different from how we communicate with those outside the company firewall. Think of highly regulated industries such as pharmaceuticals or information such as financial statements. In such cases it would make sense to instate a social media policy to provide guidelines as to what can and can't be done.

Title: Get Social Internal
Body: One may or may not need to imbibe it internally to succeed externally. so it is not a pre-requisite. but having said that, if your nature of business allows you to practice it internally, your success rate of doing it right externally may go up multi-fold. Such an internal learning will be so valuable to do it right externally.

Title: Adopt parallel streams
Body: I am not sure if going internal or external first really makes a difference. The more important question is do you have the expertise to sustain the initiative? If the staff supporting external social business have experience with the tools (e.g. staying in contact with friends and family) then that expertise can be used for interacting with customers. Other organizations might need to develop the expertise internally first.

Title: The often overlooked Corporate Intranet
Body: David, I do believe there is tremendous power in using one's existing Intranet platform as a test-bed, proving ground for creating a culture of collaboration and innovation within an enterprise.  In my opinion creating a culture of innovation, requires first creating a culture of collaboration which is ultimately based on trust. As organisations (big and small) increasingly share and work around the virtual water-cooler versus the physical location the Corporate Intranet is increasingly required to connect the workforce. A clear business case/ROI with hard and soft measures can be established around: (1) employee effectiveness and reduced operational costs; (2) alignment around company strategy and intent; and (3) tapping into the passion of the employees for where they work for new business and idea generation. One's existing Intranet is the perfect springboard to the collaboration for how the company can prepare itself to work externally,  ...   align different parts of the business and encourage cross-functional work.  Case in point, our first IBM Jam was October 2001 and in-part it was run as an experiment to prove the power of our Intranet to employees and to management. This experiment ultimately led to and fostered a new of working both within IBM and now outside opening new businesses and areas of opportunities to IBM.

Title: I agree
Body: David: I will add one thing to what Liam is saying that I believe to be quite important as well. In a large organization, there are many who have not had a great deal of social communications or networking experience, let alone experience in building personal brand or being productive members of an online community. Finding the appropriate voice, the right handshakes, a respectful level of attention to others, understanding how to share unique and valuable information that will resonate...  Yes, many of us are skilled at this, sometimes even with strangers in a meeting or etc, but having an internal home for collaboration can be a place for crafting and honing best practices in online social communications.

Title: The Intranet is a good starting point, but...
Body: While I agree that the intranet is a good training ground to get employees familiar with social media policies and to get 'social' themselves, I think what really can make a difference is loosening the Intranet's usually water tight structures and norms to closer mirror th more fluid and less regulated external social media realities.  After all, no amount of 'locker room' pep talk and training can replicate the realities of playing on the field with real opponents and a real audience, can it now?!

Title: Intranet vs External...
Body: Ashok, the value that a company gets from massive and effective internal collaboration is a strong support point for encouragement of activity within the intranet. Likewise, we all appreciate the value of external.

Title: Internal-external need to be hand-in-hand
Body: Yes, very much George, the value of strong internal orientation on Social is crucial for success in the external space. I feel it's important though to bring the 'socialness' of the external Social environment to employees in their internal Social environment too, so a seamless Social behaviour can be shaped.

Title: The business goals matter
Body: External "social" initiatives can succeed. In fact they can be wildly successful, regardless of internal culture. Social Business however, requires alignment of both internal and external as a prerequisite for success.

Title: Examining Internal and External Processes
Body: I think it's important to realize the internal and external goal for initiating social media.  By looking at your internal social process, insight can be drawn and passed to the external channels. A great example of this is Vail Resorts. By asking employees to share their experiences, they not only extended their internal practice but were able to succeed by dramatically increasing their social following.

Title: Understanding goals and objectives are key
Body: I agree with Peter's comment here. The most important aspect is to ensure that you social strategy is aligned closely to your organizations culture. It's not so much about fostering a "culture of collaboration" and "culture of innovation", but rather that the culture of the organization is aligned with, and supports, the business goals.  As Peter notes, this alignment is important when it comes to the social activities that you are planning both internally and externally to the organization. In my experience, it's not necessary that these be serial activities. To be certain, many feel that starting internally allows for there to be a "safe zone" for people to learn, but much will depend upon how much of a change social represents for your enterprise. If you buy into the idea that social business patterns will enable a new level of interactivity with your clients, you need to be practicing that process externally where your clients  ...   are. If your company's culture and processes (DNA) are not aligned to support that, it's unlikely that you can afford to wait to get that in place before engaging. You are going to need programs in place to tackle this transformation holistically and ensure the alignment meets your desired business objectives.

Title: Going Social is strategic and transformational
Body: Using social communication tools it's a strategical and transformational decision for a Company or business.  For instance, it means shifting from an e-mail centric communication towards a more open internal BLOG, Wiki communication or discussion thread.  E-mail is not only communication: it's somehow a political tool with the CC, and BC games. Of course, there can be open or closed discussions.  I  ...   think that Senior Management has to lead the transformation. I like to read on a regular basis the Blog from the more senior managers. In case these posts offer the possibilities to comment even better.

Title: It's about engaging people where they are
Body: For the most part, our customers are not on Facebook and Twitter, but partners who do what we do are. I try to meet people where they are, but also to cross connect. I often take podcasts and videos other partners are creating and paste them into an email newsletter, so the "dragging tail" people are in the loop with what the "bleeding edge" people are creating. It doesn't matter much if they are inside or outside the organization.

Title: Inside and Outside IBM (Business Partners)
Body: I agree with view it is about information where ever you are at. I am trying to jump directly to internal-external with IBM business partners and am looking for anyone with experience in this area.

Title: Inside and Outside IBM (Suppliers)
Body: I agree with the sentiment that it's time to reach out - even in a small way - and engage the outside via social business. I am personally considering how we might "dip our toe into the water", and start doing supplier collaboration via social tools, until we develop a strategy and more consistent approach.

Title: Advantages of Internal....
Body: Agree with much of what's been shared, and particularly like Liam's points. There are two advantages of going internal as a start to external. The first, creating a social culture or DNA. Secondly, and probably most importantly, there are massive business advantages to be gained through the creation of a collaborative organization. These range from business efficiency through to creativity/innovation.  Having said that, it seems that once an internal culture is formed, it helps to have programs designed to encourage external social activation.

Title: Parallel activities
Body: George,  In your experience do companies need to tackle these in such a serial fashion? I certainly agree that a "social culture" has business advantage, but I have often heard it said that "you can't force culture".  Assuming that transforming culture take time, how do you tackle these challenges and ensure an organization is effectively engaging the marketplace, without waiting for the effects of the transformation to be in place?

Title: brand and effectiveness
Body: As I stated in my reply to Liam, I don't see internal as a safe zone, nor a culture bunker first and foremost. I see it as a way for employees to learn how to build a personal brand and communicate effectively. So many learn they can't find the time to keep their blog going, or that nobody is reading or commenting. They struggle to find a voice that can add value to their work or strategic mission. They evolve as a result, and they learn to be better communicators and community members. I feel that internal social platforms can be of benefit to the greater internal employee base than it sometimes is for early adopters and the social business savvy.

Title: Agree Parallel...
Body: Agree, not needed to be sequential. Internal has collaborative business advantage, as well as opportunity for people to have an easy start. External has value we all understand.  How to start and effectively engage externally, lots of posts around this, but some that have resonated to me:  Permission, recognition, encouragement  Leading  ...   by example  Intentionality and systems (without taking away the very nature of social)

Title: There is probably no black and white answer
Body: Outside initiatives can be quite successful in a traditional [non-social] organizational culture. However then these initiatives are clearly part of a customer related effort and will probably be embedded in a marketing or customer services channel. Think in this case as social initiatives adding to call center initiatives. These can be very social and very successful connecting to existing call center systems and processes. The remainder of the organization does not have to be social.  On the other hand I am convinced that organizations will only get full benefit of outside social when they are inside also going the social route. So create that culture of open leadership, social awareness, authenticity and sharing. Then the organization will live and breath social and outside efforts will come over much more natural. Let alone that the organization will enjoy the humongous benefits of the internal social culture.

Title: Practice on the go...Get social by doing social
Body: I do not think one needs to train 'inside' and practice 'outside' -- but needs to be aware of principles (processes / tools in the case of social media spaces)-- and then learn on the go. It's like etiquette classes, you can learn theory in a class room, but what counts is the practice of doing it in real settings.  The best way to do social is by being social. Learn the rules, and then forget them and go walk the talk. Just as it is with car driving -- if you're too conscious of the clutch/brake process (in a stick shift), your car with mov jerkily and start-stop-sputter. Hardly something you'd want your brand or your employees to d via social media, right?!

Title: Understand before promoting
Body: Completely agree that enterprises would faster adopt social media techniques - may even permit Instant Messaging which more that 40% of enterprises to do not permit- if they had the experience of feeling the benefits within their own organizations. After all, isn't the Holy Grail of the enterprise 'to know what it knows'? Now this can be expanded to 'know who it knows.' As well, internal exploration will appease the legal staff and reduce the level of frustration by the inane comments of spammers and flamers (think You Tube).

Title: Two fun and interesting thinkers on topic
Body: Jeff Jonas, IBM Chief Scientist: http://jeffjonas.typepad.com/jeff_jonas/2010/03/on-a-smarter-planet-some-organizations-will-be-smarterer-than-others.html Andrew McAfee, MIT: http://andrewmcafee.org/blog/

Title: Strengthening Customer Relationships rising?
Body: Customer relationships have always been critical to the health of a business. A companies web presence is becoming more and more a primary face to the customer with social interactions becoming an ante to have a good customer web experience. I am interested in the communities view on what inhibitors are real or perceived to leveraging social aspects in their external web experiences ?

Title: Everyone's shouting and no one is listening ...
Body: ... Is what is often said about Twitter. This is not strictly true IMO but to respond to this thread there is every reason to do both in parallel. The customer we are seeing implement successfully start their external social strategy by first listening. They monitor what customers are saying about them, their brands and their products.

Title: Companies need to listen both inside and out
Body: I think it is true that to use social media well, organisations need listen and use social media both inside and outside their company walls.  Much of the information they need to succeed is generated outside the organisation. Customer complaints are too often put on twitter before a call to an automated help desk.  To really succeed staff also need to use the tools well when engaging their customers. Hence employers need to get staff skilled up and in IMO the best way is inside the safe confines of the company. Social media can add value inside and outside the organisation. Evaluate you employee skills before  ...   you start, make sure it is done well and you will see the returns.

Title: Two Ears, One Mouth.. Listen 2x talking...
Body: Scott,  Agree with your post.  Elsewhere, but don't know how to links, I posted this which talks to listening....  The  ...   Age Of Many Voices...  Consider the facts: Your staff, your clients, your competitors, your investors, your advocates and your detractors are all socially engaged. They all have voices.  Some have created a ŌĆ£personal brand.ŌĆØ Others are speaking with association to a companyŌĆÖs brand. This rise of the unofficial spokesperson has significant potential repercussions.  These voices, in the physical world, are mostly weak. In the social world, they can be powerful and influential. In the social world, there is noise and signal. Noise is retweeted, thoughtless, unconnected 140-character sets. ItŌĆÖs flaky Facebook fans. Signal cuts through. Is on message. Is connected. Signal drives to action.  Effective social marketers have intentionally high signal-to-noise ratios. Effective social marketers listen extremely well. They understand the value of dialogue. They welcome conversation.  This rise of the employee voice, of many voices, is changing marketing.  With potentially half of your employees online and socially active, there is a need for empowerment guidelines. Most will not have had the media training of traditional company spokespeople. With a desire for your subject matter experts to be engaged, there is a need for either incentives or new job profiles to encourage the behavior sought. With the massive volume of messages to listen to and interpret, there is a need for focus, for direction and connection. This is the new role of social business management.  Customer service is also changing. ItŌĆÖs faster and itŌĆÖs public. Forget Twelpforce, though this in itself is a powerful outworking of the positive. Think disgruntled customers who are not getting the attention they need or deserve. Think of the opportunity for your competitor or detractor to amplify this dissatisfaction. Or, now thinking Twelpforce, consider the competitor who listens to your customers and embraces them.  Social is the opportunity to listen. As children, we all heard that we were created with two ears and one mouth, and that meant we should focus on listening first and doing that twice as much. This truism holds for the Age of Many Voices.  Besides being polite, it will help ensure you are on message. Listening is a research opportunity. Done properly, it is a lead-capturing and -farming opportunity. And, critically, all of this is in real time.  Effective social marketers create intentional systems.  We started with ŌĆ£consider the facts.ŌĆØ Perhaps consider this: Do you know how many of your staff are socially engaged? Do you know how many of your target are online? Do you know what your competitors are saying, what your advocates and detractors are saying? Is your company socially engaged?  In the Age of Many Voices are you ready? Social business is here.

Title: Get Social internally before going external?
Body: Social Media Adoption can not be viewed as "added work". Just like we all check our email daily to get our jobs done - we must train ourselves to use Social Media Tools as a part of how we get our jobs done. Whether its internal tools like Lotus Connections or external tools like LinkedIn & Twitter - these environments allow us to gather information! I think we all recognize our ability to have information at our fingertips allows us to out perform competitors, allows us to develop solutions like Watson - our options are endless! I also believe that adoption occurs one employee at a time. Each employees use of Social Media and its benefits will become viral and before you know it - its embedded into our culture - into the way we do business on a daily basis - just like email is today!

Title: Social Media with Business Partners
Body: Even though my org is not using social media internally I am trying to leap directly to external with IBM business partners we work with on a daily basis. Most have smart phones and in conversations with them they use Facebook and Twitter already. I am looking for ideas in the jam on how to get started perhaps with a facebook account for my org that we can use with our key business partners.

Title: Get Social internally before going external?
Body: I think becoming comfortable with Social Media internally is the key to succeeding externally because it helps to overcome the gap between the use of Social Media by private individuals and by companies.  If I were to ask a bunch of simple questions "what's your favourite colour, Whats your favourite band? etc" You could probably answer comfortably as both an individual and as your company. If I were to now ask about your political thoughts or opinions on world events, as an individual you'd be fine but as a company it becomes riskier and more uncomfortable to answer without running it past legal teams - essentially turning it into an offical PR statement and missing the Social Media point.  This  ...   is a crucial difference. Business is slow to react on Social Media channels and nervous of giving opinion. This creates a rift when trying to interact with external individuals in the Social Media world  By embedding Social Media internally and ensuring that your values are simple, achievable and something that employees are passionate about, you begin to remove the difficulty and risk of all parts of your organisation taking part in Social Media and ultimately improve your external impact.

Title: Practice Makes Perfect
Body: David, I think there is a lot of value in getting a company or institution's culture comfortable with being transparent. There are lots of ways to do that from internal Jams to community spaces. I think there is behavior of respect of ideas that can come from this. I don't think it is a "must have" but I think companies who use social internally are much more successful externally. People need to get over the fear of "publishing" and being behind the scenes.

Title: The Objective is the Thing
Body: Whether starting internally with and among employees or externally with or among customers, the objective of the social strategy must be clear. There are a lot of people and organizations that have jumped on the social bandwagon and have had bad experiences because there were no driving objectives for doing it. Liam earlier pointed out the one of the key objectives of internal social business/networking is about becoming a collaborative organization. But external social business has to be about developing or perpetuating a compelling story about your organization or brand that customers will want to get engaged with. My sense is that if the former occurs first (internal collaboration around business objectives) then the second becomes easier to implement and sustain.

Title: You have to focus on why you are being social
Body: I agree with Ken about the need to focus on the objective - while it's great that businesses are starting to recognise the benefit that social media/software/strategy can bring, you need to look at what you are trying to achieve. Without clear goals and targets, and a sense of what you are trying to improve on, social will continue to just be the latest fad and will inevitably fail to meet expectations about what it can help you to achieve. Whether you are focused on improving collaboration internally - for example to improve knowledge sharing, innovation or distributed teams - or improving customer or partner relationships externally, you need to look at how those improvements will manifest themselves and how you can *prove* their value.

Title: Get comfortable internally, but don't stop there
Body: I fully agree with the sentiments expressed throughout this thread so far, as well as David's initial comments; having used social tools, both inside and outside of the firewall for a good number of years I have always felt that making the transition from traditional tools like email or IM into blogs and wikis and other social networking tools is not an easy one; it requires having a different mindset, a different look into audiences, a different model of engagement and participation and, as such, I have always thought that going internal first, although making external available as well, is the way to go.  Peter's & Rooney's comments are spot on; it's a blend of both approaches, although I know that eventually we would all be out there where our customers are; but we need to prepare our way; we first need to be comfortable with interacting with these social tools; that's why going internal is helpful.  ...   It's a trusted environment, reliable, scalable in terms of content and networks, protected, so folks who are new to these social tools would get a chance to dive into the social pool and play with the social tools, experiment, explore, make mistakes, get better and in the end get comfortable enough to decide they are ready to go!  When I first wanted to start blogging I did so blogging on the Intranet; that blog is now 7 years old; 2 years after I started, when I felt I have finally found my blogging voice and style, I decided to jump the shark and start blogging external. That blog is now over 5 years old. That decision of experimenting internally, to then go externally, was very very helpful.  Enterprise 2.0 is not a destination; it's a journey; the Social Web is where we should be heading, but we need to start somewhere ;-)

Title: Do we need to get comfortable?
Body: Given the rate of adoption of social media outside the enterprise, do we know that people need to get comfortable internally before they venture out into the wild, unknown, client-facing internet? Or could it be that some can make the leap without taking baby steps internally? Your thoughts welcome, Luis.

Title: Some would need, some won't; I did!
Body: Hiya, Boz! Great to see you over here as well jamming away! Thanks for dropping by! You are right, we should not put too many limitations; in fact, that's what I meant above already when I mentioned we should focus on internally initially to get people comfortable with new models of engagement and interactions, but still with an open door for those brave ones to go out there and have a rather vocal presence. We should not stop those, to be honest. They are preparing the road for when the rest of the community are ready to make that transition.  An example, I started blogging in 2003 and it wasn't till 2005, when I thought I have found my blogging voice and style, that I thought I was ready to go out there and create my external blog. And I did! Now, as time has moved on I regret not having started the blog sooner, but then again, not sure whether it'd've had the same impact it has now in my own personal brand.  ...   Like I said, I still believe the focus should be internal, but without neglecting the external factor; and empower those who may want to go out right away; don't stop them but facilitate their embracing of the open Social Web. There is a great chance they already know how they would want to interact and leave a digital footprint.  Also, one other aspect, remember the example from SocialBlue; thousands of IBMers still hanging out there, in that protect environment, versus other SNS like Facebook, where they don't seem to feel that safe anymore; in SB they surely do! That's what I was talking about ... Makes sense?

Title: Internal before External
Body: Ideally, I think internal and external social business channels/engagements are grown concurrently -- each medium has its own lessons and best practices that can then be applied to the other, allowing each implementation to leapfrog ahead from the successes of the other.
