And the more success produced externally, the more eager employees will be to engage externally, and more success internally will also encourage employees to engage externally. I see them intertwined, and think accomplishing one before the other will slow down the adoption and success of each.

Title: Start with existing networks, build in both places
Body: The most effective way to get a rapid start is by ensuring online networks are an accurate reflecting of already existing social networks across all communication channels including face to face. For most companies, the preponderance of social network connections will be internal. This isn't a bad thing - it creates a nice place to start. For employees who deal mostly with markets, clients, and partners, social networks may be weighted more toward external connections. To ensure these two networks are effectively integrated into one, it helps to ensure there are healthy connections between those whose connections are mostly outside and those whose connections are mostly inside.

Title: Internal-External is tough
Body: Where I've worked, external social has always been easier because companies understand concepts like brand equity, voice of the customer, and partner/supplier relations. In these relations, employees work all the time with relatively new voices and relationships, so it's difficult to stay on the same page without a more social solution.  Ironically, it's much harder to explain why employees should talk to each other when these co-workers have never had to speak to each other before. Because that culture of directly avoiding the vast majority of colleagues exists (at least in mid-sized enterprises and up), it's like you're trying to carve a new path in everyone's brain to get them to use internal social tools. A few people get it, but most of them need an extra push such as executive support and additional benefits and recognition to get started. Unless the internal collaboration shows quick personal benefits to the employee above  ...   and beyond their old mindset, it just doesn't stick. Competing against a behavioral status quo is difficult.

Title: Is "external" easier than "internal"?
Body: Hi Hyoun, I found your experiences very interesting.  Your post made me realize again, that it needs a lot more communications work to shape the image of our internal social media.  Having been working with both rather small companies and the (a bit bigger) IBM, I could not imagine work-life without them anymore.  And  ...   in addition to the more known benefits, this is simply because of productivity, efficiency and allowing for types of collaboration that would not have been possible before.  I wished, everyone could come and see :-)

Title: Get a Social Netwroking Policy for your Business
Body: When it comes to social networking in business creating a internal and external policy for your business is very important as the healthcare industry has learned. Recently several Hospitals and Heatlhcare providers have been sued because of what was done on social networks. Now several reports from iheathbeat and Healthcare Security, and the Government have issued social network policy guidelines for the healthcare industry to follow. I have read these reports and find that these policies could also be used in other business who are looking to create a internal/external social network policy.

Title: Is "internal" a good starting point.
Body: Hi David, this is a great - aka "tough" - question..  Maybe science already suggests some answers to that.  In ANY case, my personal experience and observations tell me, that finding the optimal personal way in social media life is not soo easy for anyone.  Why, with whom, how, when, where..  ...   Therefore I think that a "safe place" is a great starting point. It allows to learn the general principles, experiment and also find out what's most beneficial and what's just a waste of time.  And - the extra-bonus: internal social tools have a tremendous value in themselves. So, it's something you may start with but always remains an important part.

Title: Pick whomever is most likely
Body: I think the key is to find the most receptive group within an agency to social business and find that to start. Tied to that, find a champion who cares and stakeholders that have a problem they want to engage around  Sometimes that is a marketing/PR/comm shop as they are used to engaging.  Sometimes that is HR or a senior leader who really wants to foster internal collaboration.

Title: You go social ... if you see benefits !
Body: To become "social" is not just as easy as that, the way you communicate on social networks, especially around your business, is very different to the "marcom" approach we are mostly used to.  So Internal/external may not be the first question, but rather how do you facilitate or accompany people first experiences, make sure these are positive experiences, help them understand how this can connect to their job, and why not to their personal life to make it even more interesting ... All this to make sure you preperly initiate and accompny the "change" required.  Then  ...   I'm sure that internal and/or external experiences will be complementary and contribute to the overall evolution in people understanding and usage of social media.  For big enough companies, internal approach can be the right way (and safe one) to speed-up things, though many connections already exist internally through emails, making social approach seem less "useful" at first.  External approaches can be more enthusiastic as they bring direct contact with more people, and some people you normally do not connect with, like people outside your usual "playground" (or targets) but whom at the end of the day may bring new ideas and points of view in the discussions.  Also, you generally have many more potential people to connect with externally than internally. And as active people on the web are less than 1% of total people, the more potential people you have in front of you, the more chances you have to get interesting exchanges and relations, making the experience more positive and interesting.

Title: Get Social internally before going external?
Body: I think that going social internally first does make a difference, David.  As someone has already said here, this is how you create a truly collaborative organization. This is how employees learn to be community members, and better communicators too. From my perspective, going social internally also has a major additional advantage. Companies that go social internally can, in fact, boost their employees' morale and well-being. The monotony of work is broken, and each employee has the opportunity to contribute to his/her own company's success. Competitiveness and productivity of companies greatly depend on the psychological well-being of their employees.  I  ...   am an active member of IBM internal communities, and I wish to thank my employer for granting me such a crucial "benefit".

Title: Customers are driving force to go external
Body: It don't think that a pure internal approach could work for two reasons. First, as soon as employees leave their working place, they are private, but still keep their attitude and mind-set. And they will also use the huge amount of "external sources" available. Second and more important ... depending on the business model and customer basis, companies are forced to think external! Listening is the first step ...

Title: Different objectives internal vs. external
Body: Although I agree with many of the comments that say fostering an internal social environment first will help make the external better, our experience has been that the differing objectives have put us on separate parallel paths.  Social for external has been championed by our marketing organizations who want to connect better with our customers - objective is to have happy customers who buy more product.  Our internal efforts have been directed toward collaboration - knowledge sharing and expertise location.  ...   Working on these objectives separately has not been an issue. Things are now starting to come together in limited areas. One example is the use of Chatter for customer tracking - this has an external focus but fosters the internal collaboration needed to ensure the customer is appropriately served.  The future is likely to be a social business, but getting there through separate efforts has been the only practical way.

Title: Thanks for engaging!
Body: Sadly, my commitments following a busy week at Lotusphere have not allowed me to partake 'real-time' in the Jam as much as I wanted.  Here I am checking in Friday afternoon UK time...and the Jam is winding down, and there are some 50 responses to my only post! Many intriguing insights & opinions to distill.  Here's hoping we retain some access to this after shutdown to allow me/us to review & consider content across the sessions and perhaps keep the conversations/thoughts going.  ...   .....David  twitter: david_peacock

Title: Seems we can browse after the Jam closes....
Body: From the "About the Jam" Page...  "After 72 hours of jamming, a rich collection of conversations will be available for attendees to browse. IBM intends to synthesize these conversations to surface guiding thoughts that will help organizations in their transition to a Social Business. In addition, the output from the Jam will provide numerous ideas for new opportunities to achieve business value from becoming a Social Business. "

Title: Get Social internally before going external?
Body: There are indeed different objectives in fostering an internal social environment vs. an external one. Internal communities can help women re-connect with work and feel part of a helping community. For instance, when they are back on duty after maternity leave (this is my personal view from Italy). I think that client satisfaction is attained also through employee satisfaction. And an internal social environment has a lot to offer to have happier clients in return. But obviously the external approach is equally important.

Title: Attentionalty: Challenges in keeping & captivating
Body: The near-challenge is attention and linking it with value creation & economic value. Social Media content is infinite but attention is finite. Take twitter. Over 100 mil postings per day! Staggering, you think?! But retweets/replies over time/space are a mire shadow. Content decay? Perhaps - so the challenge will be in initially creating the surge followed by optimizing for the best time/space to engage with content consumers. Think of it as a version of Moore's Law - You find that answer and you've reached the holy grail in Social Media.

Title: How to Blend Social Media tools with other tools?
Body: Using "Social Media" is the hot thing right now. A key to success will be combining these NEW social media tools with "old" tools. For me as a UI Designer, Old tools including getting feedback at conferences. For marketers, "old" tools included speaking at conferences, distributing materials, etc.  In Collab services (Lotus) we've seen a resurgence in the Lotus User Groups (LUGs) - I think there is great synergy there. The Social media helps generate interest, increases attendance, and allows attendees to stay in touch throughout the year. For me, it is not JUST about using social media, it's about COMBINING social media with other tools -- like local LUGS-- to get the design feedback I need, to build community, and thus, promote customer loyalty.  HOW ELSE can we combine the social media tools with other tools?

Title: Combining Social with Loyalty
Body: Hi Mary Beth-Our company has combined social media with loyalty tools to create what we call the new face of loyalty. We created the website www.social-loyalty.com to assist with social loyalty awareness and education.

Title: Social Media & Conferencing
Body: How would you suggest social media tools be an additive to face to face meetings, without taking away the value of face to face meeting venues.

Title: Social Media Monitoring
Body: I am really curious if any of you use social media monitoring tools. What the reasons are for using them? And which tools you use?

Title: Customer's 4 S. And they matter - Strategically
Body: Customers's 4 S will always be as strategic influencer, stakeholder, socially responsible caretaker of his organization AND 'Success" thinker! If you were to see closely the behavioral pattern of any customer, they would eventually have covered all of these 4 at all times. It makes-up for him being a customer to be considered within the gamut of a 'business engagement'.  Think it over: the chances of our entire sales cycle or CVM-based proposals or even the simplest usage of a 'thought leader' to act as influencer ... will invariably be an individual who has all the 4S in his tenets of role. This 4S encompases the complete continumm of 'pull' agenda without much pursuasion or negotiation strategy. In social media, if 'connection' to 'revenue/leadgen' is the goal, then leveraging a customer by connecting him in all these 4S parameters will be vital!

Title: Making social business real
Body: Hello everyone. Thank you, IBM, for your kind invitation to join the social business jam. I've been reading your posts and comments with great interest. We at Citi are at the very start of our journey to transform both business interaction and client engagement by embracing social media. Some great insights here, and some unanswered questions .... how real are the concerns around negative groundswell? how do we manage IP generated through such a public process? Should we restrict client collaboration to a walled garden or embrace public forums? To what extent do we need to orchestrate and manage the social interaction with clients via this medium, and how many forums can we actively monitor and sustain?  Would appreciate your thoughts and insights to our dilemmas.

Title: The converstations will happen with or without you
Body: I find it interesting that many companies are still trying to decide IF they will engage client through social media. It should no longer be a question of IF but HOW. It's good to see that Citi has overcome this hurdle and is formulating it's strategy. One basic principle of social to understand about customers is that they are engaged whether or not the company has decided to get in the game. Customers are on Facebook, Twitter, YouTube and blog sites talking about your products, their recent customer service experience, your commercials and new products and services they want to see. If you are not watching and engaging them in these conversations, I guarantee your competitors are.  One of the best examples of customer engagement comes from McDonalds. Several years back, when the movie Super Size Me came out, McDonalds decided not to ignore customer response to the movie. Instead, they decided that they wanted to be part of the dialogue. They set up a blog to encourage conversations  ...   about the movie. They realized that these conversations would go on with or without them. They thought it best to be part of the group.

Title: The conversations will happen with or without you
Body: That is true, Laura. Do you think it plays out the same way with corporate clients? As employees of the entity that's contracted with a company like IBM or Citi, how comfortable are we to effectively represent the company in any public or semi-public social interaction, and how might we help constructively orchestrate that?

Title: Media Portrayal of Social Media
Body: Laura: Great points, couldn't agree with you more. To what degree do you think the mainstream media's negative portrayal of social media effects business's reluctance to embrace it? I think the conventional wisdom is that social media is either for teens and college students, or a gigantic security risk, or both. Thoughts?

Title: You will be led by your youth
Body: Krista, I agree with your characterization that the mainstream media pans social media, but I believe that is because main stream media is accurately reflecting the position of the business world - not the other way around. Mainstream businesses shun social media, as they did initially with texting, cell phone use, and the internet. And for the most part they will continue to shun social media until they learn how to properly harness the yield from this potential rich stream.  Ten plus years ago it was the youth of Japan that led the texting wave and now many business executives are texting like an an experienced Japanese teenager.  The  ...   management teams I speak with mistakenly look upon social media as a 'kid's toy'.  The 'science' of expanded, multi-tier communication and collaboration will march on, the neat trick will be the 'art' of managing to abstract the benefits.  You will be led by your youth.

Title: The conversation will happen with or without you
Body: With all of the negative press and fear businesses profess about participating in social media, I think the value of resolving an issue surfaced publicly is often overlooked. No one wants a negative review or opinion floated about his/her company, but the potential reach of the opportunity to so publicly 1) take note of the complaint and 2) resolve it, again publicly, is huge. If you resolve one customer's issues on the phone, that's great -- that customer is happy and may tell a friend or two. But if you do it in the public arena of social networking, and your reach is magnified by a magnitude or three. But it does require taking social media seriously, and comments and complaints seriously. For a more positive spin -- it's another opportunity to let your customers know that you care about their satisfaction.

Title: Internal and External Social Networking
Body: I honestly believe that you need two environments.  Your open environment:  - provide services to your customers  - listen to customer complaints and praise  ...   - get feedback for proposed new offerings  - an avenue for customers to propose new services  Internal environment:  - discuss what is possible or impossible  - changes to existing offerings  - dream up new offerings  - cost benefit discussions  - reality check on existing and new services  Monitoring and synthesis of these environments is probably the more difficult. The internal environment may take care of itself as long as the various product leads are monitoring the appropriate areas. The external systems require more staffing to allow you to respond quickly to customer issues and to jump on any new trends that you see from your customers.

Title: Social IP?
Body: Is there such a thing? Yes, in a sense there is per the Open source or even some closed-but-multicompany standards practices. However, not everything can become an industry standard; only so when many companies choose to adopt and conform to make it simpler to do business together.  But regular IP for often simpler yet patentable ideas is a bigger challenge. Some try to preset a license. The last time I looked just in the software industry, a company call Black Duck had license management software that covers over 500 varieties of common "open source" licenses. It becomes a downright headache to manage each of these. On the other hand, the legal profession is heavily based on individualized contracts for particular IP, and standardized ones for mass users.  ...   Are there alternatives that aren't as heavy-weight as licenses and contracts that we see today?

Title: Practices makes perfect...
Body: Mei Li, has Citi considered starting a social transformation inside the firewall first? This would be a good way to understand what social business means to Citi and at the same time it would be a great platform for employees to feel comfortable engaging in a social platform. Once they are comfortable, that may be a good point to start engaging with your customers externally.

Title: Inside / outside / both
Body: It may make sense to work from both extremes gauging sentiment. There are tools that can be run to analyze the overall Internet for customer/consumer/shareholder/employee sentiment just as you could run content analytics and surveys internally.  I think many firms would be surprised at what's being said, both in quantity and quality, out in the open networks. A small glimpse at that my indicate that internal adoption may not be such a hurdle as much as a wild horse that needs to be directed.

Title: Practices makes perfect ....
Body: Luis: indeed we have. A couple of years ago, our Technology group released Citi 2.0 in pilot to enable employees to set up communities via this platform. In the last year, the business division that I represent -- Global Transaction Services -- built a business enterprise-wide ecosystem within the Citi 2.0 platform (which is run on Sharepoint/Newsgator) which we named @GTS, and wrapped some governance around the proactive management of broadcast channels and workgroup communities across our business (which spans 96 countries). It's very well received, thanks to a proof of concept Jam that we staged with the help of IBM last year, but the journey has just begun. While there is a lot of appetite to take this over the firewall to embrace our clients (these are corporations, other financial institutions and public sector entities), we have many dilemmas around how we manage and perhaps contain this medium from a legal / compliance / risk perspective.  I  ...   would welcome any insight folks on the jam can offer in terms of success stories running B2B communities and forums 'over the wall' with clients. We are experimenting with Linked In Groups but for a deeper interaction with clients, we probably need something less public and more focused that we can sustain, draw in experts to fuel meaningful discussion and extract value from.

Title: Making Social Media a Business Driver
Body: Mei,  We have begun that journey at PayPal and it is soul searching for a company to embrace social media totally.  As Laura Bryce said customers are talking with or without you listening, so there is no choice but to engage.  But  ...   it is a journey for a large company to adopt social. It gets easy if you start monitoring your customer conversations (there are listening tools that make it easy to listen everywhere, its not as hard as it seems). The real change has to happen INSIDE the company. It is an attitude change to acknowledge the good and the bad feedback from customers, change process to let the customer lead change.  It starts with a social media department, but if you focus on making social media a business driver and engage the whole company to do social (meaning engage, listen to customers,change process to tie to social) then it happens organically.

Title: Making Social Media a Business Driver
Body: Sudha: I could not agree more. And that is where we are at as well, across our retail and corporate businesses. We are learning fast, and where we are looking to leap-frog with the help of our industry peers is the sharing of best practice around how we help orchestrate meaningful business interaction via this medium and how we re-engineer our service capabilities to be as responsive and open as this way of doing business demands.

Title: Making Social Media a Business Driver
Body: As @Laura Bryce mentioned: the question is not 'if' but 'how'. Indeed I agree. At our company we have discovered that a useful method to determine, change and improve all customer interactions is so-called 'service blue printing'. It is basically an exercise to determine, for all of the activities that take place in delivering a service, who-when-and how employees are interacting (or not interacting) with customers. The exercise always results in new ideas on improved customer interactions (moments of truth!). For example it may be discovered that back-office employees often have the experience and knowledge to help customers, whereas they regularly do not have the tools and opportunities to interact with the customers or vice versa. Evaluation of the whole service process by way of 'service blue printing' than often leads to de adoption of various social media initiatives and a better understanding of what needs to be a walled garden discussion and what does not (@Joshua Scribner). Based on these experiences, I think social media does not just start with a social media department (@Sudha Jamthe).

Title: It's all a learning process
Body: Hi Mei Li! It was a great pleasure having met you last week at Lotusphere in Orlando where I am sure you had a good time, just as much as we all did. This is a rather interesting thread with some many good questions and perhaps with no definite answer for each and everyone of them. It's interesting to see how we seem to keep focusing on capturing best practices, again, with social media and trying to transform our businesses, when in reality we all know it's much more effective to go the route of explore, learn, apply, become better at it.  Anyone remember Dell a few years back how they started? Anyone now thinks that Dell is in the same position or quite the opposite? That's exactly what I mean; making mistakes is a natural part of growing, of learning to do better, of becoming better eventually. So while diving into that social transformation I would suggest we all keep our ears and eyes open towards learning along how we can accomplish. I suspect we would be much more successful than  ...   following best practices to most of the questions you shared above.  Dell has proved it can be done; Dell proved that it learned quickly how to move faster; Dell has showed everyone the real benefits of becoming a social enterprise and guess it's up to us to explore our own learning paths in achieving that transformation ;-)

Title: Ready, Fire, Aim
Body: Your comment reminds me of a book of the above title, that I don't recall who the author is. Basically, he advocates new businesses prepare your business but then get out there and provide your service, product, etc. Then you evaluate and the Aim is refining the process.

Title: It's a learning process indeed ...
Body: Hola Luis. Likewise, a pleasure to meet you at Lotusphere last week. It is definitely a pioneering journey and we are all pushing the envelope with our respective endeavours. I would say, though, that beyond the expression of intent lies a wealth of process and smarts needed to effect transformation, and I see great value in sharing tactics and insights to spur us on that journey. We need enlightened legal & compliance partners who help pave and not block the way, willing field-generals who are prepared to transform the way they lead, and service teams that need to transform their service paradigm. The platform and technology is just the beginning and alone, cannot effect the transformation. We can show each other the way in very practical terms, I hope.

Title: Indeed, but don't forget to make mistakes...
Body: Hi Mei Li, what a great follow up commentary! Thanks a bunch for adding it further up! While I was reading through your comments I surely agree with all what you are saying; we would need to be able to tap into every single aspect of the business in order to strike for that total transformation vs. just individual pockets. What I find rather interesting and very thought provoking is how all of this social business surely is changing the way we are perceiving mistakes; before making a mistake could be rather fatal or, at least, embarrassing, definitely, not encouraged, but nowadays with social media everyone understands that people will make mistakes, they would adjust to their lessons learned and, most importantly, move on, which is what I think we should all do and we already have got living examples, like the one from Dell and other powerful brands

Title: Dell
Body: Luis: let's find some time to talk more about Dell. Post-jam follow-up perhaps. Thanks!!!

Title: Certainly!
Body: Of course, Mei Li! I think you know where to find me to follow up with it, right? @elsua in Twitter or elsewhere; let me know whenever you would have a chance... Thanks!!

Title: Walled gardens
Body: Having a private space for interaction enables participants to feel safe. It's a good move when you want to have a deeper conversation where everyone opens up about failure as well as success. Walled gardens also create a sense of privilege, which can be useful when working with company executives or academic elite.  However, there are downsides to the walls. You limit the opportunity for radically different ideas, as walled spaces too often become echo chambers. This jam, for example, is a public space -- no walls here -- and we'll get some interesting and innovative discussions happening. Things we hadn't anticipated. However, IBM also has walled spaces for working with clients 1:1, or in small industry-focused groups.

Title: Walled gardens
Body: Thanks, Joshua. Appreciate the validation of our thinking .....

Title: Situation-specific strategy
Body: I think it's important to recognize the value of both public approach and walled gardens in planning a social media strategy. Obviously, walled gardens are extremely useful for private company information. The question of where you set the walls, and to what extent you allow public into the walls, is a question that, to a large extent, is determined by and determines the future of the customer base you want to support. In the social media platform sector, privacy control is turning to be a niche / differentiating factor in obtaining users - consider for example Path.  That said, I feel that in general discussions that do not involve confidential information - anything relating to customers - should be made publicly available, whether in anonymized form or not, and the general public should be invited to participate. There are various mix-forms here, including paid access for non-customers vs. free access for customers, or moderated discussions, that may be useful for particular use cases - this can help to focus  ...   discussion while not completely blocking the general public.

Title: How of Social Analytics - and the credibility?
Body: Since now it amply clear by posts, that Conversations will happen with or without you (businesses) on social media. The next question is how do you manage it.  Different companies are doing different things including hiring people/BPOs, who manage social media communications. Others try make use of Social media analytics to analyze topics related to business that are being discussed, understanding the social media patterns to analyze connect with community, Do location based analysis or simply understand the sentiment there of.  This can transform the actions/decision that company may take in managing public relations or negative fallout and employ resources at its end to manage social media  ...   Like SEO (Search Engine Optimization) can discredit the search engine (to avoid google comes up with intelligent tracking/crawlers and fake link identification), what the equivalent for Social media, We have examples where people/ competitor continuously floated negative publicity to pull the stock price down.  How much do you trust the social Analytics, Is it going to fall trap to Social Media Optimization (SMO - coining a new word for this).  Your thoughts welcome - Nirupam Srivastava

Title: Every business needs a Social Business "GM"
Body: As I look at successful companies using Social Business, they all had a strong Leader supporting Social Business. For instance, at Slumberland we see "Brian" leading the charge, at Windsor Univ -- we see Kristina, at Cemex a strong leader!  Do you agree? If so .. have you such a person at your company?  Sandy

Title: An Executive champion perhaps... ?
Body: I can easily agree that any company will need an executive to really champion the idea of social business, but I'm not sure if a GM (General Manager) role is needed. Unless, the social business activity is a particular line of business, a general manager doesn't seem necessary. For example, Amazon's Web services which is an entire line of social business for the company quite separate from their main retail store activities would likely call for a GM--I don't know if the have such a role. However, running a community, even with millions of participants, isn't necessarily a separate business of its own.  Perhaps, I'm limiting the concept some.

Title: Strong leader may be antithesis of social
Body: We're a large university with literally hundreds of outposts in social media. Many are not compliant with things we'd like them to be compliant with -- marks, standards, etc.  But a strong leader may be the complete opposite of what we need. I'd say going social requires a guiding hand informed by lots of thought about goals, voice, and image.

Title: Understanding a "Social Business" leader's role
Body: Sandy,  These is a great point, and there is certainly value in having a leader of social business initiatives, or an ambassador/champion as Rawn suggested. I think the challenge for me is understanding how a "social business" role differentiates itself between marketing and IT or operations functions. I believe a social evangelist who really understands social media on a grand scale could promote its use for marketing as well as customer support and then perhaps also social collaboration for internal purposes could champion both. It would be difficult though. Social has to be something that everyone buys into and understands how to leverage.

Title: Erin may well be right
Body: I would concur with the link of thinking which suggests a GM or Chief Social Officer would probably not particularly helpful in many organizations. If any one individual should be responsible for spreading social awareness and engagement, the correct person is the CEO.  What you need is a small cadre of long-term, experienced thought leaders capable who promote and evangelize social media possibilities up and down the organization, leaving ultimate tactical implementation decisions to the respective department/team management already in place.  Note the long-term qualifier. You don't necessarily want a buzzword-laden crew of newbies ranting endlessly about how Instagram is so cool. Rather, you  ...   want folks whose vision includes a longer arc of online social historical experiences, from BBSes to IRC to AOL to Friendster to Now, as they're likely to bring a more meaningful understanding of the current state of affairs and focus on the underlying principals rather than site-of-the-day red shiny objects.

Title: It is a cultural issue
Body: I agree witht he fact that no-one --or maybe the CEO only-- should be a GM for Social Business. However, if you plan to use Social media tools within your organisation, you need to make sure the culture / minding within the company is the right one. It is like Jamming: if your company/organization is too rigid, too hierarchical, too... old-fashinoed, you will never get the benefits of social media. People will not dare to say what they think, will not try to be innovative, or at least will be frightened to be: "what will my boss think? what if I talk to my N+2? what if my colleagues see the idea i post on social media?..."  In the end, it goes to culture, and cultural change needs to be managed. It is not easy, it is not something you change overnight, but strong leadership from high above helps.

Title: Agree with Servane
Body: It is all about leadership and guidance to adopt the cultural change. If leadership and guidance (2.0 Attitude) are seen as a new form of traditional Command & Control, no matter what you do, failure is guaranteed.  Having a true leader, that walks the talk, will generate the adoption and culture change we are looking for.

Title: I think Social Business is broader than Marketing/
Body: Social Business impacts your IT and Marketing for sure...but also client support -- think Zappos -- development -- think Coach, IBM, and others, HR -- think Cemex and the list goes on.  This needs to be a cross business process person in my view.

Title: Social across business processes
Body: Agreed. Treating social business as just another marketing channel is a waste of resources and may even cause harm. So-called social technologies need to be brought inside the Enterprise to enable better engagement with customers. (Enterprise 2.0) #e20

Title: Social Business will be the new way Workplace Norm
Body: Not just for Marketing and reaching out to customers, but reaching out to the entire broader buiness ecosystem- eployees, partners, supply chains. As our business environment becomes more complex, we must tap into more minds to splve challenges. None of us can no longer affort to work in vaccuum.

Title: genuine
Body: I agree with Gagan.  "Social" must be "genuine", is not tactical, is not a seasonal fashion (at least I believe so).  It is like humans: it is difficult to behave in a certain way unless you really espouse the values behind those behaviors. Espousing the values gives you the credibility, the passion, the ability to always be in "movement", never arrived.

Title: new dimension to the business world
Body: Espousing value is truly essential for social business. Thought leaders should look for such values that can somewhere include what Muhammad Yunus wanted from Social Business, where Social business is a new category of business, which does not stipulate the end of the existing type of profit-making business. It widens the market by giving a new option to consumers. It adds to the competition. It brings new dimension to the business world, and a new feeling of social awareness among the business people."  If we just restricting our focus to commercial success of the social business. Its broader meaning would be marginalized.

Title: cross fields
Body: In my opinion today's marketing person needs to be multi-disiplenary, knowing IT, Marketing, Sales and Development. Depending on the industry of course. I have been told by a few developers that a person who knows the IT aspect as well as the marketing can work well with the customers which in turn is the social business, ie client support.

Title: Social business as a cross business process person
Body: I tried to find it but I didn't found any so I am putting it out loud there: wouldn't it be great to have an information designer / graphist mapping out the social business strategies of the companies quoted before so we see which ones are more outsource social business / in house social business / cross business / core business to start identifying social business patterns ?

Title: I'd love to see that too!
Body: That's one infographic I haven't seen yet either - would love to know if one is out there!

Title: Social Business 2.0 needs Leadership 2.0
Body: Leadership is key for soical business initiatives to take root and thrive, and I would say that it takes both leadership from the top and operational follow-through from all of the departments and units across a business for enterprise-wide transformation to be effective. For the 2.0 way of interacting to be ubiquitous, we all need to embrace it as the way we work .... and eventually, evangelism becomes innovation .... leading thinking on how we push the envelope on its application across internal and external arenas.

Title: Leadership 2.0 ideas
Body: @MeiLi - Some months ago, I presented on the topic of leadership for a new generation which was intentionally Millennial generation-focused but the concepts themselves aren't tied to Millennials really. The slides: https://www-950.ibm.com/files/app/person

Title: Social GM
Body: Sandy, I have heard of companies looking to create a Chief Collaboration Officer to drive this phenomenon of Social Business. I think within Organizations, there needs to be one person who owns the program from end-to-end. This way political hurdels have a better chance of being sidestepped, and authority is reinforced. Will this be a new position sprouting up in many companies? I am not sure, but I like the idea

Title: Love this idea!
Body: Great idea as well. Do you have one?

Title: Who leads the social media charge?
Body: I feel that honest and reliable communication has to be an ethic of the enterprise more than an initiative led by a senior executive. Who manages Twitter or its related trending topics?! As we've seen vividly since the release of Wiki Leaks, modern, social dialogue(s) center about the Internet principle of 'everyone knows' as no one individual on entity controls the message. How does the enterprise migrate rapidly from 'knowledge is power' to taking advantage of 'everyone knows' enabling productive focus in the process.

Title: Every business needs a Social Business "GM"
Body: No, we do not. That would be a huge step in our organization, but I do not think we are ready yet.

Title: An Executive champion, yes is needed
Body: I agree that a GM leadership role would be required. The cultivation of the idea and the business funding to deploy the idea, can be outside of budgeting process. To ensure that the business alignment and ROI are fully vetted with the adoption of idea, support with the various operational business functions is required. https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=84516&c=43435

Title: Social Business Ambassadors?
Body: I like the approach IBM took with it's BlueIQ Ambassadors - an impressive group of social media experts and advisors who were always the early adopters. They developed a charter, a community and a cause. Together this group grew to become a ground swell in a very large organization and empowered our leadership with education and incentives to join the movement toward integrating social media into our business.  I think there needs to be a marriage of efforts between the "boots on ground" teams and a select group of leaders to provide vision, strategy, and calls to action. With the bottom up drive and the top down support, any organization can reach its social business goals.

Title: But BlueIQ had a strong sponsorship
Body: I think that BlueIQ represents one of the "tools" that a strong sponsorship decision deployed to deploy its goals. When BlueIQ was born, we had an executive leading and a high level sponsor in the company who wanted, clearly, IBM to become the most advanced social company.

Title: Hybrid approach works best!
Body: As Bill & Stefano both mentioned BlueIQ is a program, of which I am part of ;-), as much as they both are, as ambassadors themselves, that tries to combine the best of both: 1. Executive sponsorship and leadership to make it happen, to want to establish the path to transform how a business becomes *the* showcase of social software adoption and 2. Grassroots effort of having a dedicated team with one or two satellite communities of social software evangelist and enthusiasts who co-jointly get to execute on a program and methodology that ensures the rest of the workforce is educated and facilitated in their adoption of the various social tools available.  It's a joint effort, a partnership of both exec support and daily work of people in the trenches, of wanting to help realise that transformation of the business and help it make it an engaged and empowered business.  ...   Time and time we keep sharing the story of that hybrid approach and it seems to have resonated with plenty of businesses, not just IBM; certainly, worth while combining both of them

Title: Why stop with one??
Body: Hi Sandy! Rather than the appointment of a single person (which might be a good start to champion the cause) - what about the creation of a Social Business / Collaboration Center of Excellence? Organizationally perhaps it could be similar to a shared services organization; it doesn't sit in one silo but serves everyone. (Although, admittedly, a cost-recovery model may present its own set of challenges!)  Infusing initiatives with members of the CoE could breathe new life into planning, operations, and service delivery by having a person or small team focused on promoting collaboration and social media. Ultimately, it can help organizations better leverage the social technologies they have in place today, and understand the technologies and social activities they will need in the future. -Jennifer-

Title: Profile
Body: If people agree that a GM role needs to exist, what does that person need to look like? This role will be a leader and salesperson within the organization. This person will need to be one to keep aware of current technical and social trends. I think even just an outgoing personality to encourage collaboration and teamwork. Personally a perky cheerleader is a turnoff, but an advocate and one that is willing to push those that refuse to change is a person that I want to work for.  For the minute details, the age, size and makeup of the organization can suggest who the ideal person should be. It may be ugly, but even gender or cultural background can feed this, again, based on the organization makeup.  ...   Then the question is, how much authority does this person have. How much support will this person have from upper management? How willing is this person to say, well this specific method doesn't fit our organization, but let's try something else?

Title: A leader - yes - for now
Body: I believe we all hope that the underlying theory behind what we all call social media will not always be a special effort that it will become the normal way of doing business - until that time there needs to be an evangelist spearheading the effort. Not an option if the efort is to be successful - depending on how you define success.

Title: Social Media champion
Body: I agree with some of the respondents here. I believe it is important to have someone - a social media (SM) champion or "owner" - to champion/advocate SM. Not only to drive adoption, but to strategize, to take a holistic view of SM and ensure all objectives and campaigns are aligned with the business/company's direction.  SM is a communication channel/tool. It is part of PR (eg: crisis management) or marketing (eg: online contest or event posting) or customer service, etc. SM should not be an "after thought" or a "plug in" and a SM champion will be a great help in ensuring that SM is not done in silo.

Title: Non Profits too
Body: The non profit I work with www.weforest.org was founded by Bill Liao founder of Xing, so no surprise that we are very social network concerned, savvy, (but always learning and always surprises; things that click and those that flop)

Title: Leadership and sponsorship
Body: I liked very much this thread.  I just had a discussion this afternoon with a Sales colleague of mines who is in charge with industrial SMBs in France. His question was exactly "what could help one of my customers to be really interested in Social?". All people talk about Social and even small companies think that it "is an interesting topic".  But there is a big difference between "thinking it is an interesting subject" and "deciding to engage the company on this" !  ...   I tend to think that a high-level sponsor is required. I am not mastering executive job titles. I do not think that, in theory, a separate role would be required except for very big and dispersed companies. But I think that an existing executive wearing the "Social Business GM" hat would be suitable in most cases.  Why an executive? It is because of the fact that we are not talking about introducing a tool here. Social Business must be genuinely built into the fabric of the company in order to become effective. It is a change much bigger than the introduction of "email" (which, after all, became so pervasive because it embodied many of the internal processes...).  Having simply some evangelists may work in highly motivated situations (like universities or research centers) because, I believe, in those situations people are more autonomous and are used to shape the way they do their business.  In a "little industrial company" there needs to be motivation and daily guidance (and lead by example) since becoming "social" may imply changing the routine job... and when these things happen, a strong leadership who is also able to reward and recognize, is I think required.

Title: Leadership involves action by many
Body: Every business needs a *group* of strong leaders. One GM may provide vision, but social leadership involves action by many leaders who have responsibilities across the business. A leadership group needs to work together to deliver consistent guidelines, policies (legal, HR, and otherwise), tools strategy, and execution. Social Business vision can come from the top, but leadership comes from active participation.

Title: Social Leadership
Body: Jeanne - Yes. I disagree that an organization needs a Social Business GM. I agree that to be successful, there must be a "group" of strong leaders. The group must be collaborative to move forward in a way that is best for the specific business's mission and objectives. The question that comes to mind is how do companies keep the group from working at a snails pace because they are distracted by fighting fires and day-to-day business objectives?  I believe that social is not inherently intuitive. Largely b/c there is much new behavior and skills that people can be incredibly resistant to. thus, I believe that achieving social requires a lot of active support and help from peers, ambassadors, reverse mentors, business partners, random helpful people. Bringing in people from within or outside an organization can help accelerate understanding and movement.

Title: A change agent
Body: With the introduction of new ways of doing business, especially when associated with adopting "disruptive" technologies, having a change agent to lead the organization, will be a good idea. This person should have a strong network across all aspects of the business in order to help mobilize the interest and support across the enterprise.

Title: Social business leadership dependent on size?
Body: I work for a small market research consulting firm and have informally taken the lead in pushing our adoption of social technologies to inform our primary research and to market our business. We don't have a current 'policy' in place, but I am developing one because our efforts thus far have been a bit scattered and haphazard. This results in some confusion in our company about how and why new tools should be used.  I agree with what some others have posted about not jumping on the social media bandwagon, to establish a social business leader just for the sake of it. But I do think it's helpful to have a leader (or a group of leaders in a larger organization) who are dedicated to defining social business strategy for the company.

Title: Thoughts from a Social Media Director
Body: I'm the social media director for a small (six-person) organization within larger IBM. On paper, I hold the social media portfolio, but in practice, everyone in the organization engages our audience through social media.  As an example, since I have come on board, we've expanded our blogging efforts, enhanced them through tweeting, and expanded our sites beyond publishing on our own blog--now reaching our audiences through other publications (in our case, GovLoop and Huffington Post).  My role in the organization is not itself responsible for our social media successes, but rather, I think, is itself an effect of the root cause from which  ...   those successes also arise: a commitment to engagement through social media both by leadership and by the staff.  From top to bottom, the people in my organization understand that the conversations that matter most to us are occurring as much within digital social media channels as they are in conference rooms and private offices throughout our sector. And everyone here wants to ensure that our content is part of that conversation.  So I think that while having a "Social Media GM" may be important, it's the outcome of a total dedication to engaging through social media. It can't be a replacement for that dedication, and the person who fills the role should not try (and can't be expected) to do all the social media engagement all alone.

Title: Look to the past for the answer
Body: Most businesses wrestled with this same staffing question a decade ago. At the time, the issue wasn't "social" - it was "e-business." You can see how teams and roles evolved from there...

Title: GM of Social Business ??
Body: There are days when I think the worst mistake the IT industry ever made was creating the role of the IS or IT Manager, which has now become the CIO. We spend huge amounts of time and talk on the "new" CIO who is an effective Business Leader as well as an effective Technology Leader. What if all Business Leaders were more technology savvy - how much more innovation could have been accomplished?  I would much rather see all GMs become more Social savvy, and not see the creation of a GM for Social Media. Working with a business now which is looking to become more collaborative - discussion keeps coming back to the fact that the CEO needs to be the business sponsor for the initiative to achieve the desired outcomes.

Title: First success condition
Body: I agree with that, Sandy. And yes, in my society, one of the biggest manager is implied in the change. He's blogging internally, and following the internal social network progress.  I think in big structure, you have to get at least one big manager implied, because there are a lot of natural barriers (age, culture of privacy, need to keep informations as a tool of power, etc...).  One has to show the path.

Title: Voted up from below?
Body: There are a couple of interesting questions arising from this. One is which way do you cut it? Do you have one person responsible for all social / collaborative relations internally and externally - and then still have people overseeing one-on-one relationships with customers and employees?  The other question for me is whether this would reinforce the hierarchical approach social business is trying to get away from. Perhaps this GM should be selected by the networks and communities in the organisation? - as their representative, not someone who would tell them what to do.

Title: Permission - Encouragement - Enablement
Body: Like the idea. No question, leadership by example is required. 'Permission' and 'encouragement' come from this leadership. We've also found that having a team member substantially dedicated, a 'social business manager' makes a significant difference. This person works to an intentional system: listening, connecting, engaging, building constituency on behalf of the organization, leading by example. They can also be a 'go to' person for those wanting to learn.

Title: Permission - Encouragement - Enablement
Body: Hi George! You bring up a really important concept. Permission.  From my perspective, permission is actually multi-faceted especially for leaders who engage with and model social media and technologies. Here are some of my takes on permission:  Permission not to be prefect (ok, to make a typo).  ...   Permission to change one's mind.  Permission to not know everything.  Permission to admit and address mistakes.  Permission to be personable and connect at a human level (not sound like a press release or a set of talking points).  Permission to dedicate time to social (just as leaders dedicate time to golf).  Now, there are risks that the legal and finance and risk and HR and other folks might bring up. That's okay. Address the issues. Work within corporate business conduct guidelines. But, social business is not social if the person being social is not genuine or authentic.

Title: Permission
Body: Thanks for the adds Jennifer. Agree with all. Attached to permission, sideways, is recognition. A really constructive way to incentivize social behavior and in many of the posts elsewhere.

Title: Social Media 'leadership' is tricky
Body: The discussion around whether social business is bigger than Marketing and therefore needs to be adopted organization wide, and managed by more than just Marketing is an interesting one.  I agree, that social touches aspects of the business that Markting cannot watch over or manage, and therefore needs a wider group of 'managers'.  That said, there's also voice out there that calls for a VP of Social to guide the social ship. So should thi person be from Marketing? IT? Production? Sales? Investor Relations? And if she is from one of these, then obviously there's a slant in how Social is seen, driven an managed. The slant also  ...   comes from who owns the budget (read: pays for social).  Suppose we instead look at Social management for an organiztion like a football team. The CEO (coach) decides the team, and plans the play. But then once on the field, everyone plays to their 'position' but is always available to take the ball and run with it... and likewise make the play for another team mate.  This is just the seed of an idea, and perhaps something worth exploring further (maybe over couple of beers and a football game) :-))

Title: No doubt executive support is needed
Body: I not sure if a SM GM is needed, but no doubt about executive support. But we must be carefull. This happens with a Bank in Peru. The GM proudly announced in an all-employees message that soon they will create the Facebook for the bank. Doing Value Alignment interviews to help them with the first steps, I found nobody was happy with the bank being social. The main reason: everybody thought that if they have Facebook they will lost their privacy.  A great effort was needed to let everybody know that they will be a Social Business, that personal information is neither needed nor shared and that great advantages will be brought to the business.  So, Top down support, but button up adoption

Title: The challenge of Viralization
Body: A few days ago we were having a Round Table to define how to implement Lotus Connections on our Business Unit.  There was a proposal about setting up a process with a clear owner and get a sponsor for its implementation: Top Down messages.  I am from new school (not that young though), I think an approach like that is obsolete. As Marcelo explained in the Bank Story, it is important a GM Support. But not to communicate Top Down messages but to set up an integrated strategy that embraces and considers the whole organization.  One thing Internet is teaching us day by day is that if a tool is usefull and available the people will do the rest...

Title: Opportunity for new job roles
Body: A Social Business GM is a good idea. Social Business presents an opportunity for new job roles within an Organization such as Twitter Jockey. MTV hired it's first ever TJ in 2010. There are also instances of companies creating executive level positions specifically for Digital Media and Social Media.  Has your company or organization created new job roles to support Social Media?

Title: Social Business is a Mindset
Body: Social business is more than a set of tactics to support transactions (i.e., a "how" rather than a "what"), then I think an organization does need a leader who is oriented in social business and who leads socially. In the present moment, then, the role is evolving as social media evolves and as social media practices are internalized by indivdiuals.

Title: A GM for Social Media
Body: Given the consequences of a social media plan gone wrong and the potential impact on brands it's crucial that organizations get it right. This means the development of a sound social media policy,choosing the right model for social media management,adoption of the right tools, management of vendor relationships, integration of social with IT, marketing, public relations, sales, customer relations, HR and more.  Without C-SUITE endorsement this won't happen.  Consider a company with multiple brands or divisions. Each specific division or brand will eventually have a social media team made up of specific skill sets eg: strategists, content developers and community managers.  ...   Who are they going to report to?  The GM of Social Media of course.

Title: More than a Leader, Visible action counts
Body: Sandy, no doubt a Leader for Social Media is important. But this leadership needs to be action oriented than just mere words. The momentum a Company's social media focus and activities can is far greater if Leaders walk the social talk, rather than simply talk the talk via emails expressing their support.  A tweet by executives is far more energizing than grand social media vision documents.  The best way to be social is by... well, being social!

Title: What is your 140 Character Elevator pitch?
Body: We used to think in terms of elevator pitches now we need to think of texting and tweets.  Are you boiling your value props down to 100 characters or less? Making room in the 140 characters for retweets and #hashtags of course. You better do it because if you don't your teams and customers will do it for you!  We now include sample blogs, micro-blogs, visuals, links in all our campaign kits, do you? Do you measure their usage?

Title: social communications
Body: Agreed. We need to get clear and simple, honest and short. All the filler words are going away -- works for me : )

Title: YES
Body: Less is MUCH more

Title: KISSS -- The way to success
Body: Before anyone gets ideas, its the new mantra of marketing success I am speaking about -- Keep It Short Sweet & Social.  The lingo of Abbrs (abbreviations for those who still prefer long form communications, or should I say commns!)  A Poke and a Like are the new 'Howdy'.  Folks  ...   today send more tweets and post more Facebook updates than they probably send email.  No wonder marketers need to think in terms of nibble-sized communications, but a wide spread that satiates the appetite of the audience. And keep them hungry drooling for the next nibble. While being delicious enough for them to tell their friends about.  The most potent marketing collateral is the social stream of their audience. The most potent marketing messages are 'open source' messages. That allow the audience to the messages, make them their own, and spread them... further through their social networks.  So it's time for marketers to pucker up to the new reality of marketing, and er, KISSS... :-)

Title: Rethinking Knowledge Work for Social Business
Body: Analysis of "social" interactions to understand customer jobs-to-be-done and desired outcomes will be key in conceiving and guiding effective engagement strategies - and will serve as the foundation for uniting the company's capabilities to do so.  One thing that I do question though is the continuing use of job roles within an company considering how Knowledge Work is evolving and where it is headed. You are given a job description and objectives to meet, very much in the tradition of Taylor. It may however be the case that due to your abilities and experiences, you are qualified to provide insights and accomplish activities which are beyond the initial scope of the role, but which can be highly beneficial to meeting organisational objectives of satisfying the customer job-to-be-done.  In Social Business, would it not make sense to unite the right people at the right time not only based not only on their role, but also on their competencies and knowledge? We now are getting the tools to do this more effectively thru for example Workforce Analytics, tagging, social search, expertise identification, connection capabilities, collaboration platforms and so on.  It work require a rethink of how we compensate and motivate people, but ultimately should lead to more effective ways of collaborating to understanding and meeting the customer job-to-be-done.  Within Social business we are fluidifying communications and breaking down the barriers between the company and the other constituants of its ecosystem. Would it not make sense to fluidify job descriptions and roles as well?  Keen to hear your thoughts.

Title: Dual objectives: Grow positive buzz and get input
Body: In Jacob Boetter's post https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=75454&c=29001 , he suggested we all should identify customers who are promoters. That got me thinking that it seems that engaging with customers might have dual objectives:  1) Getting input and feedback from customers to develop better products/services and/or to fix/avoid problems.  2) Nurture and grow positive buzz -- from both engaging with your promoters and working with influential detractors.  Are both equally important objectives? If so, how do you address both?

Title: Growing advocacy while addressing detractors
Body: Great question Amy, I believe both are equally important.  What could be more genuine than a customer whoŌĆÖs had a good experience sharing it with others? Growing your base of customer base of active advocates through sustained engagement is imperative, but requires a new engagement coverage model. One that aligns the best suited Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) across the business to effectively engage with the expertise/support/service the customer is seeking. This not only proves that we are listening but are taking action. In return, they share more good vibes with their connections. The same would be true for those who are negative. We must embrace in these dialogues to let them know we are paying attention, care to resolve and to help educate them further. If we engage with detractors, we have the ability to turn them into advocates. If we donŌĆÖt, competitors will cease this negativism to their advantage.  ...   This coverage model requires an infrastructure with the foundation of listening supported by an informed engagement response management system. One that enables SMEs across the organizations to engage effectively where they will provide value based on their unique expertise and skills.

Title: Dual objectives compliment each other
Body: Amy shared that dual objectives are:  1) Getting input and feedback from customers to develop better products/services and/or to fix/avoid problems.  2) Nurture and grow positive buzz -- from both engaging with your promoters and working with influential detractors.  I think both are equally important objectives because they compliment each other. If you get good input and feedback that enables you to provide better products/services, it will generate more positive discussion. Likewise, if you nurture the buzz around the product/services, more people will be interested  ...   to learn - and inevitably have something to say- about them. What comes first, the chicken or the egg? Does it matter in what order we pursue these objectives? Are there other objectives we should also keep in mind?

Title: Build on Customer Experience & Analytics
Body: I think that we need to be open to new thinking on social engagement with customers, but not forget all the valuable experience we all have from years of work on Customer Experience (CX) and Customer Analytics (CA) challenges. For example, our CX knowledge tells us of the importance of fixing what is broken, delighting customers where they value it, and right-sizing aspects they do not value. Our CA work stresses the importance of evidence based decisions and moving to a sense and respond model. So businesses definitely need to gather and analyse data to identify promoters and detractors, but also to identify what are the topics that get them excited. The better a business is at rapidly identifying customer issues and being seen to respond to them, the more likely customers are to become promoters (and by tracking these effects over time we can prove ROI of social initiatives). There are some new customer management challenges with social business, such as gathering and understanding unstructured data from interactions and balancing investment in new social channels with existing initiatives, but by applying what we already know we get a head start.

Title: Absolutely both are key!
Body: This is a great point and excellent questions. Social tools allow for candor. Candor shortens the ability for businesses to adjust more quickly and leverags the positive feedback from customers to drive even greater business results, while criticism by customers will be equally important to respond and react to trends that require reversal or attention. This might fall into be careful what you ask for you just might get it. But that actually is the beauty of being social IMHO.

Title: Meeting customer expectations for social engagemt
Body: In one of L.Creasey's posts https://www.collaborationjam.com/minijam3/jam/discussionThread.do?gt=true&jamId=83289&f=75454&c=30207 , he suggested that "testing through social media could speed up certain processes, but it could also create an unholy mess."  That made me think that when an organization starts interacting with customers, customers will then expect a certain level of response / engagement / interaction. Does an organization have to consider how to manage expectations to the level of social interaction it can support?

Title: market research
Body: I believe that before jumping into SM we have to prepare our strategy. But not only our Marketing strategy but our CRM. How are we going to answer our customers, how are we going to keep our relations with them, how we will answer to bad comments.  The good thing of SM is that they are perfect for market research, we can ask our prospectives and clients , what they think about our product or service, what they want out of us.  When doing MArketing 2.0 training I tell my students the good thing of this precious opportunity is small businesses can now easily affor to this market research.  BUt  ...   of course you have to keep a line on how you make things because your fans will expect a level from you

Title: Expectations not being met
Body: Very thought provoking. I expect customers will get quite agitated if they invest time and effort to try and help you and they see that effort ignored. The design and communication of the interaction will be critical. If a small group is heavily involved in social interactions with a company, I think they will expect attention and responsive interaction back....if a broadcast survey of preferences, etc, perhaps all that is expected is to see the collective results. Perhaps others can comment on implied degree of interaction depending on expectation

Title: Level of expectation and co-creation
Body: Interesting thought... Do you think organizations should always provide their 'best effort'? In fact, which is the level of expectations of our customers? Some studies prove that while this level should be met by companies, any additional effort is often a waste of time and resources.  My question: how can we measure customers' level of expectations, in order to set up a strategy to meet it, and in order to define KPIs to further enhance it, and co-create value with our customers.

Title: It's all about the customer
Body: We had some controversal discussions down here in Germany on the usage of the word "Social". There are other interpretations of Social (Yunus etc.). But what is social on Social Business? It is the interaction and communication with the customer (or with the other employees in your enterprise). This is the Social in Social business.  It is an extension and new quality of networking going beyond face-to-face meetings, traditional events and marketing.  It is a two-way or multiple-way communication. It is not one-way communciation.

Title: Social business and Digital Marketing
Body: Isn't digital marketing and social business 2 sides of the same coin? What companies are after are customer intimacy and thus customr loyalty. How do we handle and govern the convergence of our 'advertising agencies' and our 'IT departments'...working together?

Title: Social media means transformation!
Body: Dear Lisbeth, to my opinion the question has to be broadened. An integrated social media strategy within a company means not just collaboration between marketing and IT. Sales, service and development also have to be taken into account. So we will face a complete transformation process!

Title: The Role of sCRM in the Web 3.0 World
Body: sCRM (social customer relationship management) platforms are rapidly emerging as the command center and the glue between customer facing social media marketing/listening and e-business innovation as it relates to business intelligence and business processes. What are the trends emerging around this? Will those who create a holistic strategy emerge as the best practice or will the silo'd internal and external approach continue?

Title: sCRM and the customer-centric approach
Body: I asked myself the same question as you did and came to a conclusion after some thinking. It is always the same when you talk about CRM: Those who just think about IT and processes will remain in their silos, but those, who understood it as a philosophy and act this way will just be delighted about the new possibilities of getting in touch with their customers. From a companies' perspective, doing sCRM for me means the same as becoming customer-centric.

Title: Social Learning Portal
Body: Late last year we (Trivantis) agreed with the IBM Learning Group that they would deploy an internal learning portal with Connections on the IBM intranet. This is an exciting project as it targets learning and will bring it together with social business. The idea is to use social networks in learning to grow the use of individual authoring of ELearning courses. In other words, empower IBM-ers to develop courses on subjects that interest them, rather than come at training from the perspective of the company. The expectation is that that this will be extended into the customer base, again using social networks to spread the message and attract interest. As we know, we trust the recommendation of people in our social network so, the idea is that we use these recommendations to drive interest in learning creation.

Title: Adaptive Enterprises andSense and Respond theory
Body: I am curious about the interplay between Adaptive Enterprises (Stephen Haekel)- how it relies on sense and respond structures within organizations, and how those things (sense and respond) relate to the many "voices" presented in current and future social networking. I think the IMPACT would be VERY SIGNIFICANT. Your thoughts? Are there additional triggers (sense and respond) within our organizations that we could naturally (organically) capture? Is there value?

Title: Social to Prioritize Product Enhancements
Body: I am hearing of groups rapidly evolving their requirements gathering and prioritization of actions to improve or create entirely new products. I am interested in opinions on how pervasive this practice is in companies right now...Is your company using social communities to directly rank what you do next?

Title: Marketing ROI for Social
Body: I am starting to see 3rd party firms, like Zuberance, confirm that advocacy programs where companies identify and energize customer advocates are getting up to 10X ROI in media and sales value. In other words, for every $1 a company invests in energizing Advocates, the company gets $10 in positive word of mouth impressions and sales. This is significantly higher than the return that marketers get from paid search campaigns on Google and other marketing approaches. Customer advocates are highly-satisfied customers who pro-actively recommend their favorite brands and products online and offline, without being paid to do so.

Title: Building/measuring positive sentiment
Body: Scott, Zuberance is a very interesting example. I'd also site Ignite Social Media http://www.ignitesocialmedia.com as a leader in this space. And their model comes back to content. While content is king, relationships are golden.

Title: Marketing ROI for Social
Body: Scott, I also think most of the work around Social Marketing ROI is being performed in the Retail sectors. I would like to see the Industrial\Manufactoring\Channel sectors undergo some more analysis. There could be great potential for Social in the Industrial\Manufactoring sectors especially around supply chain.  good post, thanks

Title: Beyond Retail
Body: Richard, I agree, retail tends to be the popular example for using social tactics to build customer loyalty. But we are seeing similar success in other industries. For example, China Telecom, the largest fixed-line service and third-largest mobile telecommunication provider in China. To stay competitive, the Shanghai branch of China Telecom wanted a way to accelerate creation of new telecom services by optimizing use of its employee base in a unified innovation process.  They developed an innovation platform with a Web portal interface that enables collaboration among employees, partners and customers. The portal accepts ideas from this enlarged community, expanding the sources of innovation and helping to filter the best quality ideas. More than 550 new "voices" joined the development process in the first six months of the portal launch, with publication of the first idea a mere ten minutes after launch. Marketing  ...   teams can analyze new intelligence gathered directly from consumers' Web 2.0 entries and introduce new services with the knowledge that subscriber demand exists. More product ideas of higher quality reduce opportunity costs and risks, and increase the chances of marketing success. This solution enables the company to deliver exciting products to the marketplace at a faster pace than ever before.

Title: Manufacturing/Supply Chain
Body: Richard,  I'm currently working with a company where we are focusing much of our efforts around Manufacturing and their supply chain constituents..ie. Wholesalers, retailers etc. We help companies build closed loop marketing systems that integrates with Supply Chain and ERP data so as to help them better align demand with their manufacturing schedules and processes. I believe there is a huge opportunity in this market in helping companies maximize their investments in supply optimization by integrating with systems that drive demand. That's the promise of CRM.
