Title: ROI for social
Body: And what about ROI for Social within the enterprise? I mean when Social is used within the Firewall.  How do we measure and demonstrate value in terms of ROI?

Title: Great example
Body: Marcelo, we are seeing this with Sogeti. One of the world's leading providers of IT consulting services and solution integration. As it expanded across 15 countries, information silos made locating and collaborating with the vast expertise in the company difficult. Sogeti needed new ways to foster teamwork and peer communication among its many business groups and locations.  To tie together over 20,000 people across 200 locations, Sogeti deployed an enterprise-wide social networking and collaboration platform for finding and leveraging expertise, knowledge transfer, close teaming across distances and sharing of best practices. Now, integrated multiple active directories provide a unified approach to identifying expertise wherever it may be. Easier access to the tacit knowledge of others helps co-workers develop their skills, and fast identification of people's skills supports efficient staffing with the right people for any project or mission. Together, accelerated knowledge transfer, better use of expertise and the ability to staff the right people quickly is preparing Sogeti to enter new markets. And being able  ...   to share rather than having to reinvent key processes is yielding significant savings in project startup costs.

Title: ROI
Body: I've discussed ROI and built business cases for many a customer, and often times, executives want firm/hard #'s to rationalize a buying decision. We can certainly an ROI, as there is significant time savings from finding expertise, improving employee satisfaction, reducing employee turnover, improving the on boarding process, shortening the education & training timeline for new hires, reduce the need to send around large files, thus reducing storage and/or redundant emails with attachments. Hard #'s are easy, but the customer(s) have to work with the vendor to validate that they buy in to the phiolosphy and the savings stated. The last time I bought a car, I didn't do an ROI analysis, as I know I need a car to get to and from customer meetings, personal engagements, etc....  I don't remember when someone did an ROI on whether or not to buy Satellite or Cable TV. To me, Social Collaboration Tools are in the same venue. You  ...   need them! An ROI is nice, but by the time you think you have a solid ROI, your losing more ground to your competition.

Title: Compare to Google search for $ Values
Body: One way to show value is to measure your click-thrus on marketing assets like webinars, white papers, videos, etc. We use a social media campaign tool to track clickthrus from twitter, facebook, blogs, youtube all into one dashboard for our marketing team.  1) Count your social click-thrus to these assets = 1000 per month for example  2) Find out what it cost on average to purchase your keywords on google ($6 us)  3) Simply multiply your click-thrus times the avg google keyword buy and you get a pretty valid number.  1000X$6  ...   = $6000 per month in value.  This doesn't mean you stop buying keywords because social must be part of your "integrated plan". But it does allow you to show "real" dollar value to your executive teams!

Title: is ROI relevant for social networking?
Body: I heard someone recently who suggested to talk about ROE = Return On Engagement, rather than ROI.  Though I don't know how to measure an ROE, I like this idea for 2 reasons:  * I'm pretty convinced that social media is more an investment and a long-term way to connect with people, which will indirectly (and maybe directly, later) impact business. It is not when you will need these connections that you will be able to create them. It is now that you should create these links, just like you manage your own personal network, without any instant payback. Thus this idea of "engagement"  *  ...   ROI is too much used for existing marketing approach, and tends to be rather short-term, so better choose another term to clearly highlight it is something different  ==> what do you think of "ROE"?  ==> any other existing term or suggestion which would be more appropriate?

Title: ...Not everything that counts can be counted !
Body: Not everything that can be counted counts, and not everything that counts can be counted..  To piggyback on Albert Einstein's quote, I believe that we should be very careful and not rush too quickly and try to define metrics and ROI objectives for Social Business, at least as long as we do not fully understand how it "works" !  I run an initiative here in France called Club Alliances, and we leverage Social Media and Social Networks for credibility, visibility and inbound Marketing in a B2B environment.  ...   After 18 months of experimentations, I definitely believe that we should better focus on making Social Business work instead of spending too much time trying to rationalize and justify, because of this ROI cult [or even ROE - BTW, Thierry, my colleague from Marketor is a big fan of ROE...]

Title: Attracting more participants in testing
Body: What sorts of things have you tried to get more participants to comment? How are you trying to drive more traffic?

Title: Testing by default
Body: I'm sure Netflix did not mean to test their new business model via social media, but that is exactly what they did in January. It was a brief blog post on their site telling customers that they will no longer be able to add movies to their mail order DVD queue if the title is available for streaming and mobile devices. Netflix's desire to move away from the mail order DVD business was met with a wave of angry responses by customers in blog posts for all to see.  Do you think Netflix will listen and engage with the customers who are being so vocal about the services they would like to see or ignore the dialogue?  ...   Do you think companies should use social to test strategies before investing funds to change their business models?

Title: Testing with social may speed up the process
Body: Just read an interesting blog post by Shel Holtz on the Groupon Super Bowl commercial about Tibet. He has an interesting take on waiting and watching before backpedalling. Wonder if that would work for Netflix ...  I think testing through social media could speed up certain processes, but it could also create an unholy mess. Anyone have any tips on where to start?

Title: For sure it is fast, but how you measure results?
Body: If you post a "strong opinion" on a blog or forum, for sure you will get instant feedbacks. No need to spend a long time preparing a survey, finding contacts, organise meetings ... So to quickly get some qualitative feedbacks, I think it is pretty good (well, depends on the subject & target, of course). I made some tests and found it quite interesting to capture opinions, arguments, counter-arguments ... and sometime detect opinion leaders on the subject.  But for me the point is how do you evaluate these feedbacks? How representative are there? Because the people who are coming and interacting on the web are a very small portion of all the potential buyers (including thos who are not "connected" on the web)?  ...   Any experience on evaluating the relevance of such tests ?

Title: Less single issue, more a committed audience
Body: Laurent, I was thinking more of using a Facebook fan base, for example. Our fans are very opinionated (pro and con), and I think we could ask them questions about small changes first -- almost like a free focus group.

Title: CRM
Body: An application of the practices behind twelpforce would:  - act as another cog in the CRM wheel. You have a potential customer with every question asked. Its up to you to not only answer their question, but to allow followup to develop that long term relationship.  - questions and answers as a whole provide a base of knowledge to draw others to you as a knowledge resource - potentially as a thought leader in your space -esp combined with external blogs, external communities and the like.  - questions asked, combined with some analytics data can give you insights into what your potential customers are thinking which could drive innovation,  ...   and new product and service development.

Title: How can we better engage IBM customers
Body: Numerous studies tell us that standout organizations work more closely (and systemically) with their customers and partners. It leads to better relationships, products, revenue and profits. IBM has great on premise and cloud products at our disposal. How can IBM use its social business tools to interact and engage with our customers?

Title: Can IBM use soc. bus. to engage with customers?
Body: I think its an interesting question Sean.  The best examples of companies using social media to engage with customers are companies that are interested in individual people, not just other businesses. Social Media is all about the individual, their personality, likes, dislikes, interests, and I think there is a massive challenge for a corporate like ours to work out how to get the most out of social media on a business to business level, as well as with individuals who might be our customers.  Part  ...   of me wonders whether its even possible. For example "IBM" doesn't have a facebook page... is that a bad thing? or is it a necessity in an organisation so large?  The one area in which I think we could definitely make more use is in sharing experiences with our customers and partners - encouraging staff internally to engage through social media (at the moment I think people are mostly nervous they'll get in trouble for tweeting about their company!) and then using the analytics tools available to us to give us an idea of opinions of products or success stories.

Title: IBM on Facebook
Body: IBM does have a very active presence on Facebook. There are multiple pages, representing different divisions of the company. For example, I encourage you to look at (and ŌĆ£likeŌĆØ) the Public Sector Consulting Facebook page. http://www.facebook.com/IBMPSC#!/IBMPSC?v=app_4949752878 The page provides an overview of services offerings, upcoming events, thought leadership and even an area for searching for careers at IBM.  ...   The power (and necessity) to having a Facebook page is that our clients, partners and recruits expect it. But more than that, a Facebook page and other social media outlets allow for more than just the one way communication the standard webpage provides. Visitors to an organizationŌĆÖs Facebook or LinkedIn page can view the network of people interested in or associated with that company. Social is about the power of connecting people. By being able to view the network of people and then reach out to that network, social helps to engage our audience.  It is impossible for IBM or any company to be all things to all audiences online. By that I mean, you are not always going to know exactly why a person seeks out your organization. And when they do view your page, they may or may not find the information they seek. By using social media, you can provide visitors more than just the standard "contact us" button on a website. You provide them a network, a community, a group of people with names and faces and profiles with which they can connect and engage further - as an employee, partner or customer.

Title: Another IBM Facebook page
Body: I agree that social media is about the individuals...and the more individual IBMers are involved in social media, the more IBM, as a company, will be recognized as a social business. I've been using Facebook as a way to connect with our internal/external community, although I'm always looking for ways we can expand its use (along with other social media tools). I'd be interested in your suggestions.  www.facebook.com/lotustechinfo

I am 29 and I may never able to see a communication which is being done only on TV, I no longer watch TV. or via tradition communication method, its hard for me to keep up with snail mails sent by businesses

Title: agree with that
Body: Agree - Social is targetted information to opted-in followers. What's more, they probably carry a portable device - all the time - anywhere - and configure their device access modes (hours of the day / notification types) and at the click of a button have access to detailed information, the author of the piece, other people who followed the idea.  Opportunities for branding, messaging, offers, technical help, reminders, discussions, in-app purchases all where your customer is all of the time. Managed well, social brings those thins to people that matter to you.

Title: Not a question of IF anymore
Body: Organizations that are still debating IF they should be using social tools promote their products and services, engage with customers or test out new ideas will quickly get left behind. It is no longer a matter of IF but HOW. If your company or organization is not using these tools, you can probably guarantee that your competitors are. Not only do they see customer reviews of your products, they can see the suggestions for new services or the dialogue from the group that suddenly feels disenfranchised. This becomes a new market or potential new customers for them.  At the top of the list of where customers spend most of their time should be behind a computer or attached to a mobile device. Generation Y is touted as the most "connected" generation. Even in industries where laptops or computers were not traditionally used are changing with the proliferation of iPads and mobile devices.  ...   The HOW to use social media is evolving also. Sure, someone can "like" your company on Facebook, but are you thinking of new ways to use these tools or simply trying to keep up?  What are some of the new ways organizations are or should be using social media to engage, attract or retain customers?  What companies are doing a good job of it?

Title: Take one step forward in Social Media
Body: I'm pretty sure that we can take even more advanteges from social media connection than getting new customers, or making the cust-bussiness communicacion effective.  What about letting other businesses know what do you do, and what you could potencially do if you have the chance to get connected with other bussiness? Just like a polymer (a chain of amino acids) ready to become a any kind of protein by getting connected with another polymer within the right environment.  How  ...   far do you want to take your bussines? Just pick up any other industry and think what would happen if you two get connected? what kind of new product/service could you provide?  Or even better, what kind of social impact could you two provide just by embracing a single project?

Title: Not a question of have to, but how to
Body: I think it's agreed upon across the business world that social media gives companies the opportunity to reach a broad base of potential consumers 1:1.  I think one of the biggest issues I see in the execution of a customer-facing social business strategy is that companies tend to market rather than enable. The power of social communities is in tapping into your consumers and having them speak their minds whether it be good or bad on behalf of the company. Companies then need to enable these people to be more prolific. So let's say John Doe is unhappy with your product/service - find out why. Connect directly with John Doe and change his experience. Then, this guy who was spouting bad things about your company says good things.  ...   We can't market people, we can't tell them what to say - but we can identify those having a negative experience, and change it.

Title: Who really wants to be friends?
Body: I think one of the biggest issues I see in the execution of a customer-facing social business strategy is that companies tend to market rather than enable.  Totally agree with Jenny's comment. In reality, how many companies do consumers really want to be friends with in the classic "I love your brand" way? The same few come to mind, brands like Apple, Audi, Livestrong, Burton, Nordstrom. There are more--but for the vast majority of brands in all sectors, the notion of friendship is a hollow one (or the definition of friendship has devolved, in my opinion). It's all about listening to the customer, responding to their needs both in the short term (fixing their problem or answering their question quickly and accurately) and in the long term (evolving your company's products to better meet customers' needs). It is not about pushing products or serving as an additional (cheap!) marketing channel by spamming your customers. (But I could say the same for much of the drivel posted on facebook by individuals as well as companies: "Who cares?")

Title: Social Friendship is a misnomer
Body: This idea of social friendship really goes back a decade to the days of LiveJournal, then myspace and Friendster where we created social communities that consisted of our college friends or family. But as we got bigger communities like Facebook or Social Business solutions, we kept the terminology of "friendship" with the mistaken assumption that if we brought close friendships into an online social environment, that all of our online contacts became friends.  From a business perspective, relationships are based on utility and value, not just on personal aspects. The customer-based social network should still be based on providing value to the customer and not just entertainment or aggregation for the sake of advertising the size of a network. For most businesses, I don't want friends; I want what I want. (And I want it now!)

Title: Loyalty through friendship
Body: Agree with you Josh. I think back to a few interactions that I've had with businesses in the past on a personal/consumer level. I seldom "friend" a business via FB or any other channel...but there are times when I've posted a review about a business on Yelp stating a bad experience or something. What surprises me is if that business owner takes the initiative to respond and takes my comments to heart. From there is where I gain respect and develop loyalty to that business. So its not about spamming or providing coupons, similar to 10 years ago before these social tools were out there - its all about the relationship. These tools nowadays just make it easier and faster to establish those relationships...

Title: Social is forcing companies to walk the walk
Body: Completely agree with the sentiments expressed here. Companies must participate or become effectively invisible to consumers. The risk of being ignored is at this point greater than the risk of overexposure or scandal.  But the interesting thing about social media is that it forces organizations to prove themselves. They can no longer use a big powerful marketing message to drown out problems or complaints. Every customer service misstep or tone-deaf marketing ploy is a potential PR firestorm that could drive customers away in droves. So organizations actually have to do some work to make sure they're treating customers well. My colleagues and I like to say that service is the new marketing. Every single customer interaction is a piece of your brand - more so than ever before.  ...   It puts a lot of pressure on the organization, but it's also very exciting to see them realizing that following through on the promise is just as important as the promise itself.

Title: sine qua non for businesses
Body: Amulya, no doubt, businesses have to use social media. And that's why most of the businesses have some sorts of social media presence. But, the major challenges before them is how to engage the customers, and how much of their engagements ca be converted into genuine leads.  To me, engagement of customers and their conversion, and more importantly, the ROI are the major challenges for the business. Social media presence is now almost a sine qua non for businesses.

Title: Not only my business, but network communities too
Body: Experiencing less attendees to specific network events, because traffic and work/life balance is keeping a lot of people away...social media is a way to interact, to keep your network and board members active and avoiding a lot of organizing workloads. More and more blogging with a large group of experts aligned to your own interest gives you more insights and feedback then you ever will get during an event or drinks afterwards. However it is not the only way.

Title: People are already in social networks
Body: Let's face it: an increasing number of our existing or potential Customers are already in social networks, to exchange valuable information between them like "does anybody already had that problem with product X ...", and some companies are not even aware of it ... So there is no question that businesses have to connect one way or another to these social networks.  @Jen: fully agree with you that "social" is not just another media where you could push strong marcom messages to hide some troubles ... results would probably be the opposite of those expected ... it is about creating long-term relationship with individual people, built on many single interactions, which will build a "culture" shared between the company and the customers.  I  ...   see that as a new approach for most companies, but a great opportunity to show the actual value of a company, not only by declaring it but by proving it on a day-to-day and transparent basis.

Title: More than Facebook
Body: The potential customers that`s inside the social networks around the world is beyond the measures of any company. And these companies, specially the big and old ones, still doesn`t know how to use the informations that these customers give in the internet.  Blogs, videos and other medias are use to support or to show how bad is a service or product, and to give to other users informations about it. The major example about it is the smartphones: unboxing, reviews and the iOS vesus Android disputes between users.  The customers shows their satisfaction or hate beyond the facebook or something like: they get together to improve themselves the product. I believe that  ...   companies can learn much more if they could see this behavior, and use it to give much mor than the market expect.

Title: Hilton: Take Advantage of the Mugs in Hotel Rooms!
Body: I am Dan Verhaeghe, The Mobile Marketing Specialist at McLoughlin Promotions.  I've stayed at several hotels and I've always noticed that there are promotional napkins and promotional mugs, and they all say Hilton on it!  If you also included a QR code on these promotional items, you could link the smartphone-wielding guest anywhere to gain ROI from your guests or as an information service:  ...   Here are 20 QR Code Ideas that generate ROI, think outside the box! http://www.mcloughlin.ca/insights/20qrcodeideas

Title: Seeing
Body: As businesses have become bigger, more global, and more diverseŌĆöwith operations and interactions that span timezones, national borders, and continentsŌĆöit's become harder to see customers. The days of being able to grasp each interaction, what real customers look like, what their desires are, what makes them happy (or not) about your service or offering has become harder. What social business doesŌĆömeaning, social networks and social tools carefully deployedŌĆöis serve as a new set of eyes. From seeing comes belief-changing insights, that then spur action. With this new ability to see againŌĆöbut at a larger scaleŌĆöwhat might we do as leaders in improving how our businesses run?

Title: Social Media for All
Body: I agree that large global organizations are and should be using social media to engage and attract new customers. I think that the "seeing" you refer to can be applied to existing customers. Using social media is a good way to have "conversations" with customers who have "liked" or otherwise already connected to your company. Using social can be a good way to announce new products, service offerings and respond to customer feedback - when done with the customer in mind.  Social can and should also be used to "see" or find new customers. And it's not just for the large, global companies. I recently read of a business owner who is the president of a company that sells industrial and commercial HVAC systems and parts. He did a Google search on key words for his industry and found that his company did not show up in the results. This drove him to action. He built a new website to provide customers and with information  ...   on his companiesŌĆÖ products. This is something most would refer to as Web 1.0 and the bare minimum of promoting a company. However, he didn't stop there. He interviewed industry leaders to show how a piece of equipment really works and posted the videos on YouTube. He wrote many articles and blog posts on products and innovations in his industry. His company now shows up on the front page of results when entering those same key word searches. He promoted his organization and optimized social media channels effectively.  What are other ways organizations can use social to increase their customer base?

Title: New Eyes
Body: Keith:  The shift in perspective that becomes possible means that the social business can actually see itself, through the eyes of its customers, partners and even its own workforce.  Organizational myopia becomes less tenable when a company or business can truly see, hear and understand how others perceive it...and such self-awareness is critical to transformation and change.

Title: Social business for self-awareness and change
Body: Sounds like a "hot idea": Social interaction enhances organizational self-awareness, which is critical to transformation and change... which in time will improve business / organization results, because you know where to invest. How can you afford to not become a social business?

Title: What are the new eyes?
Body: Perhaps it is not that organizations purposely ignore the viewpoints of people (customers, partners, employees) but rather how they look. Most medium and larger companies use some form of survey, customer focus groups, or other ways to determine 'insights' from these constituents. While they ask, do they really listen at a level beyond looking for opportunities?  Very often this kind of information is aggregated into an "overall" message from a particular segment rather than looking distinctly at the individual inputs and messages, because it takes work. I would think with the advances in social CRM and other technology we do have some of those capabilities to consider this view from an individual viewpoint.

Title: New eyes or a mirror?
Body: You say "while they ask, do they really listen at a level beyond looking for opportunities?".I think not, because what we really miss is the ability to "get in the shoes" of others. Social or face to face, do you think it's possible to understand customers and improve our business without simply returning to the (italian) proverb:"The customer is always right?"

Title: New vision through analytics
Body: Very thought provoking...I agree with others that social business also allows organizations to "see" new customers. With the tidal wave of social data collected everyday, there are opportunities to analyze this data to "see" what people in general are saying/doing/etc. If we can do this right, Social Analytics can open the doors to whole new way of finding new customers (on top of monitoring the sentiment of existing ones).

Title: Back to the basic
Body: Is the customer happy with what you do...!!  Social is a way to monitor that...get feedback...use the feedback to make the experience and products better....  One clear way to do that has been around for mucho time but, we here at IBM still don't do it...  Product  ...   reviews...allowing our customers to give us feedback on our website about our products and make it visable to all...That builds trust and confidence...Gives us the ability to analize, respond, and make our customer experience and products better...  This example of social computing is probably called...getting back to the basics of social computing ....Making our customers delighted with what we do....

Title: Sustaining trust in an era of social media: how?
Body: The notion of trust has come up repeatedly in response to my earlier post about customers' willingness to engage socially with businesses.  So if trust is so central: how does one build trust? Are there some positive practices that stand out? Conversely, what practices should be absolutely avoided?  Share your insights!

Title: Trust
Body: Trust results from building and maintaining a relationship. It takes time, and dedication. Both parties need to gain mutual benefit over a period of time. Once you finally get 'it', you must continue to dedicate the time and energy in keeping it. It's not easy ... but well worth the investment.  So, the best practice? Dedicate the time, and commit to long term relationships.

Title: us-vs-them
Body: Trust stems from viewing the people you meet online as just that - people. We all have things in common, and those commonalities enable us to understand (if not benefit from) our differences.  There's a great deal of dissonance built into the typical business model and I think it plays a subconscious role in the lack of trust.  If "business exists to generate profits," but customers buy to maintain/improve quality of life, the two are not in alignment. If business exists to improve the quality of life (literally - not just mission statement propaganda), customers will realize true improvements in quality of life, building trust.  Think  ...   about it this way: "Customer" is a convenient way of saying "not us." Why should the customer be treated any differently than an employee? It's us-vs-them on a subconscious level, and it leads to dissonance.  Simply put, business realizes thin margins because business provides thin value.

Title: social business is about relationships
Body: Trust is key in any relationship (Dr. Phi would agree!) But in the BtoC channel, social media is a place to build on that relationship outside the transaction. It is not a soap box, it is not a bullhorn, it is not a bill board. And that means it has to be driven by engagement with conversations.... that ultimately lead to conversions.

Title: Trust is result of your commitment
Body: Social media is a system like any other  it has its objectives,process, input and an output  lets first focust on Output because trust falls there  you have brand awareness,brand building marketing and relation ship building all variants of trust on one side.  ...   Now the inputs are your commitment,engagement,loyalty and sincerity to address ,resolve and value  So as long as you can assure your inputs properly qualitatively there is no worry about output. trust will come in one form or the other which is lot more beneficial to you and the business as a whole.

Title: Trust Crushers
Body: A couple things that can crush trust in the realm of social media are ignoring the voice of the customer, not genuinely wanting to engage and listen but trying to use a social channel like other channels (one way communication), and using social only for marketing and sales purposes while turning a deaf ear to customer concerns and conversation. Communication that is not transparent and deceptive will ultimately be turned against a company until they can learn to embrace the broad conversation and use it as an opportunity to listen, engage and improve.

Title: Put Yourself in the Consumer's Shoes
Body: I couldn't agree more with Kurt Malueg (Trust Crushers post).  When I think of my own personal use of Facebook and how that may apply to business a few things jump out: Trust, transparency & value. The connections that use our "Friendship" as a loudspeaker for their values or agenda quickly move to the "unfriend" category or their repeated posts are ignored. Those that take their own personal filter off and provide insight into what they are truly thinking provide more value to me personally and help create more trusted relationship.  As  ...   a guide post for social media in business, I believe one of the very simple principles should be to look at how we personally would like to be engaged with business in this space. Nobody likes to be spammed or get the run around. Everyone has relationships that they trust and would rely on for a recommendation on a product, a service, a plumber or a doctor.

Title: Trust - Authenticity - Tending the Garden
Body: Kurt - Concur. Being authentic is one area where trust comes into play. I've watched my wife blog for the past two years. She shares pictures on animals and flowers and some news about the family to whomever is interested. I've been very impressed with how this has gone. She's had 80K hits and has 300 followers, which puts my blog to shame. I've noticed how authentic she is in her writings and also how she "tends to her garden," that is, she spends a lot of time replying to those who comment on her blog and also visiting the blog sites of those who visit her. She has developed friends from around the world - really. Trust is there and tending to the garden is one key way to build trust. I'm trying to do more of this in my portion of the virtual social business world.

Title: Sustaining trust in an era of social media
Body: Trust in all things is earned. Social media is no different than any other type of communication.  The problem lies when social media avenues are not treated as seriously as other avenues.  We as participants are compelled to dive into these channels with the same values that we live by with all other channels.

Title: How does one build trust?
Body: by being clear about Why we are in business as nearly every enterprise answers the questions What We Do and How We Do It which are not real distinguishing agents. Simon Sinek may said it best at TED video: http://www.ted.com/talks/simon_sinek_how_great_leaders_inspire_action.html

Title: What do you expect when enaging with Social Media
Body: One build trust by respecting the communication code: if you ask a question in your wall and somebody reacts and provides a feedback, acknowledge your conversation partners.  Social Media means interaction: dialog.  Simple Rules:  - Set clear close marks to engaged discussions  -  ...   Acknowledge the provided feedback from the participants in your dialog  - Explain what you're trying to achieve when you engage your community  Social Media is mainly Communication at a large scale.

Title: Transparency
Body: The key to trust is transparency. This has been proven through large companies becoming transparent and winning back their base as well as politicians who are not transparent...well, need I say more? Social media is an open book to all. It can be the model of transparency and can build trust and openness in an immediate way that no other media is able to do at this time.

Title: It's simple: messages and build trust
Body: When a tobacco company says about its contribution to health development, and Chinese or Russian steel company declares the protection of the environment, do you believe?  But when Motorola says about replacing safe-screen by sleep mode on all computers in corporations - I believe.

Title: Using social media to repair damaged trust
Body: Yes, an organization's past actions affect the level of trust they have today. I think that social engagement -- as long as it's paired with responsible actions -- could be a way to help improve trust. I wonder, for example, whether an oil company with a recent oil spill is using social tools, in addition to advertising, which is only one-way communication.

Title: Everyone Serves Customers; Mine Are Leaders
Body: Everyone, I think, can pause to consider again who is his or her customer and determine how, not if, to help them succeed via social technologies.  Who are your customers and how are you using social technologies to enable their success?  My clients are internal; they are IBM's leaders. I'm helping them succeed by designing social learning opportunities that enable them to learn from one another globally, more dynamically than they can through a point-in-time class. Classes, of course, still are appropriate for milestone-moments in their leadership advancement, but my focus is on enabling their continuous learning via crowd-sourcing challenges, online communities, immersive, 3D Internet experiences and more.

Title: Wondering
Body: It sound really interesting. Just wondering, How do you determine which learning opportunity would enable them to learn something new rather that reinforce a knowledge?

Title: I like this question
Body: ...it makes me think: A good portion of the learning that any of us does, including leaders, is validation as opposed to brand new learning, but even during validation of what we think we already know, in my experience, there is almost always new learning, even if incidental and serendipitous and not the learning we were aiming for specifically...and often, for me, that learning is the more profound learning than the targeted learning.

Title: I like serendipitous learning
Body: I've been calling it accidental learning but in spite of the spelling difficulty, I like serendipitous better. I also like serendipitous connections that can happen through social networks..and the "more connected" feeling you can get from "knowing" someone through a social connection. For example, I recently reached out to an IBM colleague whom I had met several times but did not know well, to ask if she would mentor a young IBM friend. I felt comfortable doing this because in addition to my IBM relationship, I had a more friendly connection with her through FaceBook.

Title: Who monitor IBM informations on SM & response?
Body: There are tools and technologies to search and monitor themes/topics in social medias, if there are some information on internet that are worthy to response by IBM, who will take the responsibility to monitor and resonse? How to rsponse in time?  I think these challange are not only to the external communication and marketing team only, there are challenges to technical team, labs, event customer service team and sales team should have the responsibility to response to customers information in social medias.  My suggestions is to enable a fast response team inside IBM who will be equipped with social media monitor tools, and to be authorized to response to related messages on behalf of IBM corporate.

Title: Social Business in Proposals?
Body: I work in commercial proposals to clients and have been thinking about whether or not there are ways to take social business into the arena of proposals. We do use collaboration tools like Lotus Quickr with clients during the sales cycle when appropriate, but is there potential for taking it further? Smarter proposals?

Title: Is Social Media Marketing an Oxymoron?
Body: I've included a couple of paragraphs on this provocative question below, because I continue to see people in business viewing (and applying) social as a new means to old ends, rather than new means toward new ends...actual person-to-person interaction and engagement that is its own reward in terms of an organization's reputation, values, collaborative creative capacity and authenticity.  In our opinion the concept of social media marketing is an oxymoron ŌĆ” generally speaking ŌĆō marketing means, ŌĆ£Persuading people to buy our stuff against a competitorŌĆÖs. It is ŌĆśus and themŌĆÖ thinking, while social media spaces are about facilitating social connections, building relationships and removing friction from communication and collaborationŌĆØ (Taylor, 2010).  In our opinion, a strict marketing approach to social media is doomed because a marketing based social media strategy has the wrong point of departure when defining the objectives by purely marketing metrics (Taylor, 2010).  Marketing goals are ŌĆō in our opinion ŌĆō a defective approach when it comes to social media. Brand advocates should keep in mind that consumers are not on social media to purchase and ŌĆō as argued by digital editor of Adweek, Brian Morrissey ŌĆō ŌĆ£People donŌĆÖt go to these channels to talk about brands. If youŌĆÖre not adding value, youŌĆÖre spamŌĆØ (Morrissey cited in Keane, 2009). So, instead of pure sales goals, ŌĆ£Better objectives look more like those of a research brief than a marketing brief (ŌĆ”). These objectives are not about propagating a message, but about unraveling a story, listening and joining a conversation, collaboration and discussionŌĆØ (Taylor, 2010).  Many companies are simply not succeeding in their social media approach, because they commence with the wrong objectives. Social media platforms are not well suited for traditional marketing based on persuasion and with a broadcast mentality, where the brand talks and the customers listen.

Title: This is why Social Business matters
Body: Social media marketing works. So does Enterprise 2.0. Businesses need holistic strategy to drive meaningful impact, rather than debating corporatist vs. purist POVs.

Title: the oxymoron
Body: A hello from the greater community to you Jack.  I don't see marketing as strictly persuasive and a means to an end. Rather - marketing is just want a brand says and does which leads us down a path of experiences and engagement in this day and age. In which case "social media marketing" isn't an oxymoron.  But it is no longer necessary for 'social media' to called out. Rather clever marketing today includes a social component just like it may contain other traditional components.  ...   I'm a pretty digital marketer that now sits within a big high volume ad agency. And my time there has shown me that traditional and some of the digital marketing vehicles that I love to *hate* like paid media - do indeed have a role to play pending the objectives and particular situation of the brand. But certainly, as you atest, the right strategy and plan start with the proper objective setting.  Anywho - great question.

Title: where do I start...
Body: If we were to focus purely on large enterprise social video marketing practices as a basic example here, I believe it has been fairly well proven across the www that heavily marketing good videos drives more clicks. Take the case of popular and oft-running quirky TV ads placed on YouTube. Hits can be in the millions (Intel and GE as small examples). Spend enough money on awareness and people will watch your best videos.  Thing is, almost the same could be said for great social videos promoted by fantastic PR teams. The click numbers compete with the big budget marketing vids at a fraction of the cost in dollars (it takes a lot of human resource though), and they often drive more comments and conversation. They get mentioned in newspapers and magazines, embedded into online news articles, organic blogs (non-sponsored), and etc. I do not think that any large org should overlook the power in this model.  ...   Excellent content promoted purely through social media methods can have strong results too, but will rarely meet the basic click numbers of videos that have had promotional marketing budget and the power of a great PR team. What makes pure social video different (aside from low cost production and promotion practices when done right) is that it often drives much more buzz - comments, conversation and sharing - across the web.  There is room for all sorts of approaches, especially when a large org has a variety of needs and goals, so I hesitate to discourage social media marketing practices. What I do think is that if you want to drive classic social buzz and inspire real connections as a result, PR and pure social video can be very powerful as well when done right.

Title: The Shift in Mindset
Body: I agree that a smart, social business can put many social technologies and techniques into practice to advance many different kinds of agendas. Including sales, promotion, advertising and brand enhancement  But I think the frequent, fatal mistake that typically gets made, specifically in the marketing sphere, is to view social computing, networking or media as just an extension of the old ways of persuasion and influence.  Even early on, some people would see a blog as a kind of low-cost PR or promotional vehicle. Perhaps things will change gradually as practitioners learn  ...   what works and why, and how differently we need to think about this evolving space and understand its unique characteristics.

Title: flexible vs. rigid?
Body: Not quite gelled thoughts here, but perhaps organizations - especially the big ones like IBM - can be most effective deploying a mixture of flexible (social) and rigid (old-school PR) where and when it makes sense. I think there will always be a need for the official company line (if not always, then for the forseeable future) but where customers and prospects take or follow that line is an invitation to become more social. Our interactions with clients last longer the time it takes to read a press release, so I see lots of opportunities to use the two approaches simultaneously.

Title: Social media is more about letting consumers
Body: Social media is more about consumer connections and expression than purchase. However, if a customer is following on social media.. it means something. This customer is an advocate for your brand and wants something in return. Customers are no longer just recipients of goods but producers and goods and services. I'm not sure customers are ready to listen and its difficult to make the organizational changes to truly be "consumer centric" #ibmibv

Title: Social Media vs Social Influence Marketing
Body: The modality we operate in as marketers, depends on our point of view. Many corprorations (and me personally) have been guilty of the one-way shout. Treating social media as just another channel to broadcast a tight social marketing message. The challenge is to go beyond that; social influence.  The shift that must occur is understanding that it's identifying and leveraging pockets of relevant influence that mattes. By understanding who the most influencial sites/bloggers/etc understanding their agenda and interests and then engaging them, helps drive business outcomes.  Maybe the skills needed are more like PR or Customer Service skills.  ...   Depending on an organisations definition of marketing, this might not be marketing's role.

Title: Let's Start with a Jam
Body: As with all big challenges, the first thing to get on top of, is where to start? When engaging in a discourse with customers I think what we're all demonstrating today is that jammin' is as good a way as any to engage them.  It's also pretty easy to get going. I'd be interested to hear from other people that have run Jams with their customers and what the responses have been.

Title: Social Media for advertising and promoting Brand.
Body: Many Companies have started advertising their products in Social media like Facebook. Brand ambassador is appointed and Brand message is given to customers. This has brought in new customers for the porducts. One example I can give is a liquor Brand being promoted on a Facebook page which has led to new people trying that product in the place I live.

Title: Open innovation
Body: How does open innovation influence the business model in companies?  What is the uniqueness of Jams?

Title: Jesper you may be interested in ...
Body: ...this new book by Venkat Ramaswamy: http://www.amazon.com/Power-Co-Creation-Breakthrough-Strategies-Customers/dp/1439181047/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&s=books&qid=1272383227&sr=8-1

Title: Jams
Body: I have not read the book, but now I have found it in the library ;) thanks.  Do you have any personal comments on the matter?  And what makes you participate in a Jam like this?

Title: Social Business
Body: whether Social Business will help to really solve the social Issues?

Title: Product reviews s/b major in our Social Engagement
Body: One of the ways to build trust with our customers are the ability to allow customers to comment and provide feedback (good or bad) on our products...This is one of the most basic and key way to allow our customers to tell us what we need to fix or are doing well? We will build trust and confidence when we open ourselves up to allow customers to give us feedback on our products. We gain because we get a roadmap of what to fix. Customer feel engaged and we can certainly turn customers who have recommendations from being a detractor to becoming an advocate for IBM. It also gives product managers a voice to respond in a positive way to provide IBM's view...which in many cases are very valid.  Today there is only a small pilot underway....we should expand that to all brands all products....A true way to build advocacy and brand equity

Title: Social is great for Engagement BUT, Lacks
Body: The problem with Social Engagement in IBM is that there is little connection between the engagement and the resulting lead or way to turn engagement to lead...The currect infrastructure does not allow to automatically or in an automated way to connect engaged customers to someone that can record the lead and appropriately give credit to the social engagement that started it...  IBM has a very complex sales cycle and the Social Engagement might occur early in the cycle...by the time the lead is nutured all connection to the original social engagement is lost...There is a feeling that at least 1/2 the leads generated by some either social or web leads are lost because of our infrustrure weaknesses

Title: Real-time social based customer feedback
Body: Mobile technology leads to real-time, unfettered, customer interactions. How should companies manage the both positive and negative feedback that can come from customers while interacting with your products and services? Staffing and customer service will be stressed based on the industry and type of services and products. Is this an evolution of customer service or simply another channel that needs to be managed?

Title: Social media, why companies should react...?
Body: In current travel & transportation industry, social media are largely modifying the traditional relation to customers.  In fact, companies should now react, as "doing nothing" strategy is seen as the worst one from a customer perspective.  Customers are now instrumented (PDA,Smartphones,...), social media provides two others attributes :  - Interconnected (all customers are real time connected"  - Intelligence trough information sharing about any topic or situation...  One interesting example is the case of Train Operators, as far as a non-normal situation appears, social media becomes an alternate, faster and reliable way to get information than the information channel of the rail company.  Social media as an alternate information channels, or an non controlled feedback opportunity from customers, opens a new era of interaction with the "real business" conditions.  From this perspective, companies have no other choice than react, in order to demonstrate, at least, their involvement in gathering customer's feedback and comments.  In addition to this first brick, social media strategies could be elaborated to adress more focused points...

Title: Reputation management/Brand Monitoring
Body: Hey experts, we are creating an application in social marketing. Just wondering what various factors/trends to consider when specifically targeting Brand Reputation or monitoring? I am a technical geek not at all a marketing wizard. So how do i translate such marketing jargon to technical jargon for my applcation when considering, especially reputation management.  All I can get in my geeky brain is an algortihm for key word search across popular social and web and some SEO thingys..  Is there anything else..I am missing or important factors to consider..Any suggestions would help.  Thankyou,  Dilip

Title: All too easy to get wrong and counterproductive
Body: Having a consistent communication strategy is the most important thing here.  SEO (aka "google spamming" to be blunt) isnt the best tool for that.  Communication skills and an awareness of social media helps a lot more.

Title: What is Co-creation?
Body: What if Social is a means to a larger goal of using engagement platforms to "build it with them"?  See Venkat Ramaswamy's new book: http://www.amazon.com/Power-Co-Creation-Breakthrough-Strategies-Customers/dp/1439181047/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&s=books&qid=1272383227&sr=8-1

Title: Leveraging Social Networks - Consumer created ads
Body: Will the role of creative agencies diminish?  Audi, Docomo, FritoLays, Samsung... the list may not be long but it sure is growing. Of the brands that have started sourcing consumer created ads and spending advertising dollors in media on the same.  Three aspects seem to be driving this phenomenon:  1) Near universal proliferation of Mobile and Internet (atleast amongst the population that matters to mareketers)  2) Ease of multimedia content creation made possible by technology  3) Explosion in social media / networking sites that enable users to create/post their content  4) Popularity of user generated content (Blogs, Videos, Apps, Tweets etc)  In this context, is it a safe bet to say the role of creative agencies is going to diminish in the coming years? Or is it just another fad which will not have any discernible impact?  If it does have impact, what are the implications for clients? And for creative agencies? What measures do they need to take to harness the potential of this phenomenon?

Title: Life after Mad Men
Body: The need for the well wrought user experience or engaging experience becomes more important as evidenced by such need on mobile apps where the value is all about the experience.  In the past advertising model, the the advertiser brought the product (viewer) to the client (entity who paid for advertising) via the medium of the content, ie a tv show. Now the model is that the viewer is the client and the content is the product. Creative agencies have to find a valuable role for the advertiser in this model.

Title: Lack of tool support for cultural sensitivity
Body: I'm sure you've heard somewhere of the 'great divide' between the American call for 'free speech' and the European call for 'data privacy'. I'm sure you've also often wondered why clones keep popping up and succeeding in one country but not another - and how few platforms make it to the global level. Or that cultures react completely differently to the same information - a profile that an American would consider as 'trustworthy' may be viewed as 'careless' by a European. And similarly, a profile that a European would consider as 'trustworthy' may be considered as 'suspicious' by an American. Why?  My theory is that users in different cultures have different cultural values, and these values play an important role in how users interact online with each other and how 'comfortable' they feel in each network. Sadly, most networks ignore this cultural sensitivity and impose their 'home' culture across the entire network, leading to the regional network effect.  The big question is then: is it possible to create a single network with sufficient controls to allow people to feel at home with the interface and with their presence, and still be sensitive enough to each of the cultures with which they interact?

Title: How can businesses start to get social?
Body: ...my answer is that the press and media relations department is an ideal starting point. My experience in the german market shows that these people often work very "vintage" and have a small team. Social tools help them a lot in their daily work. We had many talks with lokal heads of Press und PR departments and they all were fascinated with what social business concepts could bring them.

Title: New trends in customer care on social media
Body: Social Listening posts, 24X7 social care agents, influencer group management, reactive and proactive blogs etc are techniques that companies have already adopted in using social media for customer care / customer experience management. Are there any newer practices / innovations emerging on the social media leverage for customer care?

Title: Are your Social Differentiators defined?
Body: With the new "Social" era is driving consumer & prospect interactions across the globe, the reach and breadth of your target consumer base is limitless. However, since each engagement touchpoint with your customer is customized to the individual, does this experience is differentiating enough for retaining them as loyalists while other businesses are trying to do the same.  How would your marketing efforts be socially differentiated?

Title: Social is a channel - albeit a new one
Body: Only while it is still "new" will the Social Media interaction be a differentiator. In the same way that the first bank with an ATM had a differentiator. It will soon be just another channel. The trick is to use this new channel effectively to communicate with your customer to enhance their experience and keep them (loyalty = repeat purchase in a competitive market). So all the regular operational processes need apply - identify who it is, tailor the discussion and listen before reacting.

Title: Social way is just a medium
Body: communicating with consumers via social medium is key.. apart from how you communicate it is also important what you communicate now all business is having targeted customer information based on their social graph .. business must consider that on what message they are communicating and via what medium.. even though its just a simple announcement

Title: The multiple social networks of health care
Body: Our networks as healthcare consumers are perhaps more complicated and nuanced than as consumers of products. First we have a relationship with our particular clinicians ŌĆō and I think we would want them to engage with us directly. Second, we have a connection to the larger health system ŌĆō think HMO, Medical Center or Health Plan. Third, there are the networks of patients similar to me ŌĆō those with similar conditions or diseases. And lastly, there is my own social network to provide support and motivation.  Does this mean we should engage with different social networks? Should different individuals play different and sometimes overlapping roles within the same network?

Title: Healthcare and role of social media
Body: Excellent comment Adam.  I think social media can and should play many roles and overlap within various healthcare networks. In Japan, we found in the past that people in their 30s-60s were quite active and willing to post cancer management messages on bulletin boards (remember BBS 12 years ago) now fast forward that to less than 2 years ago and discovered on our diabesity cell phone community, less than 3% of all customers were willing to share personal healthcare stories.  Social media means different things to different people. As a healthcare technologist in my 40s, I do not feel comfortable on facebook nor do I find the  ...   need to tweet everyone about all of my thoughts and daily ( personal) activities.  However, in terms of generating user content or sharing of my own diabetes management stories or other medical related happenings, I feel that blogging or contributing to healthcare sites is of value.  What does the rest of the community feel about the role of social media in healthcare?
